Original_Search_Title: Anyim, Atiku, Babangida And 2003
Headline: Nigeria: Anyim, Atiku, Babangida And 2003
Body_Text:
It was a most shameful night when elected representatives of the people at the highest level would congregate to lie so brazenly. But I had told myself that no matter the chicanery at the event I would neither comment nor ask question. My position had been formed after the two previous nights, one in Minna, the other in Abuja. I had resolved within me that no matter the perfidy from politicians this is the time the media should rise to save this democracy in our own interest.
Before we editors left Lagos for Abuja for the fourth media roundtable with the Senate President, Chief Anyim Pius Anyim, at the weekend, we knew it was going to turn out a disaster for the man because of the Electoral Act and we were not disappointed.
After a long speech, the Senate President, who was flanked by several of his colleagues, said he would want all questions pertaining to the electoral bill to be asked first so that he could take them together. The import of that was not lost on us, he did not want any follow-up question. Everything had been pre-planned by the law makers, the same way they rig elections at source. Incidentally, on the high table was the Senate Chairman on Information, Senator Jonathan Zwingina, who only last week told us President Olusegun Obasanjo, as the 'Chief law maker' of the federation, reserves the power to re-write the Electoral law. Of course he would reverse himself by equivocating that what he meant was that the President initiates bill and signs it into law as if we did not know that before. But I know, as most people also know, that if our democracy derails tomorrow and soldiers come back, God forbid, Zwingina is a potential Information Minister, because such characters are good salesmen for the military, simply because they have the talent for rationalizing anything, including why democracy failed, if we help them to scuttle the process. It is for this reason I believe those of us in the media would have to restrain ourselves in times like this so that we do not play into the hands of opportunists who personally have nothing to lose.
Because I had decided not to ask the Senate President any question, I felt pained when, rather than answer the simple question about how the contentious proviso in section 80 entered the electoral bill, Anyim was shouting on us, even calling us "ignorant". We, however, bore all the indignities with philosophical calmness, and my uncle, Mr. Segun Babatope, the Managing Director of Anchor Newspaper made it very clear to the Senate President that our politicians still have a lot to learn, because insult would not take anything from us and any public official that indulges in such habit only demeans himself and the office he occupies. That message will be instructive for some other people too. What I found particularly painful about the session was that Professor Osunbor, a Senator and eminent lawyer for whom I have respect, would want to call white black in trying to justify how his Harmonisation Committee allowed itself to be teleguided to rewrite the electoral law without a recourse to the entire house when the issue involved is so fundamental. But as I said it is time we took this matter away from the law maker, we have to save this process.
My paradigm shift on this Electoral Act began to take shape on Monday night at a dinner party we in the THISDAY Board of Editors held for six national honours recipients who, in our estimation, have distinguished themselves and are therefore role models not only for our profession but indeed our generation. They are Mr. Sam Amuka, (known to many as Uncle Sam or Sad Sam) the self evasing publisher of the Vanguard; Mallam Wada Maida, the extremely humble but powerful Editor-in-chief of News Agency of Nigeria(NAN); Mr. Olisa Agbakoba, SAN, the fearless lawyer who pioneered the civil rights community as an institution in Nigeria; Mr. Akin Kekere Ekun, the Managing Director of Habib Bank who has shown that a bank does not have to deal in dirty money through round tripping or 'free funds' and other sharp practices to ensure a healthy balance sheet that add value to the economy; Mr. Nasiru el-Rufai, the dimunitive Mr. Privatization who has very tall and bright ideas when it comes to economic management and of course, my egbon, Mr. Gbenga Oyebode, the high flying international lawyer who has his hands in almost every big business pie from telecoms to energy.
It was a quiet dinner at NICON-Hilton with nice highlife music but as it most often happens in any of our events, there was drama. First, Chief Molade Okoya Thomas, "invited" himself into the dinner because, as he said, he is a member of our generation, having resolved that he would not allow anyone to "push him upstairs". The highly respected business man and Lagos high chief was to add a lot of fun to the night. Then as we were about to really get going, another uninvited guest, Vice President Alhaji Atiku Abubakar, breezed in. A very practical man without any air of self-importance, the V.P. said he had come to the hotel to cut his hair only to learn about the party and he decided to come in to honour our Chairman, Mr. Nduka Obaigbena even when he was not invited. His presence, as it would not turn out, not only added meaning and essence to the night, it marked the turning point for me about this electoral act. While giving the vote of thanks for the guests, Mr. Amuka had reminded us all that whatever may be our misgivings on issues, we must never forget one fact: Nigeria is the only country we have. When it was his turn to propose a toast to the federal republic of Nigeria, Vice President Atiku Abubakar waxed philosophical even as he enjoined us all to do all within our capacity to make this country great. He reiterated Uncle Sam's position, as he called on everyone present to ensure we rise above our differences in the effrot to build an enduring democracy. I can now no longer recall the exact words he said but I felt touched by it all and I left the party with a resolve that I will be part of those who will help Nigeria get out of its problems, any problem, including this electoral act palaver. Incidentally, I had spent the previous night in Minna with General Ibrahim Babangida and in his own way, perhaps without knowing it, he made me to realise that political issues are not always black and white hence the reason we must learn to accommodate each other's differences in times like this. My trip to Minna was a gamble that paid off because I spent quality time discussing, sometimes arguing, (at both multilateral and bilateral level ) with the former President.
Given that General Babangida's name had dominated several issues (Oputa Panel, 2003, new political party, Nitel sale etc) in the last couple of months, I had wanted to speak with him but all efforts, either on phone or through the usual contact, failed. That was why I was surprised two weeks ago when I got home from office and my wife told me he had called to congratulate her on our new baby. That emboldened me to go to Minna last Sunday uninvited and when the security men politely turned me back because there was no message that anybody with my name was coming, all I did was to cleverly wave my two mobile phones (none of which was working anyway) to show that I too belong (in Nigeria, such habit still works) and muttered "but the General himself called me yesterday." I also pretended as if I was angry that he failed to inform his people that I was coming. It worked. One of the security men asked for my complimentary card, telling me to wait for him to go and confirm. I knew the General would not turn me back and I was not disappointed. It turned out that he was going to the Mosque as I was coming in. As usual he greeted me warmly with banters, telling the people following him that they would have to pray for my conversion to Islam inside the Mosque and I retorted that I was going inside to be praying for his conversion to Christianity. Incidentally, when they came out one of the men told me that they actually prayed for me. If truly they did, they must have been praying amiss.
Since it was lunch time we all went to the dinning together and as I descended on the pounded yam, the discussion which had commenced continued. Of course it was on the Electoral Act. With several politicians and a senior member of the House of Representatives in attendance it was not surprising. It was a most revealing session and everybody had his say, predictably most of them expressed anger at President Obasanjo for closing the door on new parties but the high point for me was the one-on-one session I had with the General. From Oputa to Nitel (on which he said he was not in anyway involved) to 2003, we talked though it was not an interview but rather an informal session, just for him to provide insight into several issues which would benefit me as a reporter and most of which I will find useful one day when I decide to write on Nigeria. The one that is relevant for this piece though is our discussion of the electoral act and the role of the media. But since I know many people will crucify me if I do not say what we discussed on 2003 I need to state that he merely reiterated what he said earlier this year that he has no interest. Even while I am aware many people are goading him into what may turn out a misadventure should he try, I doubt if he would take the gamble. He is not a fool. What I, however, consider tragic is that in a nation of 120 million people, with several internationally reputed professionals, whenever we talk about leadership, all that we can think of are former players. Is it really not shameful for us as a nation that a man who left office almost nine years ago after spending eight years in office is the same person some people would be making cold calculations to recycle for Nigeria's presidency in the 21st century? But we shall come back to this some day. Of course some people will say "what about Obasanjo?" but it is the same problem.
In the course of our discussing the Electoral Act, General Babangida, while agreeing that he saw no reason why a democratic government would want to exclude new parties from elections told me that the media have been over dramatising the issue. "I read your column ('Here We Go Again..' published last week) and I noticed how hard you were on the President. One thing you don't know is that there is enormous pressure on the man. I have been there before so I can understand what he is going through. It is not easy, with several contending interests. Besides, is he not entitled to make mistakes? I have also noticed one trend in the media, you people are already creating scares in the minds of the people. Two days ago, I read one article in the Comet newspaper where the writer made allusion to the crisis that always happen at every ten-year interval, talking about 1963, 1973, 1983, 1993 and now making predictions about 2003. Such analysis based on superstitions is dangerous because people may begin to believe it". I told him it was no superstition and we had arguments on some of the positions but I quite agree that it is time the media began to be more circumspect about our positions on some of these political issues, beginning from the electoral act.
Now, what are the contentious issues in the Act? The first is the tenure of local government while the second is the issue of allowing new parties to contest the coming 2003 general elections.
In practical terms, these are the issues: the Governors want to conduct council elections next year so that they can rig their cronies in as council chairmen. The National Assembly members want to checkmate the Governors by postponing the Council poll till 2003 when there would be no opportunity to install surrogates through a manipulated process. The Chairmen of the 774 looting centres called local government do not want to go having been awarded an additional year free of charge (?)by the lawmakers. The President does not want new parties because he is afraid of some imaginary candidate that could come from the so-called new parties to dislodge him. Yet everybody is right in his own eyes and can use the constitution to buttress his position.
From all these we can see that none of our elected public officials making noise can actually speak from any moral high ground. Now, to talk about the new parties. The only one with any measure of seriousness is the United Nigeria Democratic Party (UNDP) but what manner of party is that one too? It is just an amalgam of ex-this, ex-that of the Babangida regime. From what we have seen of them thus far, they are not in any way different from the ruling PDP or the All Peoples Party (APP) or the Alliance for Democracy(AD). Perhaps the only defining character is that UNDP is a congregation of the displaced, the disgruntled and the malcontents of the PDP but is that what makes a political party?
But they have a point when they argue we should not put unnecessary hurdles on their way and I believe it is time we began to look at the way forward. The first is for all the stakeholders, especially the politicians to realise we dare not fail this time around and the best way to ensure that is for us to begin to test our institutions, especially the judiciary. There are several contentious provisions in the constitution, why are our politicians afraid of asking for the interpretation by the courts? Anyim, for instance, gave himself away when he was talking about the issue of whether some governors are qualified to contest 2003 polls. He repeatedly talked about their "Third Term" which means chief Olusegun Osoba and the three others, as far as the National Assembly is concerned, cannot contest the 2003 polls. Is that the position of the law? Only the court can determine that but nobody is willing to bell the cat.
Much more significantly, however, I want to appeal to my colleagues in the media that even as we report the controversy and the drama, we should be careful not to give the impression that this is the end of the world. We should encourage the political class to come out with a solution and if at the end of the day the status quo remains we should learn to live with it. We have a very good example to learn from. Almost every American journalist was aware that the Presidential election in Florida was rigged in favour of President George Bush but he would not say it. Nobody also talked about the military as a solution. As it was in America, so it should be here. The last option should be the court. This electoral act is our own Florida and we should handle it the same way.

Original_Search_Title: High Interest Rate Worries Obasanjo
Headline: Nigeria: High Interest Rate Worries Obasanjo
Body_Text:
President Olusegun Obasanjo yesterday in Abuja expressed concern over high interest rates charged by banks in the country even as he stated plans by his administration to bring the rate down.
Speaking when a delegation of the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN) visited him, the president said his commitment towards bringing down the rate was informed by the fact that it was counter productive.
"As long as the rate of interest is that high, we cannot have a productive economy. It is a problem I will continue to fight until we get a solution. It is one thing we must get right," he said.
The president debunked the claim of commercial banks that they charged high interest rates as a result of inflation, saying, it was a wrong solution.
"Today, if we have any inflation at all, it is very small, the price of gari, yam and beans have come down. Those of rice, petrol and diesel are stable. So where does the inflation come from?" he asked.
the president then directed that an immediate action be taken to ascertain the true rate of inflation in the country.
He explained that a more correct choice of the item and weighting used by the Federal Office of Statistics to determine the rate of inflation would result in a downward review of the rate currently put at 19 per cent.
"There are many things we have to do to the economy. If all other things are right, we will not have violent fluctuations in the exchange rate," he said.
He warned that government would impose punitive sanctions on any bank that  took "morality out of business and still engaged in "round tripping to the detriment of the nation's economy."
He allayed fears raised by the MAN delegation over the multiplicity of taxes imposed by state and local governments on investors.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Accuses Banks of Sabotaging Economy
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Accuses Banks of Sabotaging Economy
Body_Text:
Abuja — PRESIDENT Olusegun Obasanjo has accused banks in the country of
sabotaging the economy through the high interest rate charged on
loans.
President Obasanjo condemned the attitude of the banks while
receiving a delegation of the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria
(MAN) in Abuja.
According to the President, there was no justifications for the high
interest rate of the banks.
He said that the inflation rate on which the banks based their
interest rates was not high enough to warrant the posture of the
banks.
He said: "Today, if we have any inflation at all. It is very small.
The prices of garri, yam and beans have come down, those of rice,
petrol and diesel are stable, so where does the inflation come from?"
He, however, directed that proper action be taken to determine the
true rate of inflation in the country.
He added that as long as the interest rate remained high, the nation
could not have a productive economy.
"It is a problem I will continue to fight until we get a solution, it
is one thing we must get right."
He conceded, however, that there were some things to be done to make
the economy more stable at the macro level, adding that this would
eliminate violent fluctuation in the exchange rate.
He warned that his administration might impose punitive action on
banks which engaged in round-tripping.
"I will not allow people to ruin this country for their little
conveniences and I will not get tired of sanctioning those who clog
the wheels of progress," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Traders Return To Giri Onion Market
Headline: Nigeria: Traders Return To Giri Onion Market
Body_Text:
The Giri onion market along the Gwagwalada-Airport road junction destroyed
during the president's tour of the FCT is springing up again as traders have
begun reconstruction.
When Abuja Trust called at the market over the weekend, the usually busy
market noted for cheap supplies of onions, local and Irish potatoes, fruits
and yams was a shadow of itself.
Apart from few traders displaying their wares mostly onions on tables in the
middle of the ruins of their shanty shades, the ever busy market would have
passed for a ghost town.
Some of the traders with members of their families were seen gathering
sticks for the reconstruction of their shades.
Some of then who spoke with Abuja Trust described the incident as a blow
especially during their festive period.
"This is a very big blow," one of the traders said. "They burnt everything
here to ashes just because President Obasanjo was passing by.  Who told them
that Obasanjo was not aware of road side markets," he queried.
Abuja Trust gathered that men of the Abuja Environmental Protection Board
had destroyed the Giri market in readiness for President Obasanjo's visit
last week.
In a related development, owners of road structures destroyed in preparation
for the visit are returning to base.  Some of them affected in Berger, Bwari
and Dutse are mobilising to site, Abuja Trust gathered.

Original_Search_Title: Bone of Contention
Headline: Nigeria: Bone of Contention
Body_Text:
Do traditional healers do a better job of healing broken bones? That is the bone of contention
Godwin Ijakoli, 42, a yam trader at Ketu,   Lagos   fractured his ankle in a motor accident along   Ore/Benin Road  . Doctors, on examination of his case at the   National  Orthopaedic  Hospital  , Igbobi,   Lagos   told him that his leg was badly damaged. His chances of walking again, they said, were slim. The doctors recommended amputation. Ijakoli did not want to lose his leg. In his desperation, he opted for native treatment at Akaeze village, Ivo local government area of   Ebonyi  State  . Two months after he began his treatment Ijakoli could walk again.
More and more people with similar cases are going to traditional healers.
Richard Imhansuen, a prominent traditional bone healer at Old Army Barracks, Abule Egba,   Lagos  , said people preferred traditional treatment because modern medicine had failed many others. He told Newswatch that he had successfully treated people who could not get healed from the   National  Orthopaedic  Hospital  , Igbobi,   Lagos  .
Imhansuen told Newswatch that bone treatment methods varied from one healing home to the other. Some traditional healers use herbs for treatment. Some use what is loosely known as "chicken approach." Before the treatment begins, the leg of a live chicken is broken. "As the broken leg of the chicken gets healed, so also is the person," Imhausuen said.
Leonard Mbamara, medical director, Albina Mejeel Clinic, Iba, Ojo,   Lagos   and an orthopaedic surgeon said amputation would be necessary only when the damaged leg becomes a threat to the entire body.
He said the attraction for the traditional operators was because of its accessibility in the rural areas coupled with the cheap cost of treament.
A doctor at NOH, Igbobi, who didn't want his name in print said native bone healers were doing what they knew little or nothing about. He told Newswatch that as far as bone treatment was concerned, traditionalists only engaged themselves in trial-and-error, thereby complicating problems for ignorant patients. He said if it worked out for them, they claimed credit, believing that they had "superior forces" working for them. But when it fails, patients always bear the brunt and go to hospital as a last resort.
Sylvanus N. Omeonu, medical director and orthopaedic surgeon,   Hopeville  Hospital   and Maternity, Gboko,   Benue  State  , however, did not rule out the potentials of traditional bone healers completely. He said some native doctors have acquired the skills of 'reduction and fixation' through their traditional means of massaging the affected area with herbs and other means.
Omeonu noted that the method works for them in some cases, especially when there is no open wound. He said that patients were at the mercy of these practitioners when there was open wound and it was infected. He said the obvious handicap of traditionalists was ignorance. "They know little or nothing about the body anatomy, but are concerned only about the bones. So, any other problem affecting the body system, the patients are left to their fate," Omeonu stated.
He said the advantage doctors have over traditional healers was that they were specially trained and understood the body anatomy better. "Happily, however, many traditional bone healers are now improving on their practice by attaching medical personnel to their healing homes," he explained.

Original_Search_Title: Police Sell Impounded Yam
Headline: Nigeria: Police Sell Impounded Yam
Body_Text:
Four truckloads of looted yam impounded by the police in Taraba State about two weeks ago have been sold to the public.
The yam tubers were sold members of the public on Tuesday at the command headquarters, Jalingo, where people appeared in large number to buy the commodity. Ten tubers of yam were sold for N150 as against the market price of N750.
The police public relations officer, Mr Clement Robert, said it was necessary to dispose the perishable commodity as the owners failed to show up.
Robert said that the police impounded another three truckloads of looted yam, adding that proceeds from the sale would be deposited into government treasury.
The PPRO said that some of the yam would be bought by the state government for distribution to refugees in various camps in the state.
Commenting on the latest conflict between the Tiv and the Jukuns in Dongah Local Government, Robert said policemen had been dispatched to the area to restore peace.

Original_Search_Title: Police Sell Impounded Yam
Headline: Nigeria: Police Sell Impounded Yam
Body_Text:
Four-truck load of looted yam impounded by the police in Taraba State about
two weeks ago have been sold at a paltry sum.
The yam tubers were sold to members of the public on Tuesday at the command
headquarters, Jalingo, where people rushed in droves to buy the commodity.
The News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) reported that 10 tubers of yam were sold at
N150 as against the market price of N750.
The Police Public Relations Officer (PPRO), Mr. Clement Robert, said it was
necessary to dispose of the perishable commodity as the owners had failed to
show up.
Robert said that the police had impounded another three-truck load of looted
yam, adding that proceeds from the sale would be deposited in government
treasury.
The PPRO said that some of the yam would be bought by the state government
for distribution to refugees in various camps in the state.
Commenting on the latest conflict between the Tiv and the Jukuns in Donga
Local Government Area, Robert said policemen had been dispatched to the area
to contain the situation.

Original_Search_Title: Yam Sellers Threaten to Boycott Transport Company
Headline: Ghana: Yam Sellers Threaten to Boycott Transport Company
Body_Text:
Akosombo — A 500-Member yam sellers association drawn from Kete-Krachi, Dambai,
Accra, Krachi Asaman and some outlying villages have threatened to
boycott the Volta Lake Transport Company Limited (VLTC) unless
positive steps are taken to improve their service.
To this end, the members have called on the Volta River Authority
(VRA) to take serious view of the matter so as to avoid any
embarrassment.
The members decision was made known to the press after an emergency
meeting with the commercial director, Mr. Erzuah Miezah and Mr.
Baiden, both of VLTC at Akosombo over the weekend.
Mr. Frank Agyekum, chairman of the association, said poor service by
VLTC has plunged most members into serious financial crisis resulting
in majority of them abandoning the yam trade.
According to Agyekum, management's interest supersedes that of the
numerous customers on board the vessel, adding that in spite of the
numerous appeals to management to improve operation of the pantoon,
their request had not been heeded to.
He stated that VLTC has failed to make forklift operators do overtime
to discharge all perishable goods knowing very well that the company
has only one forklift machine to discharge several tonnes of goods on
the boat.
The chairman mentioned that for the past 30 years that yam sellers
had engaged in business of transporting yams and other things by
river, VLTC has never given them any kind of comfort, besides the
cheating and fraudulent means to live.
Agyekum cited an instance where Miss Faustina Sagoe, a toll collector
issued a Â¢40,000 ticket but paid Â¢17,000 to the company and revealed
that almost all ticket and toll collectors have built houses out of
their nefarious activities.
"We can no longer sit down for personnel of VLTC to continue playing
around us only for us to swim in the pool of debt", Agyekum
emphasised.

Original_Search_Title: Muscles Muscles And More Muscles
Headline: Uganda: Muscles Muscles And More Muscles
Body_Text:
Isaac Nsumba says the sport is not for the low class but a sport for all to keep fit
In case you have not met Nsumba, he is the young man who appears on your screen before WBS television sports news time. That man with tensed muscles, shinny skin carrying a TV set with a WBS TV logo.
To the lay person its called body building to a body builder its weight lifting, body building, and power lifting.
Isaac Nsumba says he starts his day with training at any gym, one of the benefits of his achievement. "National body builders don't pay any fee to do their training in any gym around the country," he says.
Nsumba recites the times he started the sport in 1992. "I first got inspired by the movies I watched when I was young like Commando," an action packed movie produced early 1990s starring Arnold Schwarzneger. "Schwarzneger was my idol. I wondered how he got his big body, which I admired," he narrates.
He says that when he inquired about the sporting facilities from friends, 'They told me to go to sporting gym centres and begin training."
This led him to the Nakivubo Power Gym, where upon he took his interest seriously. "I seriously begun body building in my senior one 1995 while studying at Kampala Grammar Secondary School." He then looks at me in the eye, as though asking me, is that enough? And continues, "I was 16 years old by then, weighing 40 or 48 kgs," and looks up as though the answers were pegged somewhere up on the iron roof. "Hmm...48!".
During his ordinary level he would leave school at 4:00 p.m. and run to the gym to train for two hours. At 6:00 p.m. he would leave for home. "I used to watch my colleagues compete." He never got a chance because of his under weight.
"When I made 68kgs I qualified for the first time for Mr. Kampala Lightweight Bodybuilding Competitions," in a low tone. "Though I was not so used I only got experience, this helped me gain confidence in standing and posing on stage" adds. "I did not meet the standards," meaning he didn't get a trophy. So he trained so hard also gaining more appetite in the process.
He again attended the Mr. Kampala Bodybuilding Competitions 1996 and walked away with a trophy. "I later went for Mr. Carnivore competitions-1996 in Nairobi, Kenya and returned with a trophy."
It was quite a challenge for the team of six muscular men he says, tilting his head forward, and gesturing with his right arm.
The biggest show ever came in 1999, he traveled to Egypt for the first East and Central Africa Bodybuilding Championships. Here he came face to face with the greatest challenge of his life. After a stiff competition with different participants from other African countries, he managed to return with a trophy.
"I think I have about seven or eight trophies," he tries to recollect, slides his left hand into the pocket of his deep blue jeans, draws out a wallet and searches inside for more records, fails to find them and mutters to himself.
"I think they are not here, I shall check at home later, but I know they are seven," he reaffirms. True he got seven trophies in his course of competitions.
Isaac, now 25 years says he has benefited a lot from the exercises "if you do a lot of exercises it improves your health and prolongs your life." he explains with an aura of authority and good faith.
As more body builders pour into the gym the noise also increases, the music blares. He stares at the coach who carries weights at short intervals while conversing in a staccato like voice to another trainer.
"I got to know a lot of people, got trophies, money and offers from big companies to do adverts, for their products." Not only has he gained popularity from the sport but he has also travelled to many countries and places that he confesses he never expected to set sight on. At the moment, "I have an offer from Health and Performance Products (U) ltd to do them promotions, which I think is good achievement".
From the numerous prizes and awards he got Nsumba has been able to pay his school fees. "Since the death of my father I have been able to maintain my self in school from what I earn," he says in a melancholic tone.
At noon, he starts another part of his day as a steward at Cineplex/DV 8 Bar and Bistro. He pulls out an identity card to confirm this point. "Now I weigh 76 kilogrammes, which is middleweight. I have won three trophies and other prizes with this weight."
"It took me only six months, to gain a weight of 64kgs which means my body responds quickly to food stuffs and supplements."
He says, other people's bodies may not quickly respond to the food required to build it in a shorter time. However, he quickly adds that may not mean that one's body cannot be built. His diet is a full pack of the necessary foodstuffs like egg white and Soya flour, two yam tubers or cassava plus five raw eggs, for lunch. After two hours, he takes a pint of milk and fruit salad. He also takes food supplements.
"At dinner I eat five boiled eggs, with passion fruit juice or fresh water mixed with muscle recovery powder. But at breakfast a cup of milk, four pieces of bread plus two fingers of ripe bananas," he says. All these make his daily diet.
The celebrated body builder dismisses the idea that bodybuilding is for the lowly and the naive.
"Body building is good for everyone, the young and adults alike but many people are not aware. Women also enjoy the sport unfortunately we've not had any woman participating, maybe because they don't have time," he says while gazing at a couple of women selling food.
"But in developed countries and the countries I travelled to like Egypt many people love bodybuilding and a lot of women participate in the competitions," Despite his popularity, Nsumba is not yet married but says, "I look forward to getting married when the right time comes. I had a girl friend, Matilda, we had misunderstandings and broke up."
Some girls say they are scared of his muscles they think he can hurt them if they annoy him but others are not. "They make fun and always desire to befriend me," he shyly reveals.
The bodybuilder says he does not fight people, despite his massive size. Though he fought once to prevent intruders from entering a show.
Isaac Nsumba, five feet four inch was born on the May 15, 1976 to the late Saul Richard Nsumba and Ruth Nsumba a secretary at the faculty of Arts Makerere University. His sporting career, has earned him a couple of trophies which include:
Mr. Carnivore-1996, East and Central African Body building Championships-1999; Best Sportsman of the Year, Body Builder 2000, Mr. Kampala 2000 and 2001, Mr. Hercules Bodybuilding Competitions 2001.
His coach, Sali Salongo, speaks well of him, "Since I started training him, Isaac has always been so hardworking trying to learn everything I teach him. Though it was not easy for him to catch up very fast."
Pauses and runs back to train and instruct then returns, "He's doing a great job now," he adds while nodding his head.
Nsumba is a darling of fans of bodybuilding. Peter a bodybuilder and a fan says Isaac is a dedicated body builder. "He's respectful and humble." Some young ladies, who admitted they are Isaac's fans, expressed their love for him. "He looks a hunk on stage", one lady said; "I admire his body size", another interjected.
In Uganda, the sport though popular among men of a certain class has a long history of existence as Coach Sali Salongo says. It was founded by a group of Indians then living in Jinja.
James Onyango 1972 took the interest and teamed up with a few youth like Rashid Kawayida, Agong, and Kwaha Sali among others. After the expulsion of Asians from Uganda.
Onyango and his colleagues moved to Kampala for a wider audience. Then President Idi Amin Dada loved and supported the sport, which was mixed with boxing and wrestling, and it gained more support.
Salongo Ssali the coach heads a team of over 250 body builders, weight lifters and power lifters at the Nakivubo Sports Club.
To ensure the best outcome in their competitions a selected number of committee members sit to pick out abler people or those fit for the competitions.
The jovial coach says between pauses as he runs back to exercise. "We have travelled a lot with this precious Sport, since we started, in 1997 for the first time we went for Mr. Universe Championship in Jordan which revealed that we were really serious."
Salongo the coach heads a team of over 250 body builders, weight lifters and power lifters at the Nakivubo Sports Club. To ensure the best outcome in their competitions a selected number of committee members sit to pick out abler people or those fit for the competitions.
Their greatest obstacle, the coach says, is getting sponsorship for their shows. "Though bodybuilding companies can promote their products, If companies could come out to sponsor body building activities, it will be well perceived in the whole country like football," the 100kg, large chested and healthy looking , Salongo reveals inbetween gestures.

Original_Search_Title: Hon. Leo Welfare Foundation Launched
Headline: Nigeria: Hon. Leo Welfare Foundation Launched
Body_Text:
Lagos — At an historic ceremony that drew thousands of chieftains and
faithful of Alliance for Democracy (AD), Honourable Leo Chioma
Welfare Foundation was launched in Lagos at the Ward F3, Taiwo
Street, Okota Secretariat of the party.
Speaking at the occasion, Special Adviser to Governor Bola Ahmed
Tinubu on Legislative Matters, Honourable (Chief) Ajomale described
the effort of Honourable Leo Chioma, the councillor representing Ward
F3 in the Oshodi/Isolo Local Government as a "pace setting' one.
Chief Ajomale, who represented Senator Bola Ahmed Tinubu at the
occasion, urged members of AD to begin serious grassroots
mobilisation and to be on guard so as to thwart the efforts of the
rival party, the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) from rigging the
forthcoming elections in the state.
Said he: "Lagos State has been programmed for rigging by the PDP. We
have the facts." He assured that when new local governments are
created in the state, Isolo Local Government would be one of them.
Earlier in his speech, Hon. Leo Chioma said his concern for the
people made him to set up the foundation. He described democracy as a
collective responsibility, adding that since he was elected to
represent the Okota community, he has put up a number of projects,
including road construction and rehabilitation.
Honourable Niyi Fabikun of the Lagos State House of Assembly
representing Oshodi-Isolo noted that Honourable Chioma's effort was a
challenge to other councillors in the state. He called on Lagosians
to vote massively for Governor Tinubu come 2003.
In an exclusive chat at the occasion, Chairman of the party in the
ward, Mr. Wahab A. Raji lauded Honourable Chioma for his initiative
and expressed the readiness of the party to retain the seat in the
next election.
Over 200 beneficiaries of the welfare scheme went home with
quantities of rice and tubers of yam. They included Mr. Kayode
Adebayo and Mrs. Deborah Adebiyi.
Chieftains of AD who were also at the occasion include Chief A.B.
Taiwo, Engineer Alade, Mrs. Yinka Taiwo and Elder Oguntoye.

Original_Search_Title: FCT Residents React to Bank's Proposed Coins
Headline: Nigeria: FCT Residents React to Bank's Proposed Coins
Body_Text:
A cross section of residents of the Federal Capital Territory (FCT) have
expressed reservations over plans by the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) to
introduce N5, N10 and N20 coins in the country.
Speaking with Abuja Trust on Monday, the respondents described the plan as a
further step to increase inflation, and make things more difficult for the
poor.
According to Mrs. Gloria Onyiuke who sells yam in the New Wuse and Karmo
markets, government has no business tampering with the existing currency
notes as they are.  She expressed a lot of anger with the proposed move by
the apex bank, saying "it is another step to push the poor masses deeper
into the abyss."
"I cannot understand," she fumed, "or, may be, they know better, but on my
part, and in the minds of most market women like me, the plan is most
unacceptable."
Asked why she does not support the proposal, Mrs. Onyiuke who spoke in Igbo
reverberated; "I have told you it makes things costlier, for instance, where
are the one naira and fifty kobo coins today?  Have they been phased out?
Answer me! Then, after that, how any things can you buy for one naira and
fifty kobo now? Answer me!"
Another woman, Mrs. Idenyi Asogwa, who runs a canteen in Garki, told Abuja
Trust that if the government were to conduct a survey to ascertain the
feelings of the people before implementing that proposal, they would drop
the idea because according to her, the plan is only popular among the very
rich.
"We are the people who suffer most of the difficult decisions taken on the
top.  If for instance I have N3,000, imagine the coins I would carry.  If
you are saying that I should avoid carrying so many coins, and other people
avoid carrying coins in large amount, it means the currency units which
appear in coins are gradually fading out, do you imagine the consequence of
that?"
Idenyi said if she had the means, she would mobilise all women in her
category to embark on a protest march against the proposal.
In her own reaction, Mr. Candidus Idehen, a commercial bus driver, said the
plan would die a "natural death" because according to him, it is not in the
interest of the poor, who are in the majority.
"If I had my way, I would reactivate the N1,50 kobo and other coins.  In the
first place, I do not support the minting of N1 coin, not to talk of N5 and
N10 coins.  In that case, we would be carrying so many coins per trip!"
On his part, Mr Ibrahim Musa, a student, appealed to government and the CBN
to shelve the plan.  According him, the income level of majority of
Nigerians cannot conveniently accommodate such adjustments at the moment
"Its like they base their plans or decisions on what obtains in the cities.
How convenient do they think this change would be to the villagers?
Gradually, the lower denominations of the currency would cease to attract
any purchasing power," he said.
Several other respondents who obliged to speak to Abuja Trust, condemned the
move, saying it is not acceptable by the masses, who they said, have more
need for the smaller denominations than the rich.  A banker, who spoke on
the condition of anonymity, told Abuja Trust that he and many of his
colleagues were not in tune with the plan.
"But we are just like sheep, where the shepherd shoves them, they move; who
am I to challenge a CBN decision?" he queried.

Original_Search_Title: Fears of Fresh Troop Deployment Spur Returnees' Flight
Headline: Nigeria: Fears of Fresh Troop Deployment Spur Returnees' Flight
Body_Text:
There are strong indications that the Federal Government has deployed more troops to the war ravaged town of Zaki-Biam, sending palpable fear into the returnees and forcing them to once again flee for fear of a fresh attack by the military.
The development which comes barely 48 hours after the Speaker of the Federal House of Representative, Alhaji Ghali Umar Na'Abba joined other Nigerians in the persistent call on the Federal Government to withdraw soldiers deployed to trouble spots along the Benue/Taraba border.
A visit by THISDAY to Zaki-Biam town confirmed that soldiers armed with armoured tanks also used an army truck to drive into the rubble of the once vibrant and biggest yam market in Nigeria.
The presence of more soldiers using armoured vehicles has halted the striking number of returnees who inspite of strong fear were returning into the area out of sheer hopelessness for survival if they continued staying in the bush.
THISDAY also learnt that since the October 20 army reprisal attack on Zaki-Biam and 19 other villages in Benue State the area has been reduced to a jungle with no administrative leadership, no police and no form of security against further attacks.
The council chairman Hon. Ityoakoso has fled . So have the police who a police source said have been directed to stay out of the town. The PPRO Benue State Police Command however said the police have abandoned the area due to lack of office.
A young man who identified himself as Terwsetold THISDAY, that they are once again fleeing because they fear the arrival of more military man with heavy weapons who attack the returnees.
The returnees fear that with such utterances coming out of the nations Defence Minister Lt. General Theophilus Y. Danjuma and Senator representing Taraba South Senatorial district, Senator Sangari, and the inability of the Federal Government to restrain them the role of even the federal Government is suspect in the circumstance.
Only last Tuesday the Speaker, House of Representative, Alhaji Ghali Umar Na'Abba had during his diplomatic visit to Benue State, stopped short to years when he visited the affected areas where he called on the Federal Government to withdraw the army without further delay.
Na'Abba who had also insisted that the Federal Government should never again use of soldiers of quell any civil unrest saying "I don't support the use of the military in any civil situation because it can not be a panacea", was speaking the minds of many others.
Benue State governor, Mr. George Akume had also called on the Federal Government to withdraw the soldiers from Benue State.
Apart from governor Akume, the Tor Tiv IV Dr. Alfred Akawe Torkula has also called on the Federal Government to withdraw the military men who he said rather then provide a solution to the problem they have constituted themselves into another problem, rapping women and committing other atrocities.
Already soldiers stationed along the Katsina-Ala /Zaki-Biam road have resorted to extorting money from motorists as well as food stuff as yams and animals being taken to market.

Original_Search_Title: Official Clears 'Gift' to Black  Star
Headline: Nigeria/Ghana: Official Clears 'Gift' to Black  Star
Body_Text:
GOVERNOR Peter Odili's monetary gift to members of the Black Stars
after a World Cup game against the Super Eagles should not be made an
issue, a top NFA official has said.
Rather, the gift, the official said portrays the hospitality inherent
in African culture.
According to him, "it is a common thing in Africa to give gifts to
visitors when they are about to go.
In the olden days, our forefathers gave yam tubers, goats, cowries
and so on.
So, if the Governor of Rivers State in his magnanimity decided to
give a visiting team some money after a game, he is keeping to the
culture of his people.
It will be act of mischief to read any other meaning to the gesture",
said the F.A official who pleaded for anonymity.
And in Ghana, the Executive Council of the Ghana Football Association
over the weekend cleared Mr. Ben Koufie of any wrong doing over the
affair.
An emergency meeting convened in the wake of widespread attack on the
GFA boss which roped in journalists accompanying the team, stressed
that the bribery allegations against them were "unfounded and without
merit".
The matter has gained international media attention and was discussed
widely even on the world wide web as well.
The following comment was on one such US based site: I was not in
Port Harcourt to witness the euphoria following the match, but from
the reports that I have seen, "the only real issue here is that the
Governor is apparently misspending taxpayers money.
Unless of course it is his own personal money (I understand that he
was a wealthy US-based doctor), in which case he is suffering from a
paucity of judgment (or just too giddy from too much champagne)"

Original_Search_Title: Encourage Use of Shea Butter - Gov't Told
Headline: Ghana: Encourage Use of Shea Butter - Gov't Told
Body_Text:
Koforidua — A Koforidua industrialist and herbal medicine manufacturer, Alhaji
Ahmed Idris, has appealed to the government to assist entrepreneurs
to expand the use of shea butter with the view to improving the
incomes of the millions whose livelihood depends on the wild tree
product in the north.
Idris, also known as Alhaji Awareso, who has for decades been buying
and selling shea butter routed the appeal through our Eastern Region
Bureau at Koforidua.
"After years of trading in the shea butter, I have come to observe
that our poor mothers and brothers who pick the pods and sell them
are earning too little to support them in this era when the cost of
living is getting tougher and tougher".
He, therefore, called for the diversification of the use to which the
butter is put so as to increase its demand and improve its producer
price.
He was optimistic that if researchers and manufacturers co-operate
and are backed with adequate funding, the objective would be realised
in no time.
At the moment, apart from the butter being used as pomade by village
folks and as cooking oil in the northern regions, little use is made
of the Saharan product.
But "we can train our women to extract the edible oil from it in such
a way that it will be palatable, and attractive to the sophisticated
urban dweller as well" Iddris argued, adding that the products even
have export potentials.
Other uses the extracted fat can be put to is hair Vaseline, perfumed
body cream and a variety of lotions that can cure skin decease, he
said.
"There is also no doubt that research can lead to the discovery of
other products that can be derived from the raw material," Alhaji was
optimistic.
The extraction of the butter from the pod remains a job done through
crude and inefficient methods.
According to Iddris, the storage of the butter is equally poorly
done, as lack of modern technology and the adulteration of the stuff
with yam "fufu" and the like expose the butter to fast deterioration.
Already manufacturing talcum and dealing in shea butter, Alhaji
Awareso said he knew many entrepreneurs can take up the challenge if
given the push.

Original_Search_Title: Pete Edochie: Honour For an Artiste
Headline: Nigeria: Pete Edochie: Honour For an Artiste
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ohazulike, at a colourful ceremony he and his council organised to
mark this year's New Yam Festival, honoured a few Igbo sons with
chieftaincy titles.
Recipients of such deserved honours included a renowned home video
artiste, Pete Edochie (a.k.a.
Okonkwo). FIDELIS NJOKU was there and files in this report
In Igboland, it is uncommon to see a person honoured strictly for his
or her moral service to the community or society without any
reasonable material contributions attached to it. This may explain
the reason why honours, traditional or otherwise, are most often
bestowed on money bags in that part of the world regardless of the
source of such material wealth and the characters of the persons
honoured.
Thank God, things change with time. In this generation of the Igbo
race, Ezeigbo, Lagos State, H.R.H. Igwe Hyacinth Ohazulike, tends to
reverse the negative trend. The Igwe Omereoha I of Ndigbo feels like
a few others in Igboland that money is not everything.
Service to one's fatherland, he believes, cannot be measured in terms
of material contributions only.
Therefore, as deserving wealthy citizens are honoured for touching
lives and making economic impact on the society, those who have used
their God-given skills or talents to positively affect the society
also deserve honour.
The Ezeigbo demonstrated just this recently when he honoured at a New
Yam Festival held in his palace at Ifako three Igbo sons who have
made enviable marks in the art industry through home video
productions. The most popular and outstanding among them is Pete
Edochie, now Chief Pete Edochie, the Ebubedike I of Ndigbo.
Until he acted Okonkwo in the drama version of Chinua Achebe's most
popular novel, "Things Fall Apart" Chief Pete Edochie was not quite
visible in the art industry. But with his marvellous debut in the
"Things Fall Apart" drama, Edochie rose suddenly from grace to grace.
His central role as Okonkwo, the protagonist of the book, gave him
out as an artiste par excellence. Those who have read and understood
the character and role of the real Okonkwo as vividly painted by
Prof. Achebe in the novel testify that Edochie did not only play the
vantage part excellently but that his physique also resembles that of
the real Okonkwo being referred to in the book.
It is perhaps on this note that ever since then, Pete Edochie is
called Okonkwo or "Ebubedike" which is also a title name ascribed to
Okonkwo in the novel.
While conferring the title on Edochie and other artistes recently,
Igwe Ohazulike said Igbo artistes like Pete Edochie have done much to
revive Igbo cultural and traditional values. He stated that Edochie
and others in the art industry are doing good work by revisiting
Igbos cherished traditional values and cultural heritage through
their creative art works. The Igwe opined that the value of the art
works of Pete Edochie and others goes beyond mere entertainment. As
far as he is concerned, they go a long way to remind the Igbos of
their root. For their immense service, Pete Edochie and others
deserved to be honoured and encouraged, Igwe Ohazulike stated.
Soon after being conferred with the chieftaincy title of "Ebubedike
of Ndigbo", Arts Parade had a chat with Chief Pete Edochie. Excerpts:
Q: Chief, how do you feel about the chieftaincy titles conferred on
you and others?
A: I feel highly elated and encouraged by the honour done me and my
colleagues today. I think it is a monumental recognition of our
humble moral service to humanity and fatherland. I thank Ezeigbo,
Lagos State for this singular recognition extended to us.
Q: Is acting your part time or main job presently?
A: I am presently a full time professional artiste.
Q: How long have you been in the business?
A: I can't remember exactly how long, but I have been in the business
for quite a long time. You should know that I had been in the
business working underground long before I made breakthroughs and
became noticed in the profession a few years ago.
Q: Which of your works gave you notice?
A: From what I could gather from public opinion, my role as Okonkwo
of "Things Fall Apart" gave me a big mark and attracted unprecedented
public attention to me as an artiste.
Q: Beside your role in the drama version of "Things Fall Apart" which
other work have you played a leading role?
A: A lot! Without sounding immodest, I play major part in any art
work I am involved. So, virtually all the parts I have played so far
in films and other art works can be described as central and pivotal.
Q: Which one are you working on now?
A: I am not just working on one. I and my colleagues are right now
very busy with a few films. One or two of them may be ready in no
distant time from now. Of course, I can't tell you their titles now.
Q: What are they like?
A: Oh! They are fantastic. It is better you see for yourself . As I
said earlier, it will not be long from now by the grace of God.
Q: Chief, what impact do you think your artworks have made on the
society?
A: A lot of impact. We have used art to remind our people of their
root, where they are coming from. We have created and sustained high
tempo awareness of our traditional and cultural heritage among the
city populace most of who know little or nothing about their
tradition and culture. We have this intention of letting the Western
world know that Africa has a rich cultural heritage which deserves
international attention and I think we are already achieving this
goal. Moreso, you can agree with me that African artistes have fairly
succeeded in diverting and refocusing the attention of film watchers
from Western films to African films. By this singular feat, we have
given our people a cultural identity and a sense of belonging in the
continent.
The moral education being imparted on the young and the old through
African-oriented films cannot be over-stressed. Also, the economic
and entertainment value of African art works is unquantifiable.
Frankly, African artistes, be they Igbo, Yoruba or any other
ethnic-based artistes across Africa, including my humble self have
made appreciable impact on the society.
The event of today is an eloquent testimony to what I am saying and
we thank God for granting us the opportunity to play our own small
part to make our fatherland and Africa as a whole great.
Q: Chief, what does it take to be a successful artiste?
A: Talent, hardwork, commitment and perseverance. A good measure of
Western education is also necessary.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo: Commander in-Chief of Aso Rock?
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo: Commander in-Chief of Aso Rock?
Body_Text:
"I am happy that the armed forces have not played into the hands of
the perpetrators and remain calm and at the same time (are) accepting
the situation as occupational hazard ... No amount of provocation
will make us abandon this desirable, needed and noble cause" -
President Olusegun Obasanjo, October 22, 2001.
WHEN President Obasanjo made this pronouncement during the burial
ceremony for the 19 soldiers killed in Benue State allegedly by Tiv
youths, the Commander-in-Chief almost sent me to the hospital; I
almost choked on the Gulder, (for me its Gulder or Guinness, to keep
my sanity in Nigeria), in my mouth at the time. Obasanjo has acquired
a reputation as a funny fellow who could have made a distinguished
career as a comedian of high rank if the army had not got him first.
But praising the Nigerian army for self_restraint when tempers were
still high was deadly comedy revealing how far removed the C-in-C was
from the forces he is presumed to command. So, with certainty, I told
a colleague after recovering from fits of coughing that the President
would soon eat his words. The Nigerian army the rest of us have known
since Obasanjo's friend Major Chukwuma Nzeogwu led the first coup can
be accused of many things (usually evil) but restraint is not one of
them. I knew that Tivs would soon pay dearly for the 19 lives the
army lost. The army, predictably struck back as soon as the bodies of
their fallen colleagues were lowered into the grave, giving the
C-in-C a slap in the face and went in to slaughter 200 or more in
reprisals. They even gave former Chief of Army Staff a taste of their
deadly medicine
The reasons why the massacre occurred despite the C-in-C's thinly
veiled plea to soldiers in the statement above are not hard to
discover: The Nigerian army which General Obasanjo and his Chief of
Army Staff, Lt. Gen.
Theophilus Danjuma (rtd) left behind in 1979, which by itself was a
lawless bunch from the topmost to the last recruit had, gradually
disintegrated to the point where a former Chief of Army Staff, Lt.
Gen. Salisu in departing, described it as an "army where nothing
goes". The horror show at the Oputa Panel has revealed the lower
depths to which the Nigerian Army had descended under IBB, Abacha and
Abubakar.
Thus, the army Obasanjo re-inherited in 1999 was nothing but the same
bunch of undisciplined "gangsters" (apologies to Lt. Gen. Alani
Akinrinade (rtd) his successors had groomed to terrorise civilians
and to commit genocide, if necessary, in order to ensure their hold
on power.
Granted that the Nigerian Army had not always been cohesive and
honourable according to a book written by Maj. Gen. James Oluleye
(rtd), now being serialized by Nigerian Tribune. In fact it would
appear that military discipline departed from the Nigeria armed
forces the minute the last British soldier departed and Colonel
Ironsi was made the Chief of Army Staff instead of Colonel
Ademulegun. The coup that toppled Ironsi was perhaps the first
demonstration of how degenerate the army had become in a few years.
The detachment of soldiers led by Maj.Theophilus Danjuma, the current
Minister of Defence, already had Ironsi (then Commander in Chief)
cornered with his host Colonel Adekunle Fajuyi; they could have asked
the man to surrender and put them in detention the way Babangida did
with Buhari; they could even have sent them on exile. Instead,
General Ironsi and Colonel Fajuyi were thoroughly beaten to the point
of death by junior officers before Danjuma reportedly shot Ironsi.
Army discipline has not improved since that fateful day. Quite the
contrary, it has gone from bad to worse.
Today there is no discipline. An officer and a gentleman is an
oxymoron in Nigeria, there is no gentleman soldier. That was why,
Obasanjo's grave side address and the implied plea for restraint
which if made by the President of any great country, instead of a
Banana Republic, would have been accepted as an order. But not the
Nigerian Army.
The last four or five Chiefs of Army Staff rose through the ranks of
an army where promotion was not determined by competence but by the
ability to successfully gang up against superior officers and push
them out of office.
Murderers, rapists, safe crackers have reached high ranks in the
Nigerian Army whose armed chair Generals and Brigadiers cannot
withstand an Israeli captain in combat. So, the army ignored
Obasanjo's plea for restraint and went on a rampage as if the C-in-C
had spoken only for himself because in fact he did. And when the
C-in-C ordered them to leave Benue, one saucy officer proclaimed that
they would not withdraw until they fish out the culprits. Apparently
after killing 200 people in retaliation, our blood thirsty soldiers
still want more blood.
Meanwhile, the President who once prematurely praised them for
self-restraint has found some virtue in retaliation. According to him
people who shoot soldiers are asking for trouble. Well, we knew that
all along and we don't need a President to remind us. In fact we can
tell the President that people need not kill a soldier for the armed
gang he leads as C-in-C to commit murder. Ikorodu town in Lagos State
recently experienced army brutality when soldiers went on rampage on
account of a matter that should have been handled by the police but
which was not because it involved soldiers.
Till today, if a hit-and-run driver kills a soldier near an Army
Barracks, everybody in the vicinity should run for cover, even
innocent passers by are not spared. Obasanjo, like his predecessors
might be C-in-C of Aso Rock but his influence beyond the gates of the
Rock over the armed forces is doubtful. And he has the embodiment of
military indiscipline in 1966 as his Defence Minister.
N200 million Media Tour
"First things last" apparently is the motto of some of our honourable
ministers. The Ministry of Information finally released the cost of
the media tour which according to one source was N1.7 billion and
according to another N200 million. Even if the lower figure is
accepted, in the spirit of transparency and accountability, the
honourable minister should at least circulate to all the media houses
the break down of the expenditure. It will be interesting to discover
who got what during the loot sharing. I can bet a Naira that
journalists got the short end of the stick, ministry officials and
food contractors got more.
Still N200 million to undertake in earnest a task which should have
been left to the media itself is not the best way to spend N200
million. FUTO like all Nigerians universities needs a micro film unit
to enable the university store in its library more reading materials
and preserve them from pilferage and damage and to conserve space;
one unit (tokunbo) goes for about N500,000. All Nigerian universities
and polytechnics could use one and N200 million would get 400 units.
To me that's a better way to spend the money than through a media
jamboree designed to lure the tourists to sleep. How many boreholes
nationwide? How about filling potholes on the Onitsha Owerri Road,
Oga minister?
Now a minister in a government where officials have been "invited to
come and chop"might be forgiven for not knowing that you don't have
to blow the budget to sedate journalists. Why? for under N2 million,
I can send all Nigerian journalists to bed on Gulder and pounded yam
at the Kirikiri Canal. If the Gulder does not get them, the lumpy
pounded yam will finish them.
Last word on Governor Adefarati's accident
We received a rejoinder from Mr. Pady Dare, Chief Press Secretary to
Governor Adefarati of Ondo State disputing my article of October 21,
2001.
The rejoinder was erroneously sent to Ambassador Sagay because he
writes on Page 9.
As the real author of the report, I want to observe that Mr. Pady
Dare added no new information to what the governor himself had
stated. I was on the scene of the accident and I interviewed those
who witnessed the actual impact. I repeat without fear that the dead
young Kogi pharmacist was the victim not the cause of the ghastly
accident in which he died.
What motive would I have for maligning Chief Adefarati who is a
Yoruba leader and I am from the same tribe whereas the dead young man
is unknown to me? Truth must be told because above all, I know we
shall all die one day. The late Kogien youth is not around to defend
himself. It will be a shame though if some of us allow our conscience
to die before our bodies go six feet under out of deference to
transient holders of power.

Original_Search_Title: Media Tour: The Undeserved Vituperation
Headline: Nigeria: Media Tour: The Undeserved Vituperation
Body_Text:
I HAVE always viewed the column, Frankly Speaking, as one of the few
remaining oases of professional excellence in today's desert of
cynical journalism in Nigeria.
However, the piece on the national media tour, Sunday Vanguard,
October 14, was a clear indication that the raging fire of cynicism
is already consuming the semblance of elderly responsibility and
decorum left in the profession.
In the piece in question, the writer, Dele Sobowale, chose to ignore
the national media tour's myriads of positive effects on our people's
socio economic and political awareness since its commencement in June
this year.
He also deliberately refused to acknowledge the programme's
tremendous facilitation of the right of the media to know how the
nation's new democratic governance is faring, at federal, state, and
local council levels, towards improving the lot of Nigerians.
Instead, he unleashed his usual tirade on Gana, invoking the memory
of 'Gana in MAMSER", "Gana in SDP," and so on, in an obvious rehash
of his familiar demonstration of an absurd distaste for Professor
Jerry Gana's continous loyal service to his fatherland. He may not
believe it, but God was a witness when I recently objected to some
journalists' angry suggestion in my office, after reading the piece
in question, that he nowadays cut the picture of a tired "veteran",
casting his covetous old eyes on the information minister's job,
perhaps as a parting gift for so many years of journalistic
pretensions.
It is not that he is not free to dream, or to attempt to get there by
hiding under Mr. Amuka Pemu's roof to take pot shots at all manners
of Nigerians who are doing their honest jobs. My objection against my
colleagues' angry conclusion was actually, based on personal
conviction that Sobowale is a "veteran" writer and a respectable
elder, who happens to find himself under the suffocating pressure of
today's print media in Nigeria, which thrives, almost solely, on the
cynical side of life.
I calmed my friends' frayed nerves by drawing their attention to the
implication of the writer's need to survive and remain relevant,
against such a background. It is thus clear that I am, personally,
neither surprised nor bothered by his increasing employment of
invectives and epithets of opprobrium in his column.
Therefore, this rejoinder was not prompted by his vitriolic attack on
the national media tour programme per se, as Professor Jerry Gana
himself has strongly advised us to always concentrate on the noble
objectives of development oriented information dissemination and
management and never to succumb to the distracting temptations of
direct engagement with cynics and rabble rousers in the print media
whose antics are usually tailored to meet their obvious need for
relevance and survival in today's cynical journalism.
Rather, this brief response is aimed at protesting against Sobowale's
uncharitable and vicious assault on the integrity of all journalists
in the national media tour team. His description of those diligent
and senior professional journalists, including Vanguard
representative, as 'praise and worship team" who "see no evil, hear
no evil, and report no evil" was very unfortunate. The insinuation
that those respectable ladies and gentlemen of the Nigerian media
received a brain dulling daily sedation of "pounded yam plus egusi
soup and assorted meat" and so on, was a clear indication that the
writer's effort to outdo his junior colleagues in the competitive
world of cynical journalism has indeed transformed his once vibrant
and frank column into a yellow page of diabolical humour.
Rabble rousers, snipping at the national media tour programme, have
raised a wide variety of false and baseless allegations to which
Sobowale's own "pounded yam and egusi soup" slur was only an
egregious addition. Some of his colleagues in the cynical trade had
built up various imaginary expenditures of unreasonable magnitude as
having been involved, while others insinuated that it was a
politically motivated jamboree.
I must reiterate the fact that the media tour is, and will remain, a
very important national programme, involving senior and
professionally oriented journalists from the nation's leading media
houses, and that it transcends all ethnic, regional, religious, or
partisan considerations.
Far from being a jamboree or a wasteful venture, the programme is
aimed at widening, deepening, strengthening and stabilizing the
nation's nascent democracy, by facilitating the right of the media to
know what the governments, at federal, state, and local council
levels, are doing to better the lot of our people.
It is not true that journalists were sedated with "pounded yam and
egusi soup" by the state governors in order to draw them into a
conspiracy of silence. Indeed, a number of state governors and local
council chairmen who were found to lag behind in one sector of
performance or the other are not likely to forget in a hurry the
razor sharp frankness with which their lapses were openly pointed out
by the media team, which was led by NUJ, the nation's apex body of
journalists.
It is also not true, as recently insinuated by some dodging governors
in a few states, that the programme had lost its focus at a stage.
Indeed, nobody was fooled by their non causa pro causa arguments and
excuses for banning the media team from inspecting their projects,
particularly after the journalists' unfettered tour of all the nooks
and crannies of those states, primarily to inspect federal projects,
but with their eyes trained on everything visible, and with their
ears to the ground, feeling the pulse of the largely hapless people.
Our media team was a microcosm of Nigeria itself, as it included
journalists from different media, different states, and different
socio-cultural, political, and religious backgrounds, making the
governors' whispered talk and fear of a PDP federal government's
organised media tour the ruse of the century.
By facilitating the Nigerian media's collective inspection and open
assessment of the concrete achievements on the ground, at federal,
state, and local council levels, the Federal Government has taken a
giant step towards entrenching transparency and good governance in
the new democratic Nigeria. However, good governance cannot and ought
not be seen as the exclusive business of the Federal Government
alone.
Rather, it demands the active support and participation of every
state government, every citizen, and every public and private
institution, with the media leading the way, by playing its watchdog
role, constructively and positively, without the encumbrance of deep
seated cynicism of the Sobowale type.
Lawal, is the chief press secretary to the Minister of Information
and National Orientation

Original_Search_Title: Tiv to Obasanjo: Sack Danjuma Now
Headline: Nigeria: Tiv to Obasanjo: Sack Danjuma Now
Body_Text:
Abuja and Enugu — Atiku cancels Enugu visit, goes to Benue
The Tiv people have asked President Olusegun Oba-sanjo to immediately rep-lace the Minister of Def-ence, Lt. Gen. Theophilus Dan-juma (rtd) whom they accused of fueling the Tiv/Junkun crisis which led to the killing of more than 100 people in Zaki Biam and three other communities in Benue State.
A group of soldiers last Tuesday took literally an order by President Olusegun Oba-sanjo directing security agents to get those behind the killings of 19 military personnel along Benue/Taraba border as they reportedly raided four villages in Benue with 100 persons feared dead.
But in a petition to the President, Mzough U Tiv, (the umbrella organization of the Tiv people), accused Danjuma of indiscriminate use of Army personnel to annihilate the Tiv people of Taraba State. The petition dated October 21 was signed by Brig-Gen. Atom Kpera and Prof. Iorwuese Hagher.
The 12 page petition which detailed the genesis of the crisis and the role played by Danjuma recalled how the soldiers were effectively used by the Jukun to attack the Tiv all over the conflict areas particularly in Wukari and Ibi local governments in 1992.
It also recalled how the soldiers embarked on disarming the Tiv while the Jukun militants were left with their arms with which they invaded the Tiv villages and killed people.
The petition which informed the president how the Nigerian Army personnel were drafted from 4 Motorised Battalion (the home town of Danjuma) to Kashimbilla area last year to lead attacks on Tiv villages by the Jukun stressed that "for more than three months, the soldiers attacked the Tiv villages with armoured tanks".
It also alleged that the "killings continued unabated and on 13th October 2001, the Jukun attacked Dan-Anacha, the largest yam Market in Nigeria, and the army looked on the other way as the Tiv escaping from burnt houses were hunted and slain by the Jukuns".
According to the petition "no effort was made to assist them escape. And today, Dan-Anacha has been renamed kwararafa town, confirming the spirit behind the ethnic cleansing".
The petition which provided details of how the Jukun have been using the soldiers to kill the Tiv people said, "We have attempted to show how the Nigerian Army has been misused consistently, to kill Tiv people, whenever there is conflict between them and the Jukun" adding that the "Kuteb, a major tribe in Takum local government area, was almost annihilated by the army-backed Jukun militia in 1995 and 1999".
While calling for the immediate removal of Danjuma as the Minister of Defence, they appealed to the government to set up a judicial commission of inquiry to look into the remote and immediate causes of the crises in Taraba and Nasarawa States and also take steps to ensure that the Tiv return to their ancestral lands.
The petition which called on Obasanjo to visit the affected areas to see things for himself appealed for adequate compensation of the displaced Tiv people who lost their properties in both Taraba and Nasarawa States.
Meanwhile, the widely publicised one-day working visit of Vice President Atiku Abubakar to Enugu State yesterday was cancelled one hour to the visit owing to the Tiv/Junkun crisis in Benue State.
Prior to the information of cancellation, thousands of enthusiastic indegenes of Enugu State who were anxious to catch a glimpse of the Vice President were disappointed as they were seen in groups at the Enugu Airport and Government House premises.
Those who had to go to the airport as early as 7.30 a.m to wait for the arrival of Atiku were the former Vice President, Dr. Alex Ekwueme, former Min-ister, Chief Silas Iloh, former Governor of Old Anambra State, Chief C. C. Onoh and the traditional rulers led by the chairman of Enugu State Council of Traditional Rulers, Igwe James Mamah.
The stage for the reception was set as different dancing groups from all nooks and crannies of the state were seen with their dancing attires, while youths and women were lined up in their uniforms, all in a bill to give the august visit a rousing welcome. School children and traders were not left out, as the abandoned their lessons and businesses to come and welcome the number two citizen of the country.
In a brief statement signed by the spokesman of the governor, Igbonekwu Ogazimorah, it said that "following some recent development in Nigeria, particularly Benue State, His Excellency, Atiku Abubakar will no longer be able to commission the Enugu State government projects that was slated for yesterday. He is now scheduled to visit Benue State."
The statement further said that "His Excellency's advance team already in Enugu since Sunday left yesterday morning for Benue State where the Vice President is now scheduled to join in the pe
The Vice President who returned from Italy last night was billed to inspect and commission some roads projects in Trans Ekala roads, foundation stone of Nya ba Bridge, law school link road, Nara-Ugbawka road, Nara Electricity and Ozalla Electricity projects in Enugu urban and East Senatorial zone.
In a related development the Federal Government yesterday assured foreign investors that the pocket of crises should not constitute threat to their operations in the country.
The government position was conveyed in Abuja through the Minister of State for Foreign Affairs, Chief Dubem Onyia when he declared open the meeting of Nigeria-Romania Joint Commission,
Onyia said that communal clashes were not peculiar to Ni-geria, stressing that other countries were equally going thro-ugh such minor internal uphe-avals which have not discouraged foreign investors..
In his remark General Dece-bal IIina, the Secretary of State of the Ministry of Industry and Resources of Romania and le-ader of the delegation to the me-eting, expressed optimism that the revived Nigeria-Romania joint commission would go a long way in boosting trade and attracting investors to the countries.

Original_Search_Title: Army Chief Absolves Soldiers of Blame...
Headline: Nigeria: Army Chief Absolves Soldiers of Blame...
Body_Text:
How Tiv Villages Were Sacked...Eyewitness Report
Zaki Biam, a border town in Ukum Local Government of Benue State, is the food basket of the state, as it has the largest yam market in the country. Traders usually come from far and near to buy yam which they carry to as far flung areas as the Southeast and Lagos.
Last Tuesday, the once lively town, which attracts people from across the country, lost its innocence. Soldiers, apparently on a revenge mission, over the abduction and killing of 19 of their colleagues, swooped on the inhabitants, leaving on their trail death, anguish and desolation.
Indeed, by the time the soldiers left, they left behind a city in ruin. There is no single building standing in Zaki-Biam. Those that could not be outrightly destroyed, were razed down. This was the spectacle that greeted us yesterday as we toured the affected areas. But the Chief of Army Staff (COAS), Lt-Gen. Alexander Ogomudia, said in Abuja that soldiers might have acted in self-defence on Tuesday.
As at yesterday afternoon, smoke of fire billowed from most of the buildings, an indication of the freshness of the "madness" and "mayhem" visited on the communities.
To those familiar with the city, Zaki-Biam lost its innocence in the early hours of Wednesday. Eyewitnesses and inhabitants say that about seven truck loads of soldiers from neighbouring Taraba State came into the city waving the olive branch. They reportedly told the people that there was no cause for alarm as they had come to keep the peace.
Unsuspectingly, the residents welcomed their strange visitors, unmindful of the fate that awaited them. In no time, as the story goes, the soldiers opened fire. The residents took to their heels. They fled into the bush. Those who were unlucky were felled by bullets. At the last count, eyewitnesses said that about 100 people had been killed before the invading solders retreated. When THISDAY visited Zaki-Biam alongside reporters from CNN, Reuters, BBC and newsmen from the local media, about five corpses were yet to be cleared and buried.
