The stench oozing from the bodies, did not prevent passers-by from casting glances at them as they laid motionless, breathless.
In terms of physical destruction, Zaki-Biam was unparalleled. No single building is standing in the area intact owing to the destruction. Many of them were burnt down. The city was deserted and desolate, except for individuals who occasionally came out of their hiding in the bush.
The destruction of properties and killings were not restricted to Zaki-Biam. Elsewhere in Gbeju and Anyin border villages in the area, the destruction was mind-boggling. Residents say that the invading soldiers killed between 200 to 300 persons.
As in Zaki-Biam the soldiers were said to have stormed the villages in five armoured personnel carriers.
On arrival at the villages, they allegedly told the residents to gather at the village square for a meeting. As they gathered, the spokesman of the soldiers asked the men to separate from the women. They opened fire on the men killing as many as their fire power could sustain. Those left behind, mainly women and children, have fled the villages and taken refuge in the bush and in other far-flung areas.
However, the most pathetic is the destruction of the country home of Lt.-General Victor Malu, former Chief of Army Chief and ECOMOG Field Commander.
The former Army chief's country home in Katsina Ala was utterly destroyed. The only standing building is bullet-ridden while the one-story building was burnt completely.
A Peugeot 504 saloon car with Registration number AA 479 KAL parked in the garage was torched. Ortega Bollogo who narrated the invasion said that the soldiers who carried out the action, came with hand grenade with which they burnt the house.
He added that the men said they had orders from the Federal Government to destroy the building. Malu's houses were levelled to ground zero.
Eighty-year old blind Pa. Pev Adoor, the family head of the Malu's and an uncle of the former Army Chief was burnt while in the comfort of his house. His wife, who was with him when the soldiers came, was burnt alongside him. The most visible thing in the large compound are the graves of the parents of the former Army Chief. The once imposing structure is now in ruins.
Chief of Army Staff, General Ogomudia, while fielding questions from reporters at a world press conference yesterday in Abuja said the soldiers must have acted in self-defence.
Asked if it was true that soldiers opened fire on some family members of the erstwhile Chief of Army Staff (COAS), General Victor Malu and the destruction of their home, Ogomudia said the matter was under investigation and he could not yet confirm the authenticity of the report.
However, the COAS said that if media reports on the killings were true, then the soldiers may have been acting in self-defence particularly in the circumstance that inmates of Malu's house opened fire on the soldiers.
The COAS denied that the soldiers were on revenge mission. His words: "I wish to categorically state that NA soldiers will for no reason engage in any vengeance mission during internal security operations as this is against out Rules of Engagement and Code of Conduct which are very clear.
'For instance, it is quite clear that in this type of operations, soldiers are expected to use minimum force to achieve their objectives, and that use of force are only permitted in the following situation: (a) in self defence, (b) when murder is being committed, (c) when arson is being committed and (d) when resisting arrest."
While justifying the movement of soldiers to the Benue communities, Ogomudia said the troops would remain in the areas until the militia group disarmed and turned a new leaf, insisting that they are there "to arrest the criminals who killed the soldiers, recover the remaining bodies, cordon and search any suspicious area for arms".
Furthermore, Ogomudia said the troops have been charged with the responsibility of removing "...road blocks belonging to the ethnic militias in order to encourage free flow of traffic and restore normalcy to the area".
"...I wish to state that the on going operations is in Ukum LGA only and that as long as the militias carry weapons and have refused the call to surrender these weapons and the criminals, the area is still volatile.
"The idea therefore that the Nigerian Army has invaded Tiv land is mischievous as one local government does constitute Tivland."
He said the troops were deployed to the area following the expiration of the Federal Government ultimatum on the Benue state government directing it to produce those who murdered the soldiers.
"It will be recalled too that following this cold blooded murder of these troops, the Federal Government had directed that the culprits be produced by the Government of Benue State within a specified period."
This, according to him, was not met.
President Olusegun Obasanjo has, however, ordered the cesssatin of military actions.
Obasanjo gave the order after a 45-minute telephone discussion yesterday with the Governor of Benue State, Mr. George Akume.
Akume made the disclosure yesterday while briefing representatives of world media who were in the state to have first hand account of the extent of the military raids.
He explained that Obasanjo denied ordering any action on any part of the state, adding that the destruction did not have his authorisation.
Akume said he explained the extent of damage to property and the number of death occasioned by the incident which might have informed the President's decision.
He expressed regrets about the wanton loss of lives, adding that in Gbeji alone, about 130 people were killed.
"We suffered casualties, and material damages. I took these up with the President. I told him that Zakibiam, Gbeji and seven settlements have been destroyed in the process", he said, adding "I cannot be definite on the number of casualties."
Reacting, London-based Amnesty International described the killings of villagers by soldiers earlier in the week as "an act of revenge" and "a killing spree."
Amnesty urged "a prompt, efective and impartial inquiry" and noted that the army had denied any killings had taken place.
A Nigerian human rights activist, Felix Ofou, called on the government to say who had deployed troops in the area where the Tiv and Jukun ethnic groups have skirmished for years, largely over land: "on no account should soldiers overrun civilians in the guise of peacekeeping," Ofou told Reuters.
The Constitutional Rights Project (CRP) called for judicial inquiry and described the action of the soldiers as akin to the Odi incident "which remains fresh in the minds of Nigerians."
"Constitutional Rights Project would like to emphasise that the action of the soldiers is not an act of justice but of vengeance. The soldiers took the laws into their own hands, thus subverting the cause of justice," the CRP noted.
Research and Publications Officer of the CRP, Modupe Omipintemi, in a statement further called on the military authorities to conduct what it called an immediate and full-fledged investigation into the matter.
"The Federal Government is also called upon to set up a judicial commission of inquiry and the findings of the commission published."
Meanwhile, normalcy is gradually returning to Makurdi after violent demonstration on Wednesday by
some irate youths which led to the killing of some persons and destruction of property valued at millions of naira.
Problem started when the students of the Benue State University, Makurdi trooped out of their campus to protest the alleged killing of many people and destruction of property by soldiers deployed to keep peace on the war-torn Benue-Taraba border.
The students also protested the destruction of the family house of the former Chief of Army Staff, retired Lt-Gen.
Victor Malu, in his village Adoor, in Katsina-Ala local government area during which three members of Malu family were said to have been killed.
The protesting students had blocked all the major roads in Makurdi and burnt tyres as they attempted to march to government house.
They were joined by the other youths, mainly hoodlums, while innocent citizens ran helter-skelter for their lives.
Many vehicles and motorcycles were either burnt or smashed, while shops were looted. Burnt bodies of unidentified persons were seen in parts of Wurukum, and along Gboko road. Schools were forced to close as parents withdrew their children and wards.
A newly-constructed police station at the Wurukum area of Makurdi was also burnt, while attempts to burn the mosque in the area were foiled by hundreds of anti-riot policemen who were confronted by the missile-throwing irate youths.
The protesters also condemned the recent donation of N5 million to the muslim community in Gboko by governor George Akume as compensation to enable them rebuild a mosque that was destroyed by youths in Gboko last week.
The Benue police commissioner, Mr. Cyprain Onephrojire, personally went round the state capital to ensure that peace was restored.
Benue police public relations officer, Mr. Emmanue Deebom, told journalists that 10 persons had been arrested in connection with the violence.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Urged to Uphold Oputa Panel Recommendations
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Urged to Uphold Oputa Panel Recommendations
Body_Text:
The Chairman of Ohanaeze Ndi Igbo, an Igbo pan-cultural group, Justice Eze Ozobu advised President Olusegun Obasanjo, to act on the recommendations of the Human Rights Violations Investigation Commi-ssion (HRVIC) (Oputa panel).
Ozobu said the commission's recommendations were not the types to be swept under the official carpet.
He explained that everything was in the hand of Obasanjo, adding that Ohanaeze had made a recommendation, reiterating that it was left for the Federal Government to accept it.
Speaking at the weekend in Lagos at the celebration of new yam festival by the Eze Ndi Igbo of Lagos, Eze H. N Ohazulike at the Ohanaeze secretariat, Agege, Lagos.
On the inter-ethnic crisis in Kano, Ozobu explained that Ohanaeze advised the Igbo's in Kano to compile the names of the victims, stressing that he would not comment on the matter until the list received. He stressed that Igbo were the most liberal people adding that he had never had been mass killing of either Hausa or the Yoruba in Igboland.
Ozobu explained that the Igbo would always resist any form of violation of their rights by any group. He said, Ohanaeze had been calming down Igbo in the North otherwise the boys would have reacted to the unjust killing of their kinsmen in the Northern states.
On the electoral bill, he told THISDAY that he would not comment until he got the a copy of the bill.

Original_Search_Title: Igbo Day As Cultural Outing
Headline: Nigeria: Igbo Day As Cultural Outing
Body_Text:
Lagos — On September 29, 2001, Ndigbo Marked Their Day With Funfair. There Were
Gatherings of the Igbos in Parts of the Country Including Lagos
State. Fidelis Njoku Who Witnessed One of Such Gatherings Sponsored By the
Pan Ndigbo Foundation, Kosofe Chapter in Mende, Maryland, Lagos Relives the
Artistic And Cultural Relevance of the Igbo Day
Igbo Day celebrated recently worldwide means different things to
different individuals and groups. For politicians, Igbo Day is more of a day
of political rallies for the Igbo than anything else. For social elements,
it is a day that provides an opportunity for the highest level of
interaction and socialisation among the Igbo and their admirers. For the
economists, it is a great opportunity for fund raising to prosecute the Igbo
cause. To this reporter, the cultural value of Igbo Day is of primary focus
and concern.
This year, the Igbo Day was marked worldwide in different locations in
the country and in the diaspora. Among the places the day was observed in
parts of the country was Mende, Maryland, Lagos State.
The Mende celebration was organised and funded by an Igbo socio-cultural
cum political organisation called Pan Ndigbo Foundation (NPF), Kosofe Local
Government Council Chapter. The venue was Mende Hall playground and the date
was September 29, 2001.
The acceptable attire for the celebration was the Igbo traditional
native dress comprising a long robe of Isi-agu dress upon a neatly tailored
pair of trousers that fits the colour of the robe with red cap to match. The
title holders who feature in such occasions are expected to appear in their
regalia made up of expensive traditional dress with a red cap decorated with
white feathers of eagles, as well as beads. In those days, the number of
feathers put on a man's cap depicted the number of titles he held, but it
doesn't necessarily follow these days.
Presently, it is a question of choice. Some title holders, especially
the highly educated ones don't put any feather at all on their caps even
though such titled elites have in their kitty several titles from different
traditional rulers across the country which they got while in public
service. But the basic thing is that every titled Igbo man or woman making a
public appearance on occasions as Igbo Day is expected to wear this cultural
and traditional Igbo attire otherwise the defaulter may not be given full
regards and honours at such occasions. This aspect of the Igbo culture was
fairly observed at the Mende Igbo Day. It is on record that the Igbo have
high regard for kolanuts. There are unavoidable rituals ranging from the
order of presentation of the kolanuts among communities or states of Igbo
extraction present at a given ceremony or meeting, the blessing of the
kolanuts, the sharing and distributions of them. These are rites that must
be strictly observed. Anyone who breaches or makes a mistake about them is
usually treated with contempt and regarded as a one who is not a true Igbo
son. Such an error is a very big offence punishable by monetary fine of the
person, whether titled or not. Of course, it is an open secret that women,
no matter their social standings in the land do not partake in the
traditional kola presentation and sharing process in any gathering involving
men in Igbo land. This vital and die-hard aspect of the Igbo culture was
equally, neatly observed at the Igbo Day held in Mende, Maryland.
Then, the main thrust of the occasion was the New Yam Festival, the most
common and respected culture in Igboland right from the days of yore till
date. Pan Ndigbo Foundation, Kosofe Chapter, the organisers of the
celebration made it clear that they wished to use the occasion to mark the
New Yam Festival. In other words, the Igbo community in Kosofe Local
Government Area used the Day to observe the all important New Yam Festival
culture.
As Igbo tradition and customs demand, the organisers roasted some tubers
of yam at the venue of the occasion. The roasted yams were distributed among
the guests along with fresh palm oil mixed with fresh 'ground' pepper. Such
yams cannot be cooked but only roasted and eaten with only palm oil. This
was appropriately done at the Mende Igbo Day as a major item in the
programme of the celebration.
Therefore, pounded yam with Egusi soup only as required by Igbo custom
was also served to the guests, especially, the high table guests. Other
popular Igbo dishes like Ugba (African salad) were handy and were
served. Not left out was palmwine, the most popular drink in Igboland.
Although the Igbo Day was celebrated at Mende and other parts of the
state which are thousands of kilometres away from Igboland, these
traditional dishes and delicacies the Igbo are identified with were prepared
and served at the occasion irrespective of their high cost implications here
in Lagos State, just to ensure that the cultural and traditional relevance
of the celebration was not sacrificed on the altar of observing the Igbo Day
outside Igboland.
In a recent exclusive chat with Eze-Igbo, Alimoso, Eze (Dr.) Emmanuel
Eze Okolie (Abiama) this writer gathered that in the years past the New Yam
Festival was associated with a traditional belief that barren women and
newly married women who tasted the pieces of the yam roasted in public arena
to mark the festival would bear children of their choice. On account of this
belief system associated with the new yam festival, women who desired the
fruits of the womb used to look up to the Yam Festival Day as an opportunity
to receive the blessing of children from the god of yam which was believed
to be the god of procreation. As the Eze-Igbo, Alimoso, Eze Okolie rightly
stated, this aspect of the festival tends to be affected by western and
religious influences. Even where this belief is still upheld, perhaps among
the die-hard traditionalists in Igboland it can hardly be as effective and
result-yielding as in the time of Igbo ancestors.  Definitely modernism with
its attendant civilization has touched all traditional belief systems across
races and nations including the Igbo race.
There is also this unique aspect of the Igbo Day, the display of Igbo
cultural arts such as traditional Igbo music and acrobatic dances.  For
instance, non-Igbo music whether modern or traditional are usually not
tolerated during Igbo Day celebrations. This aspect of the Igbo custom and
tradition was maintained to the letter at the recent Igbo Day celebrated at
Mende, Maryland.
Where such an event takes place in Igboland, all manner of traditional
dances as well as masquerades, women dances, "abigho' dance, "ekpe" dance,
"abigolo" dance and the popular Ohafia war dance and a host of others do
feature. But due to distance, not all these traditional dances associated
with the New Yam Festival and Igbo Day could feature at the Igbo Day marked
at Mende, Maryland. However, there was a peculiar Igbo traditional dance
that gave warmth and traditional touch to the Mende, Igbo Day.
Everything about the dance was traditional. The attire of the dancers,
the dance instruments and the music itself were tailored to showcase a
typical Igbo traditional dance. Moreso, the acrobatic nature of the dance
coupled with the artistry displayed by the dancers and the drummers held the
guests spellbound. This traditional dance called "igba-ndi-eze" usually has
girls as dancers and young men as drummers. They usually appear in dazzling
uniform and dance attires of choice. Besides, the dancers at times go extra
miles to decorate their bodies with whitish, reddish and other suitable
colours to make their appearance captivating and exciting. The dancers that
featured at the Mende Igbo Day held recently brought to the fore all these
vital cultural angles to the Igbo Day to the admiration of the guests which
cut across ethnic divides, among whom was the Baale of Mende.

Original_Search_Title: FJC Accuses Opposition of Sinister Plans
Headline: Gambia: FJC Accuses Opposition of Sinister Plans
Body_Text:
The director of press and public relations at State House, Mrs.
Fatoumata Jahumpa Ceesay has divulged plans,reportedly
concocted by the opposition in the run up to the October Presidential
election.
Speaking recently at a rally organised by the "Gikko Nyima" group on
Tobaco Road Banjul, Mrs. Jahumpha said the opposition had already
"succumbed," accusing them of using unscrupulous means to "woo APRC
stalwarts". "Even me, they (the opposition) have assigned the
Independent newspaper to ask me if President Jammeh had sacked me, so
that I would be angry and call it a quit with the APRC and join
them," she revealed.
She accused the opposition of instigating or
influencing some youths to hang around the British Embassy in
the quest for visas. She charged that this was in most cases motivated
by the opposition's desire to paint a negative image of the country by
creating the impression that a state of insecurity or panic loomed
over the country ahead of the election. "We the greater percentage of
the Gambian population (the women), are calling on the British
Embassy at Fajara not to accept such a situation.
However, such moves by the opposition would be contrary to the youth
empowerment policy of the APRC Government. More so, President Jammeh
is also currently sponsoring over 800 students, regardless of
political affiliation".
The SoS for Justice, Hon. Joseph Joof, urged APRC militants to give a
deaf ear to what he called "detractors" who were bent on instigating
violence.
Hon. Joof also challenged Omar Amadou Jallow (OJ), whom he referred to
as "East Timor," to be mute and respect constitutional provisions.
"The repeal of Decree 89 is not the problem. 'East Timor' has to
satisfy sections 52, 9 and 90 in order to be eligible," observed SoS
Joof. He also added, "the constitution states that for eligibility,
you must be in the country for at least 5 years," which OJ does not
satisfy. "Who asked him to go to East Timor?," he questioned.
"Among those that barred OJ from contesting are the findings of the
commission of inquiry, ranging from abuse of office, failure to
declare his total earnings and property and several others," said
Joof.
He also observed that the projects and aspirations of President
Jammeh, were magnanimous enough to speak for themselves as within a
span of seven years, Jammeh had accomplished projects neglected for
the past thirty years. "Vote Jammeh and the APRC and you will see
progress and development. The whole country will be electrified,
roads will be constructed and many other projects embarked upon," SoS
Joof promised. On behalf of the president, SoS Joof expressed
gratitude for the support and loyalty accorded to him by Gambians,
especially the women.
Other speakers at the rally included Aji Fatou Sallah, Mayor Samba
Faal, NAM, Saihou Omar and NAM Suku Singhateh, the adopted 'father'
of "Gikko Nyima", who all hailed Jammeh's government. "If you want to
experience another half decade of absolute progress and development,
Jammeh is the answer," they concurred.
It was also attended by Nyimasata Sanneh-Bojang, deputy mayor Biri
Njie, NAM, David Jones, Aji Yam Jobe-Jeng, the adopted 'mother' of
"Gikko Nyima' and other dignitaries.

Original_Search_Title: End Ethnic Clashes, Museveni Tells Nigerians
Headline: Nigeria: End Ethnic Clashes, Museveni Tells Nigerians
Body_Text:
Kampala — President Yoweri Museveni urged an end to religious and ethnic strife
in Nigeria newspapers reported on Tuesday.
Speaking on Monday at the Nigerian War College, Museveni called for
peace between Nigeria's religious and ethnic communities and a
market-led approach to promote African development.
The remarks come a week after clashes left more than 500 dead.
"Our need is unity, not to quarrel as to whether somebody prefers
pork or yam, whether one prays five times a day or once a day or one
does not pray at all," The Guardian reported.
"In Uganda, we never allow religion to interfere with state matters
and religions are free to evangelise," he said.
Africa's need was economic development and that meant promoting peace
and a market-led approach to the economy, he told an audience
including the Nigerian vice president.

Original_Search_Title: The Vacation Address Everybody Wants
Headline: Ghana: The Vacation Address Everybody Wants
Body_Text:
A dip in the pool at mid-night, a walk on its grounds at 5 in the morning listening to the chirping of tropical birds, and a canoe expedition to the Abokwa (Just Stones) Island, PaJohn Dadson took off away from Accra to Busua and had a 'fab' time At the Busua Beach Resort.
We had just traversed the highest waves. The sea looked calm, but on it, we could feel some heavy undercurrents at work. We were now way past the shore, and on our way when suddenly the canoe in which we were riding tipped to the right a second time. This time it swayed much further to quite an incredible angle, and I was sure of an overturn. Behind me, I overheard the minder of the outboard motor and the 'Captain' who was navigating the large pedal at the rear issuing alarming instructions to each other in Ahanta. Being my mother tongue, I understood somewhat, what they were saying, but the sheer exclamation with which they spoke suggested imminent danger. For a moment, the hairs on my body straightened in sheer fright, as I held on to the slab on which I was sitting more tightly! Awuku, our tour guide, beside me, gave me a sheepish smile. The canoe swayed further, still moving us at the point of a sure tip. Shutting my eyes, my whole life, playing in fast-forward, run across my eyes. I began to pray. "Dear God, thank you for all the opportunities and the challenges you passed my way. I am ready now, Father God... come into my..." Then, as suddenly as it tipped, the canoe swayed back, and leveled out again. We were now close to the Abokwa Island, our destination. A small piece of land some 10km off the coast of Busua, a sleepy resort town in the Ahanta district in the Western Region of Ghana. We had arrived here the day before after a rather long and winding journey from Accra by a hire tour coach. I was with a group of young American students taking a semester at the University of Ghana at Legon in Accra. About 5km before you arrive at Busua, a publicity sign for the 3-star hotel here, the Busaa Beach Resort, reads "Ghana's Vacation Address", and further states that, 'You Won't Come this Far For Nothing'! The town itself, as I said, is a sleepy habitat with some semblance of activity at the main junction where you turn off to the hotel. On the right just as you turn is a budget accommodation establishment called The Dadsons Lodge. A Methodist church building, put up in the 1960's by one of the town's more successful offspring, Mr. E. K. Dadson, who was a government Chief Whip in Ghana's first Republic, (...and who happens to be this writer's father) was about 50m down the road to the left in front of a small mausoleum where the remains of this illustrious kinsman is buried. Almost opposite is the Alaska Beach Club; another budget hotel with chalets and a rustic ambience. Lodged by the bamboo walls before this hotel is a small shed with the inscription, Daniel, the Pancake man. Daniel, I read on. makes world-class pancakes! Then before us appeared the unassuming entrance to the Hotel. I had been to Busua several times before, and indeed stayed at this resort many times, but nothing you ever remember about it, nor any updates you read about it prepares you for the tranquillity that hangs in the air at the Busua Beach Resort. Forget the quiet, crowd-less ambience; the cosiness of this fabulous place begins right at the entrance, which leads to a car park ahead, and the main reception entrance to the left. Having already booked rooms, we quickly check-in, filling the necessary forms and making our way to our rooms. Originally built and managed by Mr Dadson as the Busua Pleasure Beach, there are 52 rooms in something like twenty-six chalets in this hotel, including two suites, with a few more 'budget' rooms in the rear. UN General Secretary Kofi Annan and his wife, stayed in one of the suites on a visit here sometime in the 1990's. Now owned by the Golden Beach Hotels Group, it's been upgraded to a rather posh and dainty palace with a myriad of sports and conference facilities, and my favourite place in the 'house' - a bird sanctuary! There's nothing like walking the grounds here between five and six in the morning, amid mist and the piercing glorious chirping of tropical birds! We were due for dinner in a couple of hours, at 8, so with the air-conditioner humming away, I took a long warm shower - there are only showers in the rooms -  and joined the others on the beach for a barbecue layout. Barefooted, the beach sand caressed my feet as I kept digging into the sand while I devoured every piece of grilled chicken, chunky cut fresh vegetables and yam croquette on my plate, having had a scanty lunch of a steak and kidney pie! The company was good, and we even drank some wine, as we shared experiences about the various communities from where we came. Let loose, students will always be students-on-the-loose! What I hadn't noticed before about them, I saw now, as lit sticks of cigarette kiss many a lips within the company. The evening careered on, and we eventually broke into groups, one taking dips in the rather petit swimming pool, and the other preferring to stay indoors, and experiment with cocktails at the bar inside at the main lobby area.
The following morning, we had breakfast - which is part of the room charge - individually. Through the lifeguard department of the hotel, I arranged for a canoe to take a group of us on an expedition to the Abokwa Island.
From the shore, The Abokwa Island has the shape of a rested lion, with two palm trees in front of it. Its presence gives the town a rather romantic feel and adds to the general ambience of the location. With a low lying shoreline, ample sandy beach, and waves that sometimes rise over 15 feet, Busua is truly a vacation haven, for as the people living in the town have demonstrated, it's an overwhelming casual existence that surrounds you when you live here. All counted, we were 12 who were to make the trip and we fit on one canoe. Our life jackets strapped on, we got on board the vessel, and with anxious trepidation, we set out. Majority of the students being ladies, we the men, Walter, and myself as coordinators, Akoto and Awuku, as guides, felt responsible for the group. This feeling, I was sure we would gladly renounce at that moment when the canoe was playing up! Akoto. sitting in front of me, would not look back when I called him. He had instantly developed a stiff neck! Even Walter, a motorcycle enthusiast, had a perturbed look on his face as he turned to look at me. As for Awuku, I saw his heart beating inside his agape mouth! Of course, I was calm, as God is my witness!
The irony, however, is how very unconcerned in manner the girls sat, casually chatting among themselves and enjoying every bit of this adventure, oblivious of the imminent danger.
We spent only a few minutes on the island. Somehow, everyone found a spot on the rocks, and just sat and observed the vastness of the sea, or was it the glory of God? It was exhilarating. The journey back was not as frightful, getting back to a 'heroes' welcome from the others.
After a few dips in the sea, surfing along the waves, I joined the rest of the group as we checked out of the hotel. I felt completely relaxed after only one night here. It suddenly dawned on me that it was Monday the day after! Traffic fumes, rising dust, sound of hooting motorcar horns, the constant ringing of my mobile phone, and bad English grammar on radio, all staring me in the face. Why couldn't I just stay here?

Original_Search_Title: Not Guilty
Headline: Nigeria: Not Guilty
Body_Text:
Ex-council boss caught in corruption and misapplication of public fund controversy fights to clear his name but the odds are stacked against him
Udo Edukere, deposed chairman of Esit Eket lo-cal government, Akwa Ibom State, who was accused of corruption and misappropriation of N15 million, is now fighting on many fronts to regain the post. Edukere has taken his case to the law court to clear his name and petitioned the anti-corruption panel, President Olusegun Obasanjo and Victor Attah, governor of Akwa Ibom State, to intervene in the matter.
A high court at Eket is already looking at Edukere's complaints.
Edukere was in combat mood when Newswatch visited him at Eket July 23. "If I am found guilty at the end of the day, so be it. I am ready to go to jail. I am doing these because of my good name and because I don't want to leave a bad name for my children," he said. That looks like a long shot. But for now, Edukere has to overcome some forces against him.
"The auditor-general has recommended that he should refund the sum of N15 million and we are moving in very soon to recover the council's money he misappropriated," Nkereuwem Akwaowo, the new chairman who succeeded Edukere in office, told Newswatch. He disclosed that the state government has set a machinery in motion to recover the N15 million.
That is not the only problem facing the former chairman. The councillors in Esit Eket local government are all against him. In fact, they were the architects of his fall. On March 26, seven of the councillors tabled a catalogue of allegations titled: Notice of guilt of gross misconduct, which they used to remove him. The councillors accused Edukere of lack of probity and accountability; gross and wanton breaches of financial regulations and procedures; misappropriation of council funds through spurious contracts and execution of non-existing projects as well as violation of the local government (administration) law 2000, among others.
Specifically, the councillors charged that the budget for the year 2000 for rural electrification was N11,500,000 but Edukere allegedly increased it to N19,267,142 without approval.
The councillors also alleged that under the cost/other charges sub-head, they budgeted N29,750,000.00, but Edukere unilaterally increased it to N49,846,467.77. He was equally accused of inflating the personnel cost, salary and allowances to N10 million.
They said the former chairman inflated the contract for the construction of the council hall by more than N5 million without approval. In the same charge, they said the council voted N1.5 million for the purchase of a pick-up van, but Edukere changed it to N2.6 million and the N5.85 million he spent on the purchase of other vehicles was not authorised.
They said though the council had no budget for consultancy services, the former chairman still went ahead to spend N1.3 million for that. The councillors also charged that Edukere was not rendering monthly statement of account as required by law. They also accused him of violating the local government law.
The councillors also accused Edukere of spending N2,598,000.00 of the council's money on the burial of his father without approval. They claimed that Edukere unlawfully enriched himself through inflation of contracts and inflation of budget votes and proceeds of which he used to complete his personal house at Eket, which was abandoned for several years. They also said he was now constructing a storey building at SDP Road, Eket.
Taking all the allegations together, the councillors said it was obvious that Edukere was not competent to remain in his post as council chairman and therefore, ordered his suspension. The matter was then sent to a panel of inquiry set up by the state chief judge, which also found him guilty of gross misconduct.
Edukere denied all the allegations. He told Newswatch he was not corrupt and that he did not embezzle government money to warrant any refund. He charged that contrary to allegations, he had run an open government "that is accountable to God and to the people of Esit Eket directly through the elected councillors." He said it was untrue that he was not calling regular general council meetings and showed Newswatch reporter some of the minutes of the council meetings.
He challenged the head of personnel to produce all general council minutes from June 1999 to 2001 whether those decisions were not reflected there. He told Newswatch that he normally circulated statement of income and expenditure to council members as approved by general council meetings. He claimed that he had never done any project without the permission of the council or increased contract sums without approvals. He also showed Newswatch some of the monthly returns he sent to the council for vetting and debate. Edukere insisted that he had never inflated any contract or used his position to embezzle public funds.
Edukere said the allegation that he bought vehicles without due authorisation was false. He said soon after inauguration, it was discovered that the council lacked functional vehicles and the general meeting of June 27, 2000, resolved to buy the vehicles.
On the electrification contracts he said that the finance and general purpose committee meeting, September 28, 2000, approved them and the tender for the award was published in the Pioneer newspaper of August 14, 2000. He quoted variously from the minutes and resolutions of the council to buttress his point.
On the personnel cost, he said the head of personnel and the treasurer who handle such matters are the people in a position to explain the reasons for the N10 million increase in personnel cost.
Edukere said contrary to the allegation that he unilaterally inflated the contract for the construction of the council hall by more than N5 million, he made reference to the finance and general purpose meeting of May 26, 2000, which awarded the contract to Qua Ventures Nigeria Limited for N15.9 million.
He said the contract was reviewed upward when the contractor asked for variation. He said he did not approve the variation, even when Theophilus Umoyo, leader of the council, led a delegation to his office pleading for the variation. "I insisted that all the 10 councillors must sign to authorise me to refer the variation to the consultants," said Edukere. He showed Newswatch the document signed by the councillors to authorise the variation as well as the recommendation of the consultants, who approved tentative payment of N4 million to the contractor. But Edukere approved N3 million.
Edukere similarly denied allegation of corrupt enrichment. He said that the personal building referred to by the councillors took him seven years to build. He claimed to have built it with his personal savings, loan and cash gift from friends. The other plot of land that he has, he said, was bought from the state government in 1994 at the cost of N20,000.00. And that the storey building on the land, still under construction, was actually a block of stores. "I am constructing this building with my legitimate earnings and a loan from Citizens International Bank, Eket, and with a money gift from my brother-in-law residing in the United States of America," Edukere said.
On the money spent on the burial of his father, Edukere said with regrets: "I wish I did not accept the unsolicited Greek gift of Esit Eket local government." He argued that though it was a standard practice at any government level to assist bereaved government officials, he did not apply for any assistance. For instance, he alleged that the council gave Bassey Essien, speaker of Akwa Ibom State house of assembly, financial assistance of N1,313,500.00 during the burial of his mother.
Similarly, he said that the council gave same assistance to the tune of N1,377,500.00 to Nkereuwem Akwaowo towards the burial of his father. He argued: "All I did was to give effect to council's resolution. Indeed, I did so reluctantly after pressure from the councillors. Other than the cash gift to me, I did not, repeat did not, handle the funds or control how they were spent. The vice-chairman was fully in charge." Besides, he told Newswatch that normally, because his late father was a certificated village chief, it was incumbent on the council to give him a cow towards his burial.
Edukere said it was Akwaowo, the vice-chairman, who chaired the council meeting, which deliberated and resolved to give him financial support towards the burial of his father. "I never asked them to give me money to bury my father," he said. He told Newswatch that he received N500,000.00 cash.
He said that notwithstanding, he was not part of decision to spend N2.5 million on the burial. Edukere said it was Akwaowo, the vice-chairman, who chaired the council meeting, which decided that.
That in itself, he said, was not unusual. He told Newswatch that Essien and Akwaowo were equally given cash. He said both the speaker and former vice-chairman were given N250,000.00 each and that the balance was used to purchase such items like cows, goats, yams and other items towards the burial as well as for repairing the roads leading to their various villages.
Edukere told Newswatch that the council decided to repair the road leading to Essien's village because "if we waited on the state, they might not take cognisance of the need to repair the road. And since this is my local government area, the council met and said, what assistance can we give to him so that he may not be humiliated. Supposing people came and there was no road to his house, the first person they would accuse would be the local government, ok. They would also talk about the state and the state would turn around and blame the local government. So, we moved in .  We went and we graded the road. We brought sand to fill the road to make the road passable, because it was during the raining season. And then we gave cash. All we spent was N1.3 million. We gave him N250,000.00; we also gave him a cow, a goat and some yams, to enable him entertain the avalanche of visitors that came into the local government."
Edukere said he knew that trouble was afoot when the councillors started making some demands from him. "Severally, the vice-chairman and the councillors had sent proposals asking me to share council funds, which I have stoutly resisted.
"For instance, on September 6, 2000, the vice-chairman sent a proposal to me demanding for N5.5 million to be shared among himself and the councillors but this was turned down," Edukere alleged.
Three months after, he alleged that another proposal came from Umoyo demanding N5.4 million to be shared. The same leader, he said, later demanded N2.0 million as take-off grant, but he approved N500,000.00 to be disbursed to the councillors after much pressure. Edukere told Newswatch that he turned down another request of N2.6 million, which was meant to be Christmas bonus for the legislators.
He believes that he invoked the wrath of Essien, the assembly speaker, because he had campaigned against him in the 1998 election. He equally believes that the speaker has not been feeling comfortable to have an AD chairman in his local council. "The speaker,  in his elevated position, of course there is no way he could have felt good in that he is number three in the state and his local government is under an AD chairman. We are talking about politics if you are a leader in his position, there is no way you can talk authoritatively if your local government is in the back of the party you are representing."
He believes Attah, Essien, Akwaowo and his councillors were part of the conspiracy. First, Edukere noted that only five of the ten voted for his removal at the meeting of March 26. Four of the councillors, he maintained, were not served the notice of the meeting hence three of them did not attend. He said the councillors required two-thirds majority to remove him from office.
He faulted the legality of the meeting, saying the mace, the symbol of authority of the legislature, was still in the custody of the police at their headquarters in Uyo at the time the councillors met. Besides, he argued that the law required that he should be served notice of gross misconduct at least seven days before the meeting and he has the liberty to reply to the allegations within 14 days. He claimed that he only received the notice after he was suspended from office. On the day of his suspension, Edukere alleged that 22 armed policemen were deployed to the council headquarters to stampede people out of the place.
Edukere also flawed the panel's decision in the case. He admitted to Newswatch that he did not appear before the panel on grounds that there was a case in court challenging the legality of his suspension. "It was wrong for the panel to sit when a superior court had ruled that it has jurisdiction to hear the cases brought by councillors against and for me," he said.
Essien told Newswatch in Uyo, July 24, that Edukere was only looking for a scapegoat. He said he was not responsible for his removal or played a part in it. "As the speaker of the state assembly, of what importance or what consequence would the removal or non removal of chairman of the local council be to me?" He asked. Instead, he said he had mediated thrice between him and the councillors to ensure that peace and cordial relationship existed in the council.
He denied ever collecting any money from the council towards the burial of his mother. He also said the council did not repair the road to his village at the time. He told Newswatch that the only repairs done on the road was carried out by a friend of his who was working at Strabag at that time. He said the friend, one Chief Beke, filled the road with stones to make it passable. A Newswatch reporter, who visited Essien's village noticed that the road was stone-filled, untarred and hardly motorable. But the reporter did not meet Beke when he called at his office in Uyo.
He also defended the governor and the chief judge, saying they were only doing their constitutional duties. "It is very wrong to blame their failure or their non performance on innocent bye-standers," Essien said.
Akwaowo, who was Edukere's vice, also told Newswatch that his former boss was a liar. "Edukere is the greatest liar in Esit Eket local government," he said. The new chairman claimed that his predecessor had acted like a sole administrator and that in fact, he told someone that he was a sole administrator of the council. He charged that the former chairman appropriated money and spent money the way he liked.
Akwaowo denied the allegation that he tabled councillors' request for money before Edukere. He also said he did not approve N2,598,000.00 for the burial of his father. He did not say how much was actually spent. Instead, he promised to give Newswatch reporter a document on the issue, but in the end, he said he could not lay his hands on it. As for his father's burial, he claimed that he was given only N250,000.00 by the council.
"He approved N250,000.00 for the burial of my father. The record would show that was what was brought to me. If any money was given to anybody, the council does not know it because he was running the council like his personal estate, without consultation, without discussion with anybody. If he gave out money most of us are not in the know."
S. J. Isonguyo, the clerk of the local government, told Newswatch that he could not speak about the matter, and that no councillor would talk to me on it because they had appointed Theophilus Umoyo, the leader, to speak on their behalf. Udoyiu E. Udoyiu, personal assistant to the leader, said his boss was ill and was out of town.
Edukere was suspended from office March 26, following a council debate on allegation of gross misconduct pronounced him guilty. The matter was then forwarded to a judicial panel set up by the state chief judge, which found him guilty as charged. Sylvanus Albert, a lawyer, who headed the seven-man panel that looked into the case, said the panel was satisfied that the plaintiffs proved all the allegations against the former chairman. But the panel said that the allegation that Edukere corruptly enriched himself was not established. "It is therefore presumed that the plaintiffs had abandoned the allegation," said the panel report.
As soon as the panel submitted its report, the councillors started an impeachment procedure and removed him from office that Thursday, June 5.

Original_Search_Title: FG to Supply Free Nets to Pregnant Women, Children
Headline: Nigeria: FG to Supply Free Nets to Pregnant Women, Children
Body_Text:
Abuja — Determined to stem the rate of materials and child mortality in the
country, the Federal Government is to commence the supply of Insecticide Treated
Nets (ITN) free of charge to pregnant women and under five years-old
children in the country by July 2002.
A statement signed by the Assistant Public Relations Officer of the
National Health Primary Healthcare Development Agency, Sa'adu Salahu
stated that they were acing on President Olusegun Obasanjo's
directive that the Federal Ministry of Health should start free
supply of the materials to both pregnant women and under-five years
old children.
Mr. Salahu stated that the chief consultant in the Federal Ministry
of health, Dr. Dayo Mosanya disclosed this while delivering a lecture
at a three-day Training of Tainers (TOT) workshop for zonal technical
officers of the National Primary Healthcare Development Agency in
Kaduna State, he said.
Dr. Mosanya disclosed that the Federal Government had already removed
tariff on insecticides treated nets and reduced customs duty on
importation of yam from 15 % to 5% in its efforts to ensure the
success of the Rold Bak Malaria Campaign.
She described malaria as the commonest cause of hospital admissions
at the grassroot level and an all year round disease in the country.

Original_Search_Title: KIA Records Growth in Non-Traditional Exports
Headline: Ghana: KIA Records Growth in Non-Traditional Exports
Body_Text:
Government's effort at encouraging export of Non-traditional commodities seems to be yielding positive results as exports through the cargo village at the Kotoka International Airport (KIA) have been recording steady growth since 1995.
Imports, through the same route, however, have slowed down considerably over the past three years.
Pineapples account for about 60 per cent of exports through KIA, while yams, vegetables and handicrafts make up 35 and five percent respectively.
Statistics available to the Ghana News Agency show that 25,301 tonnes of the items were exported in 1998; 29,880 in 1999 and 32,379 tonnes last year.
Commodities exported from January 1998 to July 2001 stood at 64,071 tonnes.
Mr Maxwell Arthur, Director, Corporate Planning, of the Ghana Civil Aviation Authority said there was a drop in exports in the first half of this year, as against the same period last year. This he said was due to a slow down of economic activity in the country and the temporary ban on yam exports.
He also mentioned the rather stiff competition from the export of similar  products on the world market from the Caribbean, saying, "this really hit our drive."
Mr Arthur was quick to add that business is likely to pick up by September.
An official of African Ground Operations (AFGO), said the demand for pineapples is now on a daily basis, unlike previously when such exports were seasonal.
He said the survival of the aviation industry depends largely on the economic well being of the country, saying, " there is a correlation between Gross Domestic Product and exports especially through KIA.
Mr Arthur said spare parts; machinery freighted through KIA has been declining.
In 1999 imports recorded 16,877 tonnes while in 2000 it was 14,447 tonnes.
He said imports are expected to increase by December, when traders and importers bring in goods for the Yuletide.
Estimated revenue accruing from  exports and imports through AFGO in 2000 was 8.3 million dollars, while that of the first half of this year stands at 3.7 million dollars.

Original_Search_Title: 'I Wonder As I Wander' - Sister Hawa's headaches
Headline: Ghana: 'I Wonder As I Wander' - Sister Hawa's headaches
Body_Text:
Hon. Nana Akomea, Deputy Minister for Tourism, says, according to GTV news a few days ago, that tourism must be added to the school curriculum. In the actuality accompanying the story, however, I didn't hear the minister propose one more subject for the BECE or the SSCE.
Well, somebody surely sorely needs some schooling in tourism. But that somebody is certainly not our children, absolutely not our toddlers and infants. It is big people who badly need education in tourism.
Take the Ghana Airways. It has so many stories for getting people stranded that it is not worth getting angry at Mawuko's PR department
anymore. It is very tempting to conclude that somebody is determined to run down the company as a mission. If you have any appointment anywhere in the world, the national airline will help you miss it.
The US is fast becoming our biggest tourist market. A national airline is a logical first attraction for people coming here for the first time.
Getting stranded in Baltimore, meanwhile, seems to be the specialty of the Ghana Airways. It confuses its passengers when it runs on time.
Indeed, if we did not have a man like Mr. Sam Jonah there now, it would be appropriate to advocate that 'bookmen' of the GPRTU might run the company more efficiently.
The hotel industry has been growing since the late 1980s. But there is a lot of very basic things they are yet to learn to improve. Food is a big challenge to most small hotels in the country, in Accra or elsewhere.
You cannot take for granted that there will be breakfast for you in most hotels. That is why in Ghana hotels don't charge rooms with breakfast included.
This gets to be more challenging outside of Accra. But it has also to do with the thinking that hotels must not serve Ghanaian foods. Breakfast, for our hotels, cannot consist of koko and koose, or mpampa, or even tea and maasa. You get into a hotel in some corner of the country. You ask for a menu. You are shown some piece of paper, and the pride of the cook is "fly" rice. And this is in yam country during yam season.
I've been in a hotel in Cape Coast where I couldn't be served kenkey and fish. Our catering schools specialize in "fly" rice.
It's like most other things in our lives. We must run away from ourselves all the time. It is unthinkable to get into a hotel in Nigeria and not get to be served Nigerian foods. No. But the Ghanaian's pride, which we want tourists to pay money to visit, is how far away we want to be from ourselves.
Perhaps tourism ends in Accra. Or should. But if it must go into the country, a lot has to be done that is not the job of the ministry. What can the ministry do about torn bedcovers and sheets, torn discoloured towels, stinking bathrooms with hand folded pieces of toilet paper and half tablets of soap, or dirty mattresses in dusty rooms with cobwebs?
What about carpets and rugs that are not vacuum-cleaned and so leave hotel rooms dank and stuffy?
Tourists coming to Ghana are not likely to be your luxury types who patronize 5-star hotels. Indeed we must encourage more the kind of tourist who would patronize the kinds of hotels we have in Ghana. Even then, compared to what is available to you in places like Kenya, Uganda, Burkina Faso, Senegal, or even South Africa, hotel rates are too high in Ghana.
We don't have the pyramids. And no matter what investments we put out, the Boti falls will never be like the Victoria Falls. Bosumtwi can never draw to itself tourists because of its size. The biggest tourist attraction we can offer is not a natural landscape or a human-made monument. We must not underrate 'Ghanaian hospitality'. That is what we have to sell. It will be more valuable if we package that well by improving the services of our airline and our hotels.
Perhaps Nana Akomea and Hawa could start from a simple, modest budget survey of tourists. That should give you basis for diagnosis and for improving things.

Original_Search_Title: Taking the War to the Enemy
Headline: Kenya: Taking the War to the Enemy
Body_Text:
To Kanu, civic education is a life and death matter Okonkwo knew. He knew that if he wanted to escape the curse of his father - the debts, the poverty, the contempt of his peers - he had to tend his yams and keep his family well-fed and satisfied.
He knew what needed to be done, how it needed to be done, and when. It took hard work and sense to rise above the miasma of his father's failures.
Unlike Okonkwo, the opposition in this country knows what it wants - power - and it may have a vague notion of what needs to be done, but will not lift a finger to get it done. You have to hand it to Kanu, that party has not kept an iron grip on power by sitting on its backside and moaning. It has done so by snatching the initiative, taking the war to its enemies, playing dirty, but playing hard.
And that is why the opposition remains putty in Kanu's hands. It is not solely about Kanu having more money and access to state machinery. That is a part of it. But it also has to do with being focused, resolute and cunning.
Let's get to the point. The Constitution of Kenya Review Commission has been visiting the provinces to gather views on how Kenyans want the review to be conducted. Of great importance is the question of civic education. To Kanu, civic education is of political life and death. Being in essence a dictatorial party - by tradition and increasingly by force of necessity - Kanu believes in the control of information.
It fears that the free flow of unembellished education might enlighten the masses upon whose ignorance it has profited for 38 years. It therefore wants a civic education curriculum that does not include anything that might challenge its political well-being.
Kanu leadership understands
But it did not wait for the commission to collect the views and then make noise at the twelfth hour. The Kanu leadership understands two things: First, the Ghai commission issupposed to figure out what the majority want by listening to views.
Secondly, the Kanu leadership understands the process of the formation of public opinion and of defining the public agenda. It knows that in actual fact, there is no such thing as public opinion.
Opinions are not formed in committees and barazas because there is no such thing as collective thinking. All opinions originate from individuals, they become public opinions when their authors manage to convince others.
Which brings us to the pinnacle of the argument. Kanu wants the Church and NGOs off civic education. It also wants to have control over what ends up in the curriculum. It knew making noise in barazas is not going to influence the commission.
So it brought out the big guns - ministers Nicholas Biwott, Henry Kosgey and Julius Sunkuli - and sent them hunting. As a result, not only are its views firmly in the commissions books, but it has also set the agenda and quite possibly won a few hearts.
On the flip side, the opposition snores, deep in slumber. It has views different from those of Kanu but would not recognise an opportunity even if it bunched itself into a fist and struck it on the eye. What, pray, does the opposition think its job in the review is?
Doesn't it see that a presentation to the commission is also an opportunity to market opinions where it matters - among the people?
What a disappointing absence of focus. Okonkwo would have been furious.
In last week's column, it was said that Houston was the state capital of Texas. The political and administrative capital of Texas, where the state legislature meets once every two years, is actually Austin. Houston, President Bush's hometown, is the state's commercial, financial and pollution capital.
The writer wishes to apologise to readers for the error and thank them for their numerous corrections. He also wishes to assure them, especially those from North America, that it is not his desire to dismiss out of hand Mr Bush as a man of lower than average abilities.
mutuma@nation.co.ke

Original_Search_Title: Burgess And Hassim Win Sanlam Prize
Headline: South Africa: Burgess And Hassim Win Sanlam Prize
Body_Text:
Yvonne Burgess has won the 2001 Sanlam Literary Award for Fiction for  her novel ALarger Silence, published by Penguin. In the unpublished  category,
Aziz Hassim won for his novel The Lotus People. Burgess's novel was  preceded by Anna and the Colonel: "And the Colonel just would not die," she  explains.
So she brought him back in A Larger Silence -- and "simply took  dictation from the Colonel". Burgess lives in a retirement village in Port  Elizabeth.
Adjudicators Arthur Maimane, Tim Huisamen and Stephen Gray compiled a shortlist of Elleke Boehmer's Bloodlines, Zakes Mda's The Heart of  Redness,
Phaswane Mpe's Welcome to Our Hillbrow and Sheila Roberts's Purple  Yams.
Huisamen said Hassim's "marvellous, engrossing work" held him from  chapter to chapter, fuelling his interests in meta-fiction and the role of the writer as historian. Spanning more than a century, The Lotus People  served as "a form of catharsis" for the 65-year-old Hassim. He calls the  cleansing process his "personal Truth and Reconciliation Commission".
Gray hailed the novel as "unputdownable ... one of those one-off, unpredictable things ... a masterwork".
This year the Sanlam prize received some 100 entries.
She was fÃªted like royalty and performed for royalty when the queen  of
Malaysia attended her album launch

Original_Search_Title: Cooperatives Urged to Be Open
Headline: Ghana: Cooperatives Urged to Be Open
Body_Text:
Mr. Okyere Darko, Nkoranza District Co-operative officer on Monday called on co-operative societies and groups to exhibit transparency, accountability and probity in their activities.
Addressing the fifth annual general meeting of the Techiman District branch of the Co-operative Yam Producers and Marketing Society Limited, he advised the members to unite to promote their welfare.
Mr. James Okyere, Deputy District Co-operative Officer said the society made a net surplus of more than 24 million cedis in the 1999/2000 financial year as against 22.7 million cedis in the previous year.
Mr. Okyere said the society's total income of 54,561,036 cedis was derived from the sale of yams, bank interests and entrance fees.The society spent 30,277,748 cedis on committees' allowances, wages and salaries, transport, stationery, donations and funerals.
Mr Okyere said since the society was established no member had contributed to the share capital, adding that 2,512,934 cedis being dividend on shares accruing from the net surplus has been turned into shares for the members.

Original_Search_Title: Author's Latest Novel, Purple Yams, Released
Headline: South Africa: A South African Abroad
Body_Text:
Shortlisted for this year's Sanlam Literary Award, Sheila Roberts's
new novel, Purple Yams, is just out from Penguin. Stephen Gray interviewed
her on her recent return to Johannesburg.
Since 1975 when she made her debut with the prize-winning collection,
Outside Life's Feast, Sheila Roberts has sustained an impressive career in
publishing.
Two later collections have followed, and she has three previous novels
to her credit -- the first, He's My Brother (1977), was banned in a
notorious case for the way it portrayed foul-spoken South African men.
That year she went into voluntary exile in the United States, where she
teaches creative writing at the University of Wisconsin at Milwaukee.  One
of her recent stories was included in The Picador Book of African
Stories.
Q: Quite unexpectedly your new novel pulls your bipolar world
into a triangle.  It's set, not in the US, but in Japan.
A: I got the chance of a visiting professorship there and being single
by then I was in an ideal situation to tackle it. So I found myself a lone
professional woman, for more than two years. Halfway through the loneliness
came down on me, and I started to need to write about it. The first versions
of Purple Yams were much more autobiographical; later I saw the
possibilities in fictionalising -- and we're talking about a work that's
seen eight drafts! When I had the distance, it began to come out right.
Q: From South Africa we've only ever had William Plomer and
Laurens van der Post taking on Japan before, but their work seems a bit
touristic now.
A: Yes, but once I had my protagonist being really critical I could get,
I hope, more deeply into things. And also deeply into memories of her
earlier life here. While in Japan she is their "other", so she is forced to
remember situations at home in which there were various other "others" --
not only black people, but those troepies in Potchefstroom, for example. I
hoped in that way to force her towards a point of change.
Q: It takes a natural disaster, in those terrifying earthquake
scenes.
A: Well, when I was there the Kobe earthquake did take place, in which 5
000 people were killed. In Japan you are always aware of these frightening
tremors in any event. I needed a dramatic moment like that to alter the
situation, and although I wasn't at Kobe itself, I researched it all -- via
the documentation on the great San Francisco quakes. That disaster happens
and by the end her relationship with the Japanese has become utterly
altered.
Q: Has your being in the US for more than 20 years had a bearing
on how you see your readership?
A: Yes, but I didn't want in Purple Yams to have an American heroine,
because that would have put me into language problems I couldn't handle.
Anyway, I've never been able to rinse my mind of South Africa. My own
memories keep coming back and I often return, as you know. So logically I
kept to having a South African woman and her speciality being rock art just
followed.
Q: But Japan was obviously good for you, since you haven't
produced such a major work for years.
Thanks to the release of time I had there. I felt there I could at last
write and write forever ... Back in Wisconsin I find the workshops and so on
very, very taxing.
Q: Now Purple Yams is going to be a tough one for South African
readers, perhaps. Too mature.
A: I suggest readers put up at first with any dislike they have for my
character. Just be aware that the situation she finds herself in is not
static -- she is on the move, towards frightening events. But also towards a
broadening of her own mind and her understanding. After all, in the end she
decides to stay on in Japan, however alien.

Original_Search_Title: RUF Claims CDF Attacked its Men
Headline: Sierra Leone: RUF Claims CDF Attacked its Men
Body_Text:
The rebel Revolutionary United Front (RUF) said this week it would abide by
a ceasefire it signed in mid-May with the pro-government Civil Defence
Forces (CDF) militia despite CDF attacks on the villages of Lelewah and
Woodu in northern Sierra Leone on 17 and 19 June.
UN Mission in Sierra Leone (UNAMSIL) spokesman Major Mohamed Yerima told
IRIN on Thursday that the attacks were carried out by militiamen who had
been in Guinea and were largely unaware of the RUF-CDF ceasefire. He said
UNAMSIL patrols were investigating the incidents in which, according to the
RUF, at least 35 civilians and four RUF fighters were killed.
Meanwhile, the RUF has asked the UN for more information on a proposed truth
and reconciliation commission, UNAMSIL reported. The rebels also wanted
information on a proposed UN tribunal that would try crimes committed during
Sierra Leone's war.
The war, which began in 1991, caused great destruction, including in the
diamond-rich eastern district of Kono where, according to World Vision (WV),
90 percent of the buildings were damaged or destroyed, particularly in the
district's main town, Koidu.
WV reported on 16 June that health care and schools were "practically
non-existent" in Kono and that people were surviving on bananas and wild
yams. Up to 80 percent of Kono's pre-war population, estimated at 510,000,
had fled fighting in the district, which used to be the country's
breadbasket. WV said tens of thousands had recently gone back to the
district and more were returning daily from refugee camps in Guinea.

Original_Search_Title: Ninety Percent of Buildings Destroyed in Kono
Headline: Sierra Leone: Ninety Percent of Buildings Destroyed in Kono
Body_Text:
A decade of fighting in Sierra Leone has left 90 percent of the buildings
damaged or destroyed in the eastern district of Kono, particularly in its
main town, Koidu, World Vision (WV) reported on Saturday.
Homes, schools and clinics are among the buildings affected. WV reported
that health care and schools were "practically non-existent" in Kono and
that people were surviving on bananas and wild yams. The NGO said tens of
thousands of people had recently gone back to the district and more were
returning each day from refugee camps in Guinea.
Up to 80 percent of Kono's pre-war population, estimated at 510,000, fled
fighting for control of the district, which used to be the country's
breadbasket. World Vision had been the only international NGO operating in
Kono since 1996, until it, too, was forced to leave the area after it fell
under rebel control in 1998 and became inaccessible.
A World Vision team returned to Kono last week after an initial assessment
of critical needs in the diamond-rich area.

Original_Search_Title: Industrialist Recommends Guarantee Agencies as Security for Smes
Headline: Nigeria: Industrialist Recommends Guarantee Agencies as Security for Smes
Body_Text:
THE provision of guarantee agencies as security for the 10 per cent
profit before Tax (PBT) Loan to small and medium scale enterprises
(SMEs) has been suggested as a solution to the fears being expressed
by banks that the beneficiaries may not redeem their loans in the
absence of collateral security.
According to the Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of Shower Limited,
Mrs. Ekema Akpan, "since guarantee agencies work like insurance
companies they could be used as security in event of default".
She added that the credit should be disbursed in growths with a
guaranty agency so that there could be some measures of checks and
balances among members.
On the Africa Growth opportunity Act (AGOA), Akpan was of the opinion
that the Nigerian government will have to enlighten the people
especially the small scale entrepreneurs on how they could benefit
from the programme, while condemning the mentality of some Nigerians
who would not take pride in having "made in Nigeria" label on their
product.
According to her, "the government must create time and make a policy
that will re-orientate the people on how to be proud of their work.
We should also encourage a lot of Nigerians abroad to come and invest
in the country.
Most Nigerians abroad export Moi-moi, Pounded yam and Egusi soup in
cans to Nigeria. But they were able to do that because the
environment there was conducive for such".
She regretted that in fabrics and textile production, that Ghana is
still ahead of Nigeria, saying "we need to catch up with them
otherwise we will lose our opportunity in AGOA because it is not
charity".
Lamenting that a lot of industries in Rivers State have closed shop
as a result of multiple levies and high cost of production, she
called on the government to intervene so as to shore up the
productive base of the nation.
Asked how the manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN) has assisted
the SMEs in the past, Akpan who is a member of SME committee of MAN,
said MAN encourages bigger companies to assist the smaller ones
provided the products are good and competitive.

Original_Search_Title: Boy 13, in Court for Stealing Mobile Phone
Headline: Ghana: Boy 13, in Court for Stealing Mobile Phone
Body_Text:
A 13 year-old primary four pupil, who stole a mobile phone belonging to a student of the University of  Cape Coast while running an errand for her, was on Monday arraigned at a Community Tribunal at Cape Coast.
In the dock with the boy was Ebenezer Acquah, alias Jojo, unemployed, who was charged with dishonestly receiving the phone.
The boy pleaded guilty and the tribunal presided over by Mr Peter Asare-Duah bonded  him to be of good behaviour for a year and put him on a three million-cedi bail, pending a later sentence at a juvenile court and the disposal of the charge against Acquah.
Acquah, who pleaded not guilty to the charge of dishonestly receiving, was remanded in custody until June 18.
Prosecuting Police Chief Inspector Henry Tanor told the tribunal that on May 11, the student Miss Elaine Foxon-Owoo, asked the boy, who normally runs errands for the students to send a tuber of yam to her roommate.
He said Miss Foxon-Owoo later went to her room and detected that her mobile phone valued at two million cedis was missing and suspected the boy of taking it.
She, therefore, made a report to the Police, who arrested the boy and  questioned him. The boy admitted taking it and giving it to Acquah.
Chief Inspector Tanor said Acquah, however, denied taking any phone from the boy. He said he had collected a toy mobile phone a month earlier from another boy.   GNA

Original_Search_Title: Reverend and Son Docked for Assault
Headline: Nigeria: Reverend and Son Docked for Assault
Body_Text:
Lagos — Seventy-year old Reverend Emmanuel Shodipe and his son, Ademola
Shodipe, have been charged before an Ikeja Chief Magistrate Court
with assault.
In suit number MIK/A/295/2001 filed against the duo by prosecuting
police officer, Inspector Okonkwo Okorie, they were alleged to have
committed the offence in March 2001 at 3 Olaniyi Street, Idi-Oro,
Mushin. They allegedly assaulted Isaiah Ezeogu, contrary to the
provision of Section 355 of the Criminal Code.
They were also accused of maliciously damaging the windscreen of a
Mercedez Benz valued at N21,000, property of their victim and thereby
committed an offence punishable under Section 451 of the Criminal
Code.
They pleaded not guilty to the charges and Chief Magistrate J.
Osinowo released them on bail in the sum of N100,000 while the case
was adjourned to 21 May 2001 for further hearing.
In another development, three men were also brought before the same
court for allegedly conspiring to steal properties belonging to a
family at No. 19 Cappa Estate, Mobolaji Bank Anthony Way, Ikeja in
December, 2000. In charge number MIK/A/295/2001, the trio were
alleged to have broken into the dwelling house of one Mrs. P. E.
Odigbo and stole several properties worth over N1.9m, contrary to the
provisions of sections 516, 411(C) 412 and 390 (9) of 1993 Criminal
Code.
The charge against them reads: "That you Nyiam Ekairo, Martina
Ogwakong (F) Ezekiel Ejim and others at large stole gold jewelry
valued at N1.4m, one National yam pounder valued at N10,000, rug
valued at N2,500, two security lights valued at N15,000, two painted
pictures valued at N14,000 some children stockings valued at N500,
two pairs of men suits and the sum of N10,000 property of Mrs. P. E.
Odigbo..."
They pleaded not guilty to the charges while the presiding magistrate
adjourned the matter to 9 May 2001 for further hearing.

Original_Search_Title: Defence Minister Visits Troops
Headline: Ghana: Defence Minister Visits Troops
Body_Text:
Accra — Dr. Addo-Kufuor, the Minister of Defence last Thursday visited troops
undertaking exercises in and around the Dagbon capital of Yendi.
Dressed in military fatigues, the minister landed at the Yendi
aerodrome in the company of the Acting Army Commander, Major General
Yaachie and the General Officer Commanding the Northern Command,
Brigadier Ayiku.
The minister and his entourage were met on arrival by Lt. Col. Biah
who briefed him about the exercise. He told the minister that due to
the paucity of funds available for the exercise, certain vital field
items were in short supply.
In reply, the minister said the government attached great importance
to the exercise and asked the military top brass to include the cost
of such exercises in the MOD's budget so that adequate resources
would be voted to obviate the kind of problems those undertaking the
exercise were facing.
He made a personal donation of one million cedis to the troops.
The minister later paid a visit to the Yaa Naa who presented him with
a ram and 50 tubers of yam. He told the Yaa Naa that he had called on
him to explain the rationale of the exercise, which in addition to
the military's own programme of field exercises was also to foster
cordial civil-military relations.
The exercise, code-named "Cohesion Khozar" is a joint exercise
between Ghana, La Cote'Ivoire, Togo and Burkina Faso.
The minister has since returned home and the exercise ends tomorrow.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Foodstuffs Soar
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Foodstuffs Soar
Body_Text:
Lagos, Nigeria — Prices of foodstuffs have been on the increase in the past few months
across Nigeria, according to figures released by the Federal Office of
Statistics (FOS).
The FOS said in its latest report that the Consumer Price Index (CPI)
increased from 8.6 percent in January to 10.3 percent in February.
"With an increase of 10.3 percent in the CPI, this means that a fixed
market basket of consumer goods and services purchased with 100 naira during
the 12 months period ending this month now sells for 110 naira," the report
said.
It said the increase in CPI was largely caused by the appreciable
increase in the prices of foodstuffs like gari, a local stable processed
from cassava, as well as guinea corn and yams.
"Prices of fish, processed foods and edible oil also rose in many price
collection centres between February 2000 and February 2001," the report
added.
Experts attribute the increase to the high cost of transportation,
resulting from the scarcity of petroleum products experienced in the country
for the past several months.
The falling value of the national currency, the naira, has also affected
the prices of foodstuffs, even when most of them are processed locally.
The naira has fallen from about 88 naira to the dollar in May 1999, when
President Olusegun Obasanjo took office, to about 115 naira to the dollar at
present.

Original_Search_Title: Multi-national Peace Keeping Exercise Kicks Off In Ghana
Headline: West Africa: Multi-national Peace Keeping Exercise Kicks Off In Ghana
Body_Text:
Accra, Ghana — A multi-national peacekeeping exercise involving the Arm Forces of 10 West African countries including Ghana took off in the Upper East Region of Ghana and Togo on Wednesday 18th April.
The exercise code named "EXERCISE COHESION KOZA" will last for a week. Other participating countries are: Nigeria, Senegal, Cote'd Ivoire, Togo, Benin, Niger, Mali, Burkina Faso. It is under the sponsorship of the participating countries and the French Government.
Briefing the Press at Yendi on Tuesday, the Commanding Officer of the Ghanaian troops, Lt. Col. George A. Biah explained that it is the aim of the exercise, among others,  enhance the capacity of the forces to be able to cope with any emergency crisis in the West African region.
He said it would also improve upon the inter-operation ability of the forces in the sub-region by harmonizing operation between French and English speaking forces in the sub-region.
Citing the scenario of the exercise, the Commander said an imaginary country called "Republic of Fibaloland" has been thrown into confusion and anarchy following dissatisfaction by one of the three major ethnic groups about the result of recent elections, which it claimed, had been rigged by the ruling government.
He said that at the request of the ruling government, the United Nations Security Council decided to deploy a multinational force, "United Nations Mission in fibaloland (MINUFI), to contain the situation.
The forces are therefore to calm tension and facilitate the return of peace, create favourable conditions for the provision of humanitarian services, supervise the cease-fire and ensure the return of displaced persons. Lt. Col. Biah said that was the purpose for a peacekeeping mission for which they are exercising.
Earlier, Lt. Col. Biah and his officers paid a courtesy call on the Overlord of Dagbo, Ya-Na Yakubu Andani II, during which he briefed the Overlord on the exercise.
Ya-Na Andani II donated a bull and hundred tubers of Yam to the soldiers and wished them well in the exercise.

Original_Search_Title: KMA to Pay ¢8.8m T&T Monthly
Headline: Ghana: KMA to Pay Â¢8.8m T&T Monthly
Body_Text:
Kumasi — The Chronicle has firm conviction that the Kumasi Metropolitan
Assembly (KMA) would pay Â¢100,000 monthly to each of the 88 members
of the House as travel and transport (T&T) allowance.
The Administrative authority has approved of an increase in sitting
allowances for members.
Members would now take Â¢35,000 per sitting as approved by the
authority since March 30, 2001.
Meanwhile, 28 persons have been confirmed and sworn in as government
appointees to the KMA.
They include Messrs I. K. Gyasi, Kwame Boafo, Dr. Baffoe Bonnie, Nana
Kwame Agyeman Atwereboanda. Nana Boakye Yam, Oheneba Kwame Kyeretwie,
the Apagyahene and Baffour Appiah Dankwah, the Anantahene of Kumasi
among others.
