The appointed members were sworn in by Mr. Justice G. M. Quaye, a
Kumasi High Court judge.
Justice Quaye congratulated them for their appointment and reminded
them to work in line with the President's zero tolerance of
corruption.
They also swore the oath of allegiance to bear true faith to the
Republic of Ghana and preserve, protect and defend the Constitution.
The election of a Presiding Member was, however, postponed to today,
April 12, 2001.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Urges Support for Scientific Researchers
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Minister Urges Support for Scientific Researchers
Body_Text:
Abidjan, Cote d'Ivoire — Stakeholders in the Ivorian
economic field have been urged to support scientific research by the
country's academics because end-products would eventually benefit
their ventures.
Speaking at the 2000 Scientific Research Award presentation
ceremeony, Cote d'Ivoire's higher education and research minister,
Sery Bailly said promotion of scientific research would significantly
contribute to the progress of science and technology, the economic,
social and cultural
development of the country.
Three groups of Ivorian researchers, winners of the fifth annual
national scientific research prize, were presented with 15 million CFA
Francs and diplomas at a function held at the University of Abidjan.
Research by the first group, led by Professor Tea Gokou, was on
anti-viral molecule related to the fight against AIDS.
The second team, which focused on food and the improvement of yam
production in forest and savannah zones, was piloted by Assa Ayemou
and Ettien Djedji.
Professor Ouattara Tionan was awarded for his study on the
Fohobele minority ethnic community in the Bandama valley, 434 km north
of Abidjan.
"They emerged above the best ones. They are real heroes for they
obtained high quality results despite the difficulties of all sorts
that our country has gone through", Bailey said, commending the
researchers' work.

Original_Search_Title: GSM: Is the Competition Won And Lost Already?
Headline: Nigeria: GSM: Is the Competition Won And Lost Already?
Body_Text:
The only good news coming from NITEL since the GSM auction is the news last week that it could continue serious discussions with MSI as a core partner that could take up to about 49 per cent of its GSM stakes.
Mind you this piece of news has nothing good to say about the ignominious treatment meted to Mr. Emanuel Ojeba, the former NITEL boss who was retired just three months away from going into full retirement. After serving this country for about 35 years, the man deserved a better treatment from some politicians who hold the yam and the knife.
On this page we had said that Ojeba didn't have the steam to stand the competition that was coming but nobody suggested such a shameful exit. There is always a reason for a sack but we dare say here that the gentleman is not responsible for the eclipse enshrouding NITEL's GSM license which it got on a platter of gold.
Those people who suggested that NITEL had no business with GSM but should instead concentrate on offering is primary but backbone services are already being justified. And quite interestingly, the two compertitors already rearing up to go, don't even believe that they have a fight coming from NITEL. MTN in a massive radio campaign says it is the better service provider while Econet on the hand is providing its own information bulletin on the advent of the GSM technology, where the company comes in, and what it plans to do. Merge this with the print campaign already awash in the newspapers, it becomes an over-kill.
Somebody asked me, where is NITEL? Moaning and being disillusioned. The answer comes straight from within the organization. On being told that both ECONET and MTN were due to rollout by next month, a highly placed NITEL source screamed: That is three months before we start. But the point here is that while others are busy recruiting personnel, importing equipment, and building a campaign pending a massive rollout, with a boast by one of them that it will hit a million mark in the first one year, dear friends, NITEL is quiet, extremely quiet and there is every indication that it will now come in as an under-dog.
The good news is that there will be phones soon with or without NITEL if you trust MTN , with time to operate outside NITEL network as company officials have promised. The very sad thing is that NITEL would have narrowed down the competition to just a straight fight between ECONET and MTN. The implications are grave, two of them being loss of revenue and playing a second fiddle in a land where it has been a superman.
The easier thing would be to leave NITEL to the mysterious strength of market forces but the most patriotic and expedient thing to do would be for concerned Nigerians to save NITEL from itself and from the choking hands of politicians.

Original_Search_Title: Only True Federalism Can Make Nigeria Great-Omeruah
Headline: Nigeria: Only True Federalism Can Make Nigeria Great-Omeruah
Body_Text:
Yenagoa, Bayelsa — Former Minister of Sports, Air Commodore Samson Emeka Omeruah (rtd) has declared that only the practice of true federalism where all the federating units control their resources can set Nigeria on the track of actualising its full potentials.
The former airforce chief who was reacting to President Olusegun Obasanjo's declaration that the clamour for resource control will lead to another civil war said the gruesome 30 months civil war which rocked the 60s was not the struggle for resource control but because of the genocide committed against the Igbos.
Commodore Omeruah who spoke to Vanguard at the Port Harcourt International Airport described the Nigerian system as imperialistic and autocratic in nature for the federating states to attain their full potentials.
His words: "What is going on now in this country is not true federalism, if we must energise the kinetic energy existing in the individual state we must practice true federalism.
"The system that is presently operating in the country is imperialistic and autocratic. So we have to put in place true federalism. That is the only time when you can now release the abundant energy that exist in all the facets of our polity. If you really want to live in a place where your land grows yam and corn, you will want to be part and parcel of the eating of that corn so I don't see anything wrong with it. In the American system which we are trying to copy, resource control is in the states. What the federal government does at the national level is to tax those states with resources."
Continuing, he said: "If government can realise that there is need for those who are in the environment where the resources are to benefit from it, it will do our country well because it is God endowed and if God has given you a talent you should exploit it. I don't see anything wrong with it.
"But the fact is that the federal government, in order to meet up with other exigencies and expenditure should tax the people appropriately, that is the way it is done. The people within the vicinity should have control over their resources.
On the civil war, he said "most struggle this way has been that of resource control but the Nigerian civil war was not really resource control fight. The war started because the Igbos were purged, they were being massacred and they didn't know where to go but only to run to their homeland. "There were some argument which were not met and because of disagreement in meeting those agreement there was a civil war. "So it was basically not resource control war. If we know there was so much oil in Igboland as it is now the war would have been highly competitive."
He called on the people to be steadfast in their legitimate aspiration to have control of their resources saying the president's statement was meant to frighten them into abandoning the struggle.
On the alleged marginalisation of Ndigbo he said "marginalisation, I think, is a thing of the mind. Luckily for the Igbo race they have the capacity and sagacity do away with marginalisation."
According to him "if the Igbo can do those things they know best which is their belief is communalism and their drive for commercial ventures they should be able to do away slightly with the talk of marginalisation."
Said he: "you discover that even those we think are better off in terms of infrastructures are also talking about marginalisation. So you don't know who is being marginalised."

Original_Search_Title: OPD Settles Mile 12 Market Rift
Headline: Nigeria: OPD Settles Mile 12 Market Rift
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Office of Public Defender (OPD) has settled the age-long rift
between warring factions at the popular Mile 12 market.
The rift, which resulted in Yoruba/Hausa ethnic clashes last year,
continued unabated until the OPD waded in recently.
Speaking at the peace meeting brokered by the OPD, the Vice President
of the Shukura Yam Sellers Association, Alhaji Ismaila Umaru Lado,
expressed gratitude to government representatives at the meeting.
He appealed to both sides to forgive and forget the past in order to
give room for peaceful cohabitation among Yorubas and Hausas.
Alhaji Lado explained that the cross-cultural trading existing in the
market has led to different problems and misunderstandings. He,
however, stated that what mattered most is the peaceful resolution of
the cases without rancour.
He also called on the traders to report their grievances to their
respective leaders in the market so that such issues can be resolved
amicably.
While appealing for calm and patience from the different ethnic
groups in the market, he advised that all conflicts in the market
should be channelled through the OPD.
Responding, the head of OPD, Mrs. Akinlade called on all aggrieved
parties in the market to embrace peace.

Original_Search_Title: Supreme Court Decision Not Final
Headline: Nigeria: Supreme Court Decision Not Final
Body_Text:
Chief Bola Ige, Attorney-General and Minister of Justice, is a man in
the eye of the storm. Since he went to the Supreme Court to seek
judicial interpretation of sections of the 1999 Constitution central
to resource control, he has been the butt of attacks by those who
believe that the action runs contrary to his long-held notion of true
federalism. Even among his colleagues in the Afenifere, Ige has been
under serious attacks for differing with some of them on their call
for a Sovereign National Conference. Not one to be humbled by
critics, Ige has been firing back. For more than one hour on Monday
night, he entertained questions from TEMPO's team of WILSON UWUJAREN
and Goodluck Ebelo
We would like to begin with the Sharia issue. You have been quoted
as saying that the Sharia is constitutional. What informed this view?
I guess newspapers have their way of construing whatever you say.
I gave an interview to ThisDay and they decided to do a caption in
the way they liked which was not quite the same as what I said in the
body of the interview which they reported verbatim. What I said is
this, from 1979, both in the 1979 Constitution and in the 1999
Constitution, Sharia is provided for. You in fact, had not only
Sharia courts, you had Sharia courts of appeal whose custodians, the
Grand Khadis, our constitution recognises. I said what some of the
states in the North have been doing is political Sharia. The Sharia
allowed in the constitution has to do with how Sharia affects the
civil life of Moslems. Political Sharia pretends that it is invoking
everything that a Moslem accepts as his way of life. The penal code
of the northern states, which is different from the criminal code of
the southern states has always taken into consideration some of the
elements that you might call criminal Sharia and they are part and
parcel of the penal code. What our latter day evangelists of Sharia
are doing is to say we are going to establish Sharia courts which
will deal with the total life of Moslems.
Now, if you meet any Moslem, whether northern or southern or eastern
or western, any Moslem will tell you that anybody who accepts the
Islamic faith accepts that his life will be governed by the Sharia.
And so, what the political Sharia people are doing is to use that
emotion to take Moslems to Sharia courts where the Moslems allow
themselves to be governed by the criminal aspects of Sharia which is
not in the penal code.
And so, what they're doing is not flying in the face of the
constitution, but it is not at the same time what you will say is
what is provided for in the constitution. Up till today, in spite of
the fact that Bariya Magazu, that is the girl who was given a hundred
strokes of the cane... I have received about 10,000 protests from
within and outside Nigeria but the girl herself has not complained
that her rights have been violated.
It is true that we do not like some of the things that are taking
place in the Sharia courts, the so-called Sharia courts. But the
truth of the matter is if somebody willingly submits himself to the
jurisdiction and says at the end of it Allahu Akbar, God is Great,
how does the Attorney-General of the Federation go to court and say
'you have violated the man's rights.'
And the Christian Association of Nigeria and Olisa Agbakoba have
taken cases to court and the problems that they have had to face is
the issue of locus standi. That is, wetin be your own. The people
about whom you are complaining have not complained and I said I am
waiting for someone whose rights have been violated to come and
complain and then see whether I will not do anything. That's what I
said and I have said, and I want to repeat it, not one Moslem has
gone to court to say his rights have been violated and no Christian
has complained that he has been taken to the Sharia court, which the
political Sharia people have set up, and tried in accordance with
Sharia whether the civil or the political Sharia after he has told
them that he is a Christian.
And, I still ask any of you journalists, whether anyone has come to
your office to say 'look I am a Moslem or I am a Christian, I have
been taken to a Sharia court and I have told them that I shall not
submit myself to their jurisdiction' and they have been tried in even
this Sharia court about which we are complaining. That's what I said.
You have made a distinction between civil and political Sharia.
Of course.
Is the political Sharia constitutional?
If it was constitutional, I would not make a difference between
the Sharia that is in the constitution and political Sharia.
What are the goals of political Sharia?
Plainly political. You see, people don't do their homework. The
people who started political Sharia are the All Peoples Party
governors in the old Kebbi, Zamfara and Sokoto states. And it is and
was an attempt of the APP governors and mark you, all the nine
governors of the APP are in the North. Not one is in the South. The
intention of the APP governors in introducing political Sharia is to
put the back of the PDP state government in the North up and to show
them to the Moslems that they're not true. And, of course, the PDP
state governors in reaction now want to show that they are more
fundamentalist and more Sharia people of the old Sokoto State. You do
not know that there has always been what you would call theological
tension between the Quaddriya Moslems of the old Sokoto State and the
Tijjaniyya of the old Kano State. And those of you who are Moslems
will know that the Kano people are more liberal politically but when
it comes to a point where they want to show that they are true
Moslems, they can be more fundamentalist or appear to be so than the
Quadriyya people.
Now, people who don't even know what is happening, they dabble in
some of these things and then they would notice however, that whereas
the governors of the three states of the old Sokoto are what you
would call unabashed in their advocacy of Sharia because they live
within the Sokoto caliphate, the others are only pandering to the
politics of Sharia. Now, part of the problems we face in Nigeria is
that people who don't even know the nuances of these things and who
don't find out exactly what the situation is, they just make comments
and what I continue to say is, for example, the governors who say
they're going by Sharia will tell you that they have not imposed a
state religion. Or have you heard anyone of them say that they have
imposed a state religion? Because they know that the day that they
say they have imposed a state religion, that is contrary to the
constitution. But they say what they're doing is to affirm what every
Moslem affirms. And that what they want is to give them a playing
ground where Moslems can be truly Moslems. And can you fault that?
People have talked about double standards. You mention the fact
that nobody has come out to complain that his or her rights have been
violated by the implementation of Sharia laws. Ironically, we are not
aware that anybody complained about resource control, yet you went to
court why?
I have not gone to court for resource control.
Who went to court?
Have you read my writ of summons?
They said you've gone to the Supreme Court for an
interpretation...
Of section 162(2). I have not gone to court for resource control.
I continue to ask you my friend, go and read my writ of summons and
go and read my statement of claims which are only 10 paragraphs. Have
you read it?
I have
What did I ask for?
You were asking for interpretation of that section you quoted.
Good, where did you get the idea that I went to court for resource
control where did you get that from?
But the issue of littoral status and off-shore/onshore dichotomy
which is central to your suit is a crucial element of the resource
control advocacy...
Now what is the meaning of resource control?
I was going to ask you what the meaning is.
Do I know? Was I the one who introduced it?
If I understand those who use the word they say they've been
dispossessed of their natural resources and they want resources based
in their different states or communities back.
They are perfectly right to want anything. And I have never said
that I am opposed to what they want. I have never said so, have I?
But that is what your action presupposes
It is not, young man!
Then what informed your decision to seek interpretation of that
part of the Constitution that deals with control of resource between
units of the federation and the centre?
Go and read section 162, subsection 2
You can educate us, you are the attorney-general
Go and read my statement of claims. Look, this matter is in court.
It is what in law is called subjudice. I cannot discuss it. Go and
read my statement of claims. You know, people just talk glibly that I
have gone to court for resource control. I have not gone to court for
resource control. I have gone to court for an interpretation of
section 162(2) and that is the territorial limit of a littoral state.
You say you have read it, is it different from what I've just told
you?
No sir. But this...
So where is resource control in that? Not only that, I have asked
for a determination. I said the federal government said this, the
littoral states said this and by the way, it's not just the people of
the Niger Delta that are littoral states. For the third time, I am
letting Nigerians know that Lagos, Ogun and Ondo are also littoral
states. In the past, nobody considered them as littoral states, were
they? Were they?
They were not and that is probably because there is no oil in
commercial quantities in some of those states.
Don't you think I have done Nigerians some good? And so where do
you people find that I took anybody to court for resource control?
I'm interested, where did you people find it?
That is the key element in the argument of people clamouring for
resource control.
Isn't that what I've asked the court to do for us? That Federal
Government thinks this way, states which are littoral states think
this way. Court, please, what is the correct thing to do. Is there
anything wrong with that? In your heart of hearts. And if you read my
statement of claims. I said, FG thinks this way, littoral states
think this way, what is the correct position.
Have I done any wrong?
But there are many Nigerians who have followed your political
career over the years and they look up to you as someone who has
always fought for true Federalism in Nigeria. Many of them feel
disappointed by this whole action of offshore/onshore dichotomy,
littoral states and all that. What have you got to say to them?
I just feel sorry that what we need in this country is to be able
to understand what we mouth glibly. At the risk of being called
immodest, I don 't think there is any federalist icon bigger or
greater than myself in this country. And, therefore, it ought to be
granted that my track record is not such that I would throw away my
belief. I will never do that.
And why is it that people never even look at the positive side of
things. Why have our people become so cynical? And I ask a question:
In 1982, Ambrose Alli went to court against the Federal Government,
did he not win? Why does everybody think negatively and cynically.
The action that I've taken to court, can someone enlighten me how
that infringes on my credentials as a federalist?
I suppose that when you include this onshore and offshore
dichotomy, people think that Nigeria is supposed to be a federation
and that you are fighting against the same causes you have espoused
over the years.
What have I said? Again, I go back, go and read my statement of
claims. Have you read my statement of claims? What is it in that
statement of claims that makes you think that my statement of claims
does not uphold the federalist cause? You tell me one paragraph.
Like the issue of dichotomy between on-shore and offshore. Nigeria
is supposed to be a Federation of 36 states. Doesn't that settle the
littoral argument?
If the constitution is not clear about that, should I not ask the
court to make it clear?
Is the constitution ambiguous the way it is now?
It is so. If the constitution is not ambiguous, I would not go to
court. Or do you think that section is clear?
But we have been managing it over the years and people fear that
you are merely working to the answer by going to court.
Which years? Since 1999, is that years? Have you really studied
the constitution? Have you studied section 162? Why didn't you my
young brothers, when you were coming to me, take the constitution and
come and challenge me. Why must you just come merely because of
people throwing words about. About which very few have thought out.
Sir, these are not just idle talk. A lot of people who really love
you, are genuinely worried?
I agree. I want to express my appreciation especially for the
people who love me but I also have a duty to the people who love me
that we must always pursue truth and that I, at my age let those who
love me know the truth of what I am doing. And they have a duty also
not to allow themselves to be prejudiced by people who have other
motives.
As Attorney-General, given that there are ambiguities in the
constitution wouldn't a review of the constitution be a better and
faster all inclusive solution than going to the Supreme Court.
Yes and no
How?
Yes, in the sense that if we were sure what the result of the
review of the constitution would be, I would agree. Do you know what
the review of the constitution can bring forth? Do you know?
We can't tell.
If you can't tell, then the answer is no. In other words, we must
take a situation as it is because you yourself say you do not know
what the answer will be. So let us take the matter as it is. The
Federal Government feels one way, the littorial states feel another
way. Let court be the arbiter. And I continue to say one thing, the
judgement of the court, whatever it is, will be the beginning of
further political engineering in Nigeria. I hope you understand what
I mean.
Would the review of laws going on lead to the removal of the Land
Use Act and the Petroleum Act of 1969?
The Nigeria Law Commission has a statutory duty to look at the
laws that need to be removed. I have all the decrees which have been
passed in the last 10 to 15 yeas. We are looking at that and those
that need to be thrown out will be thrown out if they are in conflict
with the constitution. With regard to the Land Use Decree, I can say
specifically that you know it has been entrenched into the
constitution and if you want anything done, it would have to be
through a constitutional amendment.
Why is it that you did not so much disagree with your colleagues
in the Afenifere until you took up this appointment to serve under
President Obasanjo?
Actually, the truth of the matter is that there is no fundamental
disagreement. I can tell you that. Some people are playing games but
there is no fundamental disagreement in the leadership of Afenifere.
What is this game about?
I don't want to talk about it
Let's talk about the Alliance for Democracy I remember some time
ago you told TEMPO in an interview to ask Chief Ayo Adebanjo if he
could call Ambassador Yusuf his party leader. Today, AD has had a
convention and a new leadership under Musa Abdulkadir. People want to
know from you if you could refer to him, an alleged ex-convict, as
your leader?
You are the ones who said so because if what you say is libellous,
it's you who have said it. I haven't said so. I think, with very
great respect, Alhaji Abdulkadir will call me his leader.
But he is the National Chairman of your party and you're believed
to have installed him.
Do you install somebody who is your leader?
But is he the leader of your party?
My dear friend, I thought that is a simple thing to understand.
It seems that you doubt the story TEMPO did on Abdulkadir
I have not read the story
I'll get you a copy. Recently, your friends, some of your old
friends in the progressive camp met and they said they wanted to form
a new party of the progressives and we were surprised that you were
not in the meeting. Why were you not there?
Quite frankly, I didn't receive a notice to the meeting until
after it had taken place because I was outside the country. I saw a
letter but the letter did not indicate that people were going to meet
to form a new party. Quite frankly, I didn't know what they met for.
People saw the move as part of the realignment of political forces
you have talked about.
I strongly believe that there will be a realignment of forces this
time. And I pray to God that it should happen within the next 18
months. There must be a realignment of forces. What shape it will
take will depend on those who are going to bring these about but
there must be a realignment of forces. If you know the structure of
each of the three parties, you would know that although the AD is the
best of the three parties, there's no doubt about it.
Whatever internal quarrel the AD has, it is the most focused. It is
the one that people expect to be ideologically correct, it is the one
that people respond to very genuinely.
As for the two other parties, they want to exercise power, that's
all. And that's why you find birds of unidentical plummages, if I can
use a plural for plumage, gather together. But I am praying to God
that within the next 18 months, there should be a realignment of
forces. But the realignment of forces does not mean people who have
already predetermined what they want. No, people should come together
and decide, look what do we want to make of Nigeria. The realignment
of forces that I'm thinking of is the type that will position Nigeria
as a truly great country on the basis of a truly federal set-up which
will be able to bring about the best of Nigerians in which Nigerians,
whatever region or zone or state they belong to will know that the
goal for the greatest good of the greatest number is the one that is
important. Let me give you an example. You probably read in the last
few days that of the six hundred and something thousand who to took
JAMB and WAEC examinations, I think Gombe has fewer than 700
candidates. Lagos had more than a hundred thousand followed by Oyo,
followed by Rivers. In fact, probably all those who wrote the exams
for JAMB from Gombe State were not as many as four secondary schools
in the South West Local Government of Ibadan: Ibadan Grammar School,
St. Anne's School, Yejide Grammar School, and Ibadan People's Girls
School. Now the realignment of forces must be such that could say
while we will not stop people who want to grow, we are going to make
people who are on the floor grow and fast too. And therefore the
mechanism for creating a revolutionary educational situation in the
educationally backward places because Nigeria cannot go on without
serious trouble when you have a state who will send less than 1,000
children for JAMB. And that state probably takes more money from the
Federation account than Osun State which probably has about 20,000.
And in the realignment of forces who will agree that this is the type
of life we want for our people wherever they live. This is the type
of government we want. There must be a realignment of forces,
political forces.
You played a major role in the setting up of the three political
parties, the PDP, APP and AD. Are we to expect another major role
from you in this realignment of forces.
I pray God to use me in any way he wants. One thing I don't do is
to predetermine for myself what activity I engage in politically.
Those who know me know that I don't do that. I accept jobs as they're
given to me. 'Oh, Uncle Bola, we want you to do this' and I will do
it to the best of my ability. I certainly intend to campaign and to
work for a realignment of forces in Nigeria because that is the thing
that can help our country develop.
Talking about realignment of forces and how it affects the
politics of this country in 2003 one of the things everybody has been
talking about is the candidacy of President Olusegun Obasanjo and
Atiku for a second term.
Quite frankly I think the newspapers just want to fill their
newspapers with something that they like to salivate on. I have no
reaction to the thing yet because President Obasanjo and I have not
discussed anything about 2003.
Based on his performance so far, do you think he's ready for a
second term?
You know I don't answer hypothetical questions. I never do.
Let's talk about Osun State before we come back to Nigeria. The
problem in the state between the governor and his deputy, some people
think the problems are compounded by your personality, that you've
refused to sit the governor down and talk to him
What truth do you want me to tell the governor?
That he retrenched workers in disregard of the AD manifesto to
provide employment for all?
Now, is that a crime?
And these are people that have not been absorbed elsewhere. The
economy is not strong enough to absorb them and the state is trailing
other states of the federation in all areas.
Do you really know that? Can you honestly swear to God that you
know? You see, the truth of the matter is, when Osun State was carved
out of the old Oyo State, about 60 per cent of the workforce went to
Osun State. Is that not a problem? Secondly, Osun State is the
poorest state financially in federal allocations, is that not a
problem? Thirdly, because people moved into Osun State and
particularly into Osogbo, the lives of civil servants, all of them
congregated there. Some of them, their qualification is BA Yoruba, BA
Home Economics. And you had situations like in a school of 15
teachers, 9 had qualifications in religious studies. If you are the
governor, would you like that situation to continue? I always ask
people, what would they do if they were in Bisi Akande's position.
Nobody has ever answered that. What would they do? Here is a man,
nobody has accused him of mismanagement. Nobody has accused him of
dishonesty. Nobody has accused him of abuse of office. Nobody has
ever challenged his integrity. Does anybody think he's just a callous
person that he just wants to sack people. By the time of the
retrenchment, Osun State was spending 75 per cent of its revenue in
paying about 23,000 civil servants and teachers. It was when it was
to go up to 89 per cent that he took the action that he took.
Does it sound reasonable for anyone in government to spend 89 per
cent of the revenue of the state in paying 23,000 people? In a state
of over two million? Now, these are some of the things you the
younger ones must sit down... because sometimes I think you just
enjoy being in opposition and criticizing those in government whereas
you could be governor of Osun State tomorrow. If you are the governor
of Osun State and you have to employ 89 per cent of the revenue of
year state on paying salaries of 23,000, are you going to go on?
I'll like to ask you a question arising from your analysis. You
said Osun State receives so little from the Federation account, and I
suppose that many states in Nigeria especially in the South-South
have these complaints...
Please, don't talk about South-South. There is no governor in the
South-South who will complain about the money they take home.
I'll tell you why they complain, sir. Given what they contribute
and given the kind of challenges they have in their state, they have
to complain...
Agreed. Now you're talking but don't compare the two. What I think
you should ask is, the man who has 22,000 writing JAMB exam and the
man who has only 700.
It's all part of this national issue we're talking about but...
What I'm saying is... I'm sorry my dear young people, are some of
the things that we in the past when we wrote for newspapers, we
brought facts from all parts of Nigeria. You just don't focus on
something about which you have very little information. I don't know
what has happened to journalists in this country. People will just
take something and fight over it and you don't look at the total
picture.
Now, the situation in Osun, I think Governor Bisi Akande had said a
new beginning had begun in Osun State in which Osun State is moving
from poverty to prosperity.
And I want to say, and I have no apologies to make, I don't think any
governor in Nigeria can claim to be more focused, to know what he
wants to do and to have experience of doing it as Bisi Akande. And
that's why whatever people say, they've not been able to challenge
his integrity. Right now, I know for example that he's building 330
blocks of classrooms. I told him a couple of months ago that part of
his problem is that he does not have sufficiently good relationship
with the press. If you go to Osun and you find out what has happened
as far as education is concerned, and the amount of money Bisi Akande
is putting into educational development, and health development,
you'll be amazed. But he thought that well, 'if I do my best, the
press will appreciate it.' He does not know that these days, you' ve
got to blow your own trumpet and I've told him that he should change
his style and be more journalists proactive.
My original question is this sir, what is to be done about states
like Osun which are not the least contributors to the Federation
account but receive so little in return.
That's part of why I say there must be a restructuring of Nigeria.
The issue of derivation must be in the front burner, nobody can doubt
that. He who does not work shall not eat, simple. And those who
produce must eat a lot. I'm an unrepentant supporter of the cause of
the oil producing states because God who put oil there knows that
there must be devastation, there must be ecological problems, the
people there don't have good portable water to drink, they have no
land to produce food, they have no water to breed and eat fish.
Therefore, God has given them that oil, the money from there they
should be able to go to Abakaliki and buy big yams, go to Plateau and
buy Swan water to drink and import food with the money which God has
allowed them to have from oil. There's no way we can run away from
these things.
From these oil issue you are talking about, up to barely few weeks
ago, you were seen as a hero but with these whole thing about going
to court the situation seem to be changing...
Don't let us go back to that. I have asked you two intelligent
people, the writ that I have taken to court, what is in it that
denies the people of the oil producing states what belongs to them?
What have I done?
The problem between Akande and his deputy has continued to get
worse. We want to know what you, as a senior citizen of the state
have done in respect of it?
I think you better ask the two of them, not me
You should tell us, you're a senior citizen in that state
Yes, you see...
And it is being claimed that because you brought Bisi Akande to
power therefore you must take responsibility for his actions.
I suppose the people who think that way are entitled to their own
opinion. It's like saying... no I don't want to go into it. I have no
apologies to make for the support I gave to Bisi Akande and continue
to give to him because I am satisfied that he's a honest leader, he's
a hardworking administrator. He has had problems about finance but
nobody has been able to impinge his integrity. And If I am said to
support such a person, I thank God.
And you know, talking about that, has anybody pointed to a PDP
governor whom any of the AD states should emulate? You know when they
talk about PDP wanting to take over in Yorubaland, I said the
simplest question a Yoruba man will ask is in the whole of Nigeria,
which PDP governor are you going to point to, to say this is the type
of governor that we want. And I ask you two gentlemen, is there such
a PDP governor?
What of Chief James Ibori of Delta State.
When we're talking serious things, you're talking such things.
Lets talk about deregulation. There is fuel crisis and the federal
government is insisting on deregulation. People are suffering...
I know. I know about the suffering of the people.
Why isn't it possible to get the government to forego its plans of
deregulation.
I'm not the person you should ask that. I think it's President
Obasanjo you should ask that.
But you're in government
Yes, The opinion that I will give, I will not tell you. It will be
irresponsible to tell you.
But deregulation is it a policy thrust you support?
I am a left wing socialist and I make no bones about it. You see,
when you people talk about wholesome competition and how you want to
open Nigeria to capitalism, once you accept capitalism, you must
accept capitalism, you must accept everything about it. You can't eat
your cake and have it. If you want Nigeria to go capitalist which is
what all of you are clamouring for, you cannot run away from
deregulation. Deregulation is one of the planks of capitalism. I have
never accepted capitalism and it's too late in the day for me to
accept it.
Have you made your opinion known to President Obasanjo?
There is nobody in Nigeria who doesn't know my ideological views.
If you want capitalism, you must have deregulation, you must.
So what is to be done, not on the part of the government but on
the part of the people, what's to be done?
An ideological orientation. Nigerians must decide to take their
own destinies in their own hands but you can't do that in a
capitalist economy.
Deregulation, is the best option for the government?
Why are you asking me? Somebody who does not accept capitalism.
You want me to be making a case for capitalism? You're wasting time.
How do you solve this problem of the oil industry?
Me, I'm not in the oil industry
But you are in Nigeria and you buy petrol too.
Yes.
Of course, as a Minister now you don't queue but members of your
family, you friends they all queue.
That's true. I also know that Nigerian oil is being sold as far as
Burkina Faso and I hear Mauritania, all over.
Isn't it the duty of the government...
It is what capitalism spurns. The people, the Mafia, that manage
to get our oil out of the country, do you think they are people like
me and you?
They may not be but these are human beings that any government
with the police would deal with.
If you want a capitalist economy, do you think of socialists
methods...
In other words, we are right to conclude that President Obasanjo
has failed the nation because...
I have not said so. I have not said so.
As it is now, one is tempted to come to the conclusion that
President Obasanjo has failed, nothing is working anymore.
I think you are very unfair. If General Obasanjo has failed, I
won't be in that government. In fairness to him I don't think there's
any other Nigerian who works harder, who's more committed, who is
truly committed about what should happen for the good of this
country. He loves this country to the marrow of his bones and he is
doing everything humanly possible to succeed.
I can also say that in many ways, he has succeeded. What has happened
is that there are one or two areas which there is compounding of
things but General Obasanjo has done very well considering that he's
only been there for 21 months. He has done very well and I can tell
you that if he didn't, I wouldn't be in this government.
It is difficult to appreciate that with so many problems like fuel
scarcity, NEPA and all these social infrastructure that are
collapsing.
Now, in fairness let me give you an example. The amount of road
work that has been done in the last 18 months, nobody has done it in
the last 40 years. Nobody.
Where is the evidence, I don't see it.
The roads are being made all over the country. I also know about
the infrastructure that is being put in place in regard to power.
Even this year alone, over N60 billion is going to be used for the
improvement of the power situation. You may say that we are not half
done but there's no doubt that a lot of work is being done. I also
know that, take for example on the human rights angle, a lot is being
done and in the next month or two, a unique bill is going to the
National Assembly for children and women. It's going to be a
revolutionary bill in the history of Nigeria particularly given the
various interfaces in Nigeria.
I agree with you or the issue of fuel scarcity. I know that President
Obasanjo himself is extremely worried about this. I sincerely hope
that he and those who are in charge will do something about it but I
tell you he himself is very much concerned about it. And in regard to
power, he has promised that by the end of this year, that will be a
thing of the past. And I know that everything is being done to see
that the promise is fulfilled. May I also add that when it comes to a
vigorous desire to move Nigeria in the capitalist way, a lot of
effort is being put in place.
A lot of people are not happy with the Obasanjo economic outlook
especially his romance with key Western financial institutions like
the IMF and the World Bank, There is genuine fear that he is about to
mortgage Nigeria's sovereignty.
I know him enough to say that he loves Nigeria sufficiently that
he will never want to mortgage the future of Nigeria. But I also know
that his party and the leader of the party in government are
committed to a capitalist tag for Nigeria and frankly, just as during
the socialist hey days in which people were complaining that there
are traumas, difficulties about the socialist structures. I guess
when they also went to try capitalist structures, people are bound to
suffer. But I try as much as possible to be fair because I have never
beern satisfied that Nigeria must follow a capitalist path.
A few months ago, my very good friend and colleague, Adamu Ciroma,
said that I was a loss to the socialists cause because when I was in
NEPA, it had to be privatised, that's the policy of the government.
Let us face it, after Gorbachev betrayed socialism, the world became
a unipolar world with the United States bestriding it like a
colossus. And the whole world now has to tow the line that the United
States plot and Nigeria is an underdeveloped country. We want to
catch up. The socialist countries, because of internal betrayals, are
no longer in a position to be shown to the world as examples. So,
we've got to face this thing pragmatically. And I want to say that
Nigerians will have to be patient. When I see the actions of students
and the actions of labour movements, these organisations act as if we
are in a socialist society. We're not. We're a neo-capitalist society
and the earlier we realise that the better. May be what we should do
is to gather our heads together and say okay since we're doing
capitalism, how do we do it reasonably, I mean properly. Maybe that
is a matter which should come up at the constitution of a national
conference.
I was surprised when you mentioned the achievements of Obasanjo.
You failed to mention the anti-corruption campaign.
Because you were asking me about physical things. As for the
anti-corruption campaign, General Obasanjo and many of us have set
ourselves against corruption. It is the worst cancer that has
destroyed the human fabric of Nigerian society and it is something
that has to be fought very vigorously. But you see, corruption comes
in different forms. When there are incompetent people who ought to be
thrown out of their jobs and you want them kept, that is corruption.
People seldom look at it from that point of view. You see that this
thing is not good and you go ahead and go on with it, it's
corruption.
I was going to ask about corruption in the federal executive
council because you were quoted recently in the media that you were
not appointed a Minister to come and eat.
I never said that. Again, I'm surprised that you would be
misquoting me in my presence.
That was what we read in the papers, that you were appointed to
serve. Are there people who are not serving?
No, I never said such a thing. You must have read it wrongly.
That you were in the federal cabinet to serve and not to eat?
Yes, because Chief Afolabi said that the PDP invited me into
government to come and eat. And I said General Obasanjo did not
invite me to come and eat, he invited me to come and serve. And that
there is nothing in any government for me to eat. That's what I said.
But people are insinuating that your government presupposes that
there are cabinet ministers who are just eating.
They must be stupid, very stupid. Because people know me. I don't
insinuate, I say what I want to say.

Original_Search_Title: This Dan is Cool
Headline: Nigeria: This Dan is Cool
Body_Text:
Dan Foster is Cool FM's biggest on-air personality. The African
American has been setting new standards for radio presentation since
he arrived Nigeria early last year. He took Yinka Ijabiyi through his
life and work
The voice is deep and rich. Though delivered in the true Southern
American accent of its owner, the voice commands the attention of the
listener. And you only have to listen just once, to be hooked.
Behind the deep-throated voice is the intelligence of a man with the
crafty style of a master storyteller. Every weekday beginning at 6
a.m, the masculine voice of Dan Foster helps thousands of listeners
start their day. He does so with plenty of jokes and good music.
Indeed, Dan has managed to carve a niche for himself as breakfast
radio's best host. His delivery is impeccable. He may not speak
Queen's English but his American accented English has millions of
fans.
Dan has a natural style which is as smooth as silk. He puts on no
airs on air. Or off it for that matter. Around Dan, the Big Dog, as
he calls himself, there is never a dull moment. Rib-cracking jokes
and stories that would make you laugh until you shed tears are
familiar elements of his company. He can tell a good story and he
knows it. He has known it since his days as a sound engineer with
Kathy Hughes in the United States. Kathy Hughes, he says, is a black
sister who owned three radio stations in Washington DC back in 1992.
Today, Kathy also presented a live talk show on her stations. Then
Dan was her sound engineer. He communicated with her via a little
microphone in her ear. He'd tell her to take calls, pause for
commercials and even remind her of her appointments when she got off
air. He recalls that he would say things like: "You'll probably want
to take caller number four because he's really an asshole. You'll
have a good time with him," into the tiny mic in her ear. So good was
he with Kathy that she would continually thank him for doing a great
job for her even while she was on air.
Off air, she still showered him with praises. Because of this
familiarity, Dan said he soon learnt to relax in her presence rather
than be shifty anytime she was around like the other workers. He got
so familiar with her that he called her Kathy even though she always
insisted he called her Miss Hughes like the other employees.
It is to Kathy that he owes his success in the radio world today.
According to him, Kathy had told him that she wanted a special
package for February which is the Black History month in the United
States. "So I came up with these creative stories, black stories to
tell. I did some research and came up with some stories, added some
music and sound effects to it and people loved it," he remembers.
It was for one hour. At the end of that hour, people were so
enthralled that they asked for more.
For his one hour of storytelling, he won the Broadcaster of the Year
award.
That was the beginning of his career as an on-air personality (OAP).
Dan is one great storyteller. As he tells his stories, one tumbles
into the other creating a perfect story with a great ending.
For instance, as he's telling the story of how he started out in
radio, he smoothly incorporates the story of how Kathy's devotional
period on radio provided him with the idea of dedicating some time to
God on his show every morning. While telling this story, he continues
to counsel his teenage nephew back in the States about the evil of
giving up to the devil for any reason.
Dan continues the story of his big break. "Somebody else heard me (on
radio) and they started getting me to go tell stories for children of
all ages in libraries, big library though, the Washington National
Library.
I was there and everybody got off to come and see these little kids
gather round me with this little wired microphone. 'Hi kids! How's
everybody? They gave me five hundred dollars just to tell a story for
Halloween. It was an amazing story.
I was telling them a story about this little boy that changes into a
little monster or something and the lights were a little low. And I
said: "They say it's in my family too. And I don't know when it can
come back. It can just... It's getting cold, has anyone seen my
jacket? I'm really getting cold. Where's my jacket? Anyone seen my
jacket?' And they said: 'There's your jacket right there' And I said:
'Okay. I got ta put it on, I'm cold.' I'm putting the jacket on and I
put this ugly ass mask on and I said: 'Oh, there's something
overcoming me. Could someone please pull the jacket.' No one said
anything. And I said: 'Pull the jacket.' They didn't know I had this
ugly, ugly mask on. And the little kids came and said: 'We'll pull it
off.' And when they did, they were so scared they were tumbling over
themselves trying to get away, even their teachers.'
The newspapers made a big story out of this great performance at the
library the next day. On air that morning, Dan says Kathy wanted to
know how much he was paid for the job. And I said, 'you gonna ask me
that on air live? What If I told you it's none of your business?" And
she says: "Oh, none of my business? I'm the one that pays your
check,' she says "Nigger, Please! You will meet me in my office when
you get off the air."
The argument got bigger off air and it strained their working
relationship.
It was about this time that he got a job offer in the Virgin Islands.
A new radio station had just been opened by a young man and he
offered Dan the morning belt. It was called 'The Dan Foster Breakfast
Jam.' Kathy asked him not to go but he chose to anyhow.
Two years later though, he almost had cause to regret his decision. A
hurricane swept away the radio station. Not wanting to return to the
US directly, Dan got a job to coach American Football at a Catholic
School. Again, Dan has a couple of wonderful stories to tell about
his time at the catholic school. He says for instance that, the
catholic school was filled with children from very rich homes whose
parents were only too glad to hand them over to the priests at the
school so they could help train them because the kids had become
spoilt brats. "These were some of the worst kids in the world and
their parents knew it. And of all things, this catholic school was
the one that could beat the public school in fight," Dan says.
Dan says he had a hard wonderful time working with the kids. He was
also given an opportunity to teach at the school even though he
didn't have a teaching degree. He describes teaching as one of the
best things he'd like to go back to sometime. While at the school,
Dan also had the opportunity to write, direct and produce a nativity
play. This play and the funeral of one of his best students are
memories which he continues to cherish.
"The play I wrote for the school was about abortions. So many kids
were having abortions. And this was supposed to be a Christmas play.
The principal was like, 'it's Christmas time. Why do you want to talk
about abortions at Christmas time.' I I said 'don't worry, I got the
theme, it's a heavy theme.' She says 'I hope you're good with this,
don't forget this is a catholic school.' Oh, it was a rough play from
the beginning. It opened up with a scene with a brother coming down
and saying, 'Eh, Sharon, how is it doing?' And she says, 'there's
something I got to tell you, I'm pregnant.' He was like, 'What? It
ain't mine. It must be John's. I know John wanted to kiss you. Oh, it
must be Mike. I heard you hooked up with Mike. So don't come blaming
me.' And she says, "How dare you' and they go on into a fight and at
the end of that, his shirt is torn."
For Dan, acting is about reacting. He explains that his success with
the nativity drama then was helped by the fact that he simply chose
the right personalities in the school for the right roles. He says he
painted a scenario and asked the students to react to it and thus
they got their parts. And this, he says, was what was lacking in the
home video, Face of a Liar in which he acted a part.
"I'm supposed to be an African. A Nigerian that left Nigeria when he
was six and then I come back, what, thirty-eight years later?! I'm no
longer an African, I'm an American. I've been in America for
thirty-two years. So, when I come back and somebody says, 'oh, by the
way, I ain't your father,' I'm pissed.
I say, 'what you mean you ain't my father.
He's like saying, 'No, no Dan, don't get so emotional.' I say 'what,
I'm not an African.' He says 'Don't do that, this is Nigeria.' I say
I need to be me, if someone tells me that right now, I'm going to
react to that situation. I'm like 'yo, big dog's back. What's up Dad?
Thanks for sending me to America. I know I was in Nigeria but, eh,
I've been to America, I've been in DC. You not my father? Eh, what
the hell you mean you not my father? Well who is? You mean you've
been lying to me all these years sending me money and stuff? Who's
the man that abandoned me? Damn it. I wanna know who the hell he is.'
And then when I see him, 'What the hell you abandoned me for?' I want
to be aggressive with the role and do it this way." But he doesn't
get his way and so as far as he's concerned, the film was certainly
not to his taste.
Whether to his taste or not though, Dan says the film sold at least
eighty thousand copies just because he was in it. Right now, he has
vowed to write his own home video because he thinks the Nigerian
market is right for it. All he needs right now, he says, is a good
story.
Dan loves Nigerian dishes. His best Nigerian meal is, wait for it,
pounded yam. Yes, pounded yam and very peppery soup. He loves pounded
yam because "I just love the way they pound the hell out of the yam,"
he says. And as for peppery soup, he says he loves eating the soup
hot and having sweat breaking out all over him courtesy of the
pepper.
His favourite colour is sky blue. He loves a chapman and won't say no
to a chilled bottle of Star lager beer. Though not an avid night
crawler, he says he has begun to be a regular face at Incognito,
Towers and Tiberias.
Coming to Nigeria for him was fraught with quite a number of
difficulties.
After he left the Virgin Islands in 1997, Dan said he headed back to
the United States. The only reason he left the Caribbean where he was
enjoying himself so much was because the girl he was dating at the
time claimed she was pregnant for him. An American like him, she said
she wanted to go back to the States and that if he did not follow
her, he would never see the baby. Not wanting to miss being a
first-time father, Dan said he threw in the towel in the Caribbean
and headed back to Washington. In Washington, he was well received at
the radio station where he formerly worked.
However, when they didn't get back to him with a job after he had
dropped a tape of his voice, Dan says he approached another radio
station, Mix 106.5, for a job. Though a white station, he was offered
a job immediately.
Some three weeks later, the girl from the Virgin Islands came and
called him from the airport that she had lost the baby.
She went on to say she didn't want to see him again. Dan was
devastated but he knew he had to get on with his life and this he
did.
He was at Mix 106.5 FM for two years. He knew that it was time to
move again when he walked into the station one day and saw everybody
filling out their resumes. When he asked why, he was informed that
the radio station had been sold. The implication was that most of the
staff would be replaced by new people. Nobody wanted to wait for such
a day. His next job was as a credit control officer with a credit
firm. The firm helped people with debt problems work out their
credits and even counselled them. It was while on this job that he
saw the Cool FM job on the internet. He applied on-line and he got
the job. At his office then, there were like four Nigerians working
with him. Three of them had sung the praise of Nigeria to high
heavens.
The last one had painted a dismal picture of a Nigeria filled with
crime and death. On the United States official internet site, Dan had
been advised, after he wrote in to seek information on Nigeria, not
to even think of travelling down here. Stories of death in the hands
of the police, armed robbery, and other such violent crimes were used
to discourage him. Dan said that despite all these, he chose to come
to Nigeria anyhow because he loved broadcasting and he, a former
marine, could not be scared into hiding by stories of violence. Dan's
father too was not too enthusiastic about his first child's desire to
go to the 'jungle of Africa' to work. The prevalent mentality in the
United States is of Africa as a continent of highly backward people.
Dan's other siblings, a brother and two sisters, would also probably
have stopped Dan from making the trip had he not been the oldest. Dan
recalls that when his younger brother who is a medical doctor called
him at the station recently and was put on hold with the music of
Britney Spears playing on the station, he could not believe a
Nigerian station could be playing Britney Spears' latest music!
Dan's younger sisters, a nurse and a lawyer, have largely come to
understand he's doing a job he is enjoying.
One year on, he's not had cause to regret his decision to come down
here. He describes the time he has had here so far as fun. He's fully
settled and thanks to the free hand he's given to innovate and create
on his five-hour morning programme. Dan is having a ball. Dan is not
married yet but this is a situation he might remedy in a very short
while. Courtesy of the love he's found right here in Nigeria. Dan's
life has certainly taken on a new kind of fun. He won't allow her
name in the papers yet because he doesn't want to pre-empt or
pre-spoil anything. He has found love and she's a Nigerian.
