been lying to me all these years sending me money and stuff? Who's
the man that abandoned me? Damn it. I wanna know who the hell he is.'
And then when I see him, 'What the hell you abandoned me for?' I want
to be aggressive with the role and do it this way." But he doesn't
get his way and so as far as he's concerned, the film was certainly
not to his taste.
Whether to his taste or not though, Dan says the film sold at least
eighty thousand copies just because he was in it. Right now, he has
vowed to write his own home video because he thinks the Nigerian
market is right for it. All he needs right now, he says, is a good
story.
Dan loves Nigerian dishes. His best Nigerian meal is, wait for it,
pounded yam. Yes, pounded yam and very peppery soup. He loves pounded
yam because "I just love the way they pound the hell out of the yam,"
he says. And as for peppery soup, he says he loves eating the soup
hot and having sweat breaking out all over him courtesy of the
pepper.
His favourite colour is sky blue. He loves a chapman and won't say no
to a chilled bottle of Star lager beer. Though not an avid night
crawler, he says he has begun to be a regular face at Incognito,
Towers and Tiberias. Coming to Nigeria for him was fraught with quite
a number of difficulties. After he left the Virgin Islands in 1997,
Dan said he headed back to the United States. The only reason he left
the Caribbean where he was enjoying himself so much was because the
girl he was dating at the time claimed she was pregnant for him. An
American like him, she said she wanted to go back to the States and
that if he did not follow her, he would never see the baby. Not
wanting to miss being a first-time father, Dan said he threw in the
towel in the Caribbean and headed back to Washington. In Washington,
he was well received at the radio station where he formerly worked.
However, when they didn't get back to him with a job after he had
dropped a tape of his voice, Dan says he approached another radio
station, Mix 106.5, for a job. Though a white station, he was offered
a job immediately. Some three weeks later, the girl from the Virgin
Islands came and called him from the airport that she had lost the
baby.
She went on to say she didn't want to see him again. Dan was
devastated but he knew he had to get on with his life and this he
did.
He was at Mix 106.5 FM for two years. He knew that it was time to
move again when he walked into the station one day and saw everybody
filling out their resumes. When he asked why, he was informed that
the radio station had been sold. The implication was that most of the
staff would be replaced by new people. Nobody wanted to wait for such
a day. His next job was as a credit control officer with a credit
firm. The firm helped people with debt problems work out their
credits and even counselled them. It was while on this job that he
saw the Cool FM job on the internet. He applied on-line and he got
the job. At his office then, there were like four Nigerians working
with him. Three of them had sung the praise of Nigeria to high
heavens. The last one had painted a dismal picture of a Nigeria
filled with crime and death. On the United States official internet
site, Dan had been advised, after he wrote in to seek information on
Nigeria, not to even think of travelling down here. Stories of death
in the hands of the police, armed robbery, and other such violent
crimes were used to discourage him. Dan said that despite all these,
he chose to come to Nigeria anyhow because he loved broadcasting and
he, a former marine, could not be scared into hiding by stories of
violence. Dan's father too was not too enthusiastic about his first
child's desire to go to the 'jungle of Africa' to work. The prevalent
mentality in the United States is of Africa as a continent of highly
backward people. Dan's other siblings, a brother and two sisters,
would also probably have stopped Dan from making the trip had he not
been the oldest. Dan recalls that when his younger brother who is a
medical doctor called him at the station recently and was put on hold
with the music of Britney Spears playing on the station, he could not
believe a Nigerian station could be playing Britney Spears' latest
music! Dan's younger sisters, a nurse and a lawyer, have largely come
to understand he's doing a job he is enjoying. One year on, he's not
had cause to regret his decision to come down here. He describes the
time he has had here so far as fun. He's fully settled and thanks to
the free hand he's given to innovate and create on his five-hour
morning programme. Dan is having a ball. Dan is not married yet but
this is a situation he might remedy in a very short while. Courtesy
of the love he's found right here in Nigeria. Dan's life has
certainly taken on a new kind of fun. He won't allow her name in the
papers yet because he doesn't want to pre-empt or pre-spoil anything.
He has found love and she's a Nigerian.

Original_Search_Title: Traditional Medicine Men Invade Street Pavements
Headline: Zimbabwe: Traditional Medicine Men Invade Street Pavements
Body_Text:
Harare, Zimbabwe — Zimbabwe's traditional medicine
men are seldom far from controversy.
They are at it again, this time claiming they have
discovered an effective AIDS drug, the African potato.
As if the claim is not controversial enough, the medicine
men have invaded major cities in the country, vending the drug on
street pavements.
Street corners and shop fronts in the cities, particularly
the capital Harare, have been turned into open air pharmacies,
much to the annoyance of medical authorities who think the drug
is fake and perhaps harmful.
The African potato, a yam-like tuber whose botanical name is
hypoxis, first hit town in January this year, and has since
spread throughout the country.
The medicine men claim it cures many ailments, such as
cancer and AIDS, which conventional medicine is not yet able to.
With a quarter of Zimbabwe's 12.5 million people estimated
to be suffering from AIDS, the drug has found a ready market.
Officially, 5,000 people are thought to be dying from the
incurable disease weekly in the country, a figure many say is a
gross under-estimate.
"During the first few days, we used to sell about five
pieces (tubers) but now we are selling much more," said Kurauone
Phineas, an African potato vendor.
"I am not disclosing how much because there are many people
who are fighting us," he said, evidently excited about the
booming business.
Cost is another major attraction of the drug to sufferers.
It costs between Z$30 and Z$50 per tuber, depending on size. The
vendors say this is enough for one person for a month.
Conventional retro-viral drugs, by comparison, cost hundreds
of times much more, and are beyond the reach of most Zimbabweans.
But, as in similar cases in the past, the traditional
medicine men are not having it easy with medical authorities with
their latest discovery.
Conventional doctors, including Minister of Health Timothy
Stamps, are warning people against using the African potato which
they say could be harmful as it has not yet been subjected to
laboratory tests.
"Purchasing the African potato from the street is very
dangerous. These herbs are toxic and can kill people on the spot
or damage their kidneys permanently," said David Mabaya, a
director of government pharmacies.
Stamps agreed: "There are eight different types of African
potato classified as poisonous and medicinal herbs, implying that
whoever prescribes it must know what he is doing as it can be
poisonous."
"The herb has a strong action in the human body. An overdose
can lead to poisoning. In fact, it does not really cure or
reverse any illness. What it does is that it makes someone feel
different," he added.
But the traditional medicine men say this is yet another
ploy by modern medical practitioners to under-play their
discovery, in order to dominate the medical industry in the
country.
"Some of them are buying (the African potato) from us and
then reselling to their clients at very high prices. They are
charging patients in excess of Z$3,000 for a stomach ailment but
we charge Z$100 or less for the same concoction," said Phineas.
Patients, for now, seem to support the traditional medicine
men who have frequently locked horns with the country's medical
authorities for dismissing most of their medicines as fake.
"I was in a serious condition when I came to this clinic (of
a traditional medicine man) a month ago. But Mr Ncube prescribed
for me among other things, the African potato. I am now feeling
much better and I have re-gained my weight," said an AIDS
sufferer.
In spite of the medical authorities' skepticism of the
African potato's medicinal value, AIDS sufferers in Zimbabwer are
flocking to the traditional medicine men's pavement pharmacies,
including tourists.
"There are many whites flocking into this market looking for
the African potato. Some are tourists while some are locals,"
said Lizwe Baloyi, who operates a thriving pharmacy mainly
specializing in the drug.

Original_Search_Title: Prof. Nkrumah Recalls Accra Of The '50s
Headline: Ghana: Prof. Nkrumah Recalls Accra Of The '50s
Body_Text:
Accra —  Pof. Joe Nkrumah of the Ghana National Museums and Monuments Board
had part of his childhood days in the city of Accra in the then Gold
Coast. He recalls those good old days with nostalgia in a chat with
the Accra Mail in his office.
SOCIAL LIFE IN The CITY
Accra in the 50s was an interesting and hectic place especially in
the central part, the Orgle Street area or Faase as the Gas called
it. Faase means behind the stream. This is an implication that there
was at a certain period of time a body of water or a stream at the
place. There were various cultural activities in Accra because of the
different ethnic groups existing in the city. The half mile square
radius area around the present day Bank of Ghana and the Parliament
House were scenes of many social activities at weekends and even
during week days. The present day Ghana Commercial Bank was the site
of the famous Rodgers Club, a social centre built by the one-time
colonial governor of the Gold Coast, Governor Rodgers. This spot was
open to all races unlike the European Club, which was an exclusive
all-whites club. Across the road in front of the old Parliament
Building was the Accra Town Hall built by the then Anglican Bishop of
Accra during the tenure of Governor Hodgson. The old Polo Ground
where the Kwame Nkrumah Mausoleum stands today used to be the Accra
Community Centre. There was also the Baden-Powell Memorial Hall,
which also served as a social centre. When the aforementioned centres
were occupied the Bishop's Girls' School could also be used for
social functions.
CINEMA HOUSES
The elite of society had their own cinemas and these were the four Rs
cinemas - Rex, Royal, Roxy and Regal, which were owned by Captan. The
Temani Brothers also built the Cinema Palace, which was the first to
show daytime programmes at 12.15 not forgetting the 8.15pm shows.
Then the Opera Cinema came on board to compete with Cinema Palace. It
cost 6pence to watch a show. As kids in those days we raised money to
enable us go for these shows by selling newspapers like Graphic,
Evening News, Daily Echo and Morning Post. This venture could fetch
us some 5shillings a day.
GROWING UP IN THE CITY
As kids we slept little waking up at 3am because we had to engage in
a number of ventures like selling newspapers and helping the
fisherfolks to earn some money and at the same time go to school.
This was a time when discipline was at its highest peak. When fish
was in season we went to the beach through a stairway near the Ussher
Fort to help the fishermen cart their catch home. We created a sort
of balloon with a cloth and this we used to hold some of the fish we
carried for the fishermen. We carried with us a basket each. We added
the few fish we were able to get to the ones the fishermen gave us
and sold them. This done we went to collect the morning newspapers
which we sold before going to school.
SCHOOLS
As a kid I lived at Osu RE initially and it was at this place that I
started schooling. While there I sold cut yams for my mother. The
pieces of yam were arranged in a pan and to keep them fresh I carried
water in the pan for watering.
In 1951 the Gold Coast District was situated at Nima junction and it
was responsible for primary education. Giffard Camp was for Middle
School education and this was where I had my middle school education.
The late General Kotoka was one of my teachers at Giffard Camp. At
that time all the teachers in the garrison schools for kids of
soldiers like myself had teachers from the military's Education
Corps. My father was a soldier in the Gold Coast Regiment fighting in
both India and Burma. At the end of the war he re-enlisted as a
teacher.
I was not strictly a barracks boy because my father was part of the
administrative and professional class in the military who lived
outside the barracks. I can not remember the feeling I had when my
father was leaving for the war because I was too small.
CHILDHOOD DAYS IN AKWAPIM MAMPONG
I had part of my childhood days in Akwapim Mampong and it was
interesting watching soldiers engage in military exercises during
which they used live ammunition. After the exercises we took the
unused bullets and the empty cases of the spent rounds with which we
played with. We opened the brass shell and emptied the powder and
made it to generate a fine smell. Another thing we did was create
holes on a plank and place the live rounds in them. We then supported
this between two stones and employed a certain trick to do target
practice.
BARRACKS LIFE
The only time that I experienced life was in Kumasi. In Accra in the
40s administrative and professional staff lived outside the barracks.
It was only the infantry men and bands men who lived in the barracks.
SUPERMARKETS
The Kingsway stores was next door to the Ussher Fort in those days
and the building still stands today. It occupied two floors. The
shops were later moved to the new Kingsway when the place was
finished. Other shops were the CFAO, A.G. LEVENTIS, SCOA, UTC, SAT,
G.B. OLLIVANT, John Holt. The Unilever House was built by UAC.
ACCRA IN THE '50s
Places like Abossey Okai, Mamprobi and others were seen as being
outside Accra. From Abossey Okai one had to pass through Fadama,
which was a reclaimed land and the Owusu Memorial Park where the yam
market is. The city had two cemeteries but churches like the
Methodist Church and others had their own churchyards.

Original_Search_Title: Issues: the $5.8 Billion External Debt
Headline: Ghana: Issues: the $5.8 Billion External Debt
Body_Text:
Accra — Ghana's external debt makes a mouthful of zeros behind the main
figures. Dressed, we simply call it Â¢31.7 Trillion just wondering how
many zeros fill our imagination. The loan itself is not as seriously
high as the length of the actual figure expressed in cedis if it
comes to be written. Why do Ghanaians want to ridicule their nation
this way?
The only comments available on the internet concerning the Ghanaian
currency is about the story of a foreigner who was going to receive
money transferred to him through one of our banks. He said he saw
people in the queue going away with filled black plastic bags. It was
when his thousand dollars were packed into a sizeable BLACK PLASTIC
BAG - a good seven million cedis of fourteen bundles that he started
trembling. He had nowhere to hide the trash!
POCKET MONEY
One tends to carry his pocket money in his wallet and not on his head
to the glare of all thieves and armed robbers. And exactly that is
how heavy our small loan of $5.8 Billion has become! Indeed to some
of us it only goes to show that we are not credit worthy enough as a
nation.
To think of it this same amount would have been around seven trillion
cedis just three years ago. Our cedi was then around Â¢2,300 to one
dollar. We have chosen the path of making the same amount of $5.8
Billion cedis sound 31.7 Trillion. This is more than four times what
the Ghanaian tax payer is being asked to pay for that same amount of
dollars!
Meanwhile, for those three years the best many employers have been
able to do has been to increase the yearly salary by a maximum of 25%
per annum. This has in only a few cases ended up in a 100% increase.
Petrol prices got about 25% increase each year to at most 100%. Beer,
soft drinks and other taxable products have not had their prices
increased much. Yet these tax payers are being asked to pay three
times the cedis for the same amount of dollars. This does not make
any economic sense.
If that foreigner were to carry that bag of seven million cedis
exchanged for his one thousand dollars back to New York NO BANK WOULD
EXCHANGE The LOAD TO GIVE HIM EVEN ONE DOLLAR FOR HIS BUS JOURNEY
HOME!
Which means that the cedi is globally worthless no matter how low we
sink it in relation to the Dollar.
On the other hand if that stranger took twenty thousand of that load
of cedis and bought two tubers of yam, he could sell it to some
Ghanaian couple he or she knows in the same New York for some $ 5 or
even $10 to get a good meal. This goes to show how we are just
burdening ourselves to try to cook a price for our cedi. It is
worthless outside.
If we do not wise-up about this fact we shall be putting a useless
yoke on the tax payer for nothing. It is only the products we put on
the international market that can help us to pay the external debt.
And many of these debts have only one digit interest rate and many
years of moratoriums!
The worth of the cedi should therefore not be allowed to stifle the
very process of production! Anyone who borrowed dollars for any means
of production three years ago can simply not pay for it.! Many may
have to abandon their dream some have gone back to Europe or America.
The wonder story of the ex-ministers' cars are all a part of this
problem. Â¢38 million price of the car was around $16,000 three years
ago. One quarter of that amount is now $4,000 after 3 years. This is
today Â¢28 million.
Why do those who operated that kind of cedi depreciation of the
economy not understand the simple mathematics of it and pay Â¢28
million? Ghana listens to any foreigner who comes along as an
economic expert to talk the book knowledge we seen too much to
understand and adore with no ability to re-shape before applying. Yet
we claim to be better educated than most African countries.
The reader should just open page 33 of the latest West Africa
magazine.
On this page is listed the worth of each African country's currency
to the dollar. Even Somalia without a government manages around 2,680
of her currency to one dollar. We are only next to Mozambique whose
floods have shattered her economy.
There is a lot of carelessness in our micro and macro fiscal
management within the various zeros behind our currency figures.
Normally the small child would have asked for a unit figure, one
cedi, to buy herself something. We have trained and are still
training our children to ask for one thousand cedis, five hundred
cedis or the least two hundred! It is the same training we are giving
to our economic managers. It is one point or three point something.
Our cedi is worth nothing! We can as at now never get an
international worth for it! The Minister of Finance would himself
recognise how many pebbles would clear of his eyes and the eyes of
all Ghanaians if we were to say today that our new currency is now
called so so and so,.That it is worth 70 to the dollar and worth
Â¢100. This means the cedis become like our pesewas. This would not
change our external debt much.
BURDEN
Even more courageously the minister can give a new worth to our own
cedis. Seven hundred cedis to one dollar shall never change the
dollar. It shall change the necessary artificial burden we have given
ourselves. It shall make us able to produce more without the tension
of fear!
Surely our internal debt burden can be reduced through that
corruption promoting good for nothing so called market forces induced
high interest rate. There are more than enough computer experts in
the transparent market of the USA. Yet only a few days ago when the
interest rate was going to be changed BY The NEW ADMINISTRATION no
one knew whether it would go up or come down. No one could guess by
how much.
Republicans lower interest rates. Democrats increase it. Both do so
for particular purposes. It was increased by half of one per cent.
This was never done through market forces!
Which market forces in Ghana then are supposed to determine the
interest rate? The sale of cocoyam leaves and snails in the
hinterland? We should take a radical care of the worth of the cedi to
the dollar. If we are able to add a radical cut even a surgical
amputation to the level of the interest rate, this country would see
wonders! People are prepared to borrow, produce and market!
Those who want to remain at their desk in the office should watch us
produce! Artificial manipulation of interest rate and the worth of
the cedis through supposed market forces is no capitalism. It is just
a lazy manipulation.

Original_Search_Title: Bekwai Youth Bare Teeth
Headline: Ghana: Bekwai Youth Bare Teeth
Body_Text:
Accra — A meeting of the Bekwai Traditional Council was last Monday disrupted
when a delegation of the Abusuapanin, Nana Kwame Osei Tutu decided to
meet the council on issues bordering on the proposed destoolment of
the Omanhene of Bekwai, Nana Appau Karikari.
A group of concerned citizens had earlier called on the Omanhene to
abdicate the Ayeboafo stool within two weeks.
Hardly had the delegation led by the spokesman, Opanin Akwasi Poku
briefed the council on their mission that the Queenmother, Nana Adwoa
Pramang III, prompted the council to close the meeting to avoid the
delegation.
The delegation which had been prompted into action following concerns
raised by the concerned citizens wanted to know the reaction of the
council to the destoolment charges against the chief.
Nana Appau Karikari is said to be ineligible because he is not a
member of the Oyoko royal stock.
He is also reported to have impregnated an SSS1 graduate of the local
SDA Secondary School causing her to drop out of school. The said
student (name withheld) is presently married to the chief.
Present at the said meeting were the Krontihene, Nana Okofo Asiedu,
Akwamuhene Nana Ntiamoah Amankuo III, Twafohene Nana Kwabena Gu,
Gyasehene Nana Kwabena Asante III and Akyeamehene, Nana Asante.
In an interview with the Chronicle, Twafohene Nana Kwabena Gu said
the Abusuapanin's delegation came at the time the meeting had closed
having exhausted the agenda.
Nana Gu admitted that the Omanhene had duly been served with a letter
calling for his abdication and that neither he nor the council could
react to the said letter because the Omanhene had travelled outside
the country.
Abusuapanin Nana Osei Tutu and some kingmakers have already initiated
moves to prefer destoolment charges against the Omanhene before the
Asantehene.
Manhyia sources have confided in the Chronicle that the Asantehene's
senior linguist, Nana Boakye Yam has summoned the Abusuapanin of
Bekwai, Nana Kwame Osei Tutu and Opanin Akwasi Poku to a meeting to
this effect.

Original_Search_Title: Hop On The Jazz Train
Headline: South Africa: Hop On The Jazz Train
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — It's a weekend of celebration as local jazz musicians bring out four
new discs. In the past week, it has been difficult to talk to anyone
in the music industry without mentioning the name Moses Taiwa
Molelekwa. His tragic death has cast a haunting shadow on the scene.
This weekend four artists and two groups feature in two concerts that
will no doubt lift the gloom. The range of talent on offer is broad "
from the mesmerising, eclectic sound of Zimbabwean Oliver Mtukudzi to
the lilting timbre of Gloria Bosman. It'll be a fitting, albeit
unofficial, tribute to the late pianist.
Mtukudzi, the legendary native from our beleaguered neighbour, has
practically made South Africa his second home, or rather one of many
as his stature in world music grows with every album. He flies in
from the Perth International Arts Festival to present material from
his latest album, Bvuma (tolerance).
For this appearance he will be joined by a long-time collaborator, as
well as a more recent musical acquaintance. Steve Dyer was musical
director of the ensemble Mahube, of which Mtukudzi was head honcho,
and has been Mtukudzi's producer for five albums over the past four
years. He has found time to put together a solo album, Down South in
Africa, and will undoubtedly take pleasure in sharing the stage with
a man he has been friends with for a decade.
Around the middle of last year, Mtukudzi happened upon a recording of
Ringo Madlingozi's Into Yam from his latest offering Buyisa. The
track grabbed Mtukudzi so much he composed a Shona translation for
himself. The recording, now a firm favourite at Kaya Fm, is said to
be the first that combines Shona and a Nguni language, in this case
Xhosa. The reception has been mind-blowing.
On a recent tour of Zimbabwe by Mtukudzi and Madlingozi, crowds of
10?000 " in Harare and Bulawayo " formed mass choirs to sing along.
The people in the capital accompanied the lagging Shona section while
Madlingozi led in Xhosa.
In the Ndebele-dominated Bulawayo, Madlingozi found strong
accompaniment in Xhosa while Mtukudzi followed with his enchanting
tenor. This weekend, South Africans get their turn to savour the
magic for the first time live on stage.
In a second and equally thrilling concert the country's progressive
jazz label, Sheer Sound, unveils new offerings from an established
exponent and two new finds.
Gloria Bosman has practically had everything fall into place since
the release of her debut album, Tranquillity. She scooped the South
African Music Award for best new artist. She then crossed the great
divide to collaborate with kwaito artist S'bu in the latter's Abantu
Abazwani.
She's collaborated with serious music divas Sibongile Khumalo and
Sylvia Mdunyelwa and has been joined on stage by Bongo Maffin's
Thandswa Mazwai and the celebrated Busi Mhlongo.
Bosman's intended tour with Mhlongo " a phenomenal idea " is unlikely
to happen since it was the brainchild of musical director Molelekwa
and his late wife, Florence Mthoba.
For her new album, The Many Faces of Gloria Bosman, she was supposed
to combine covers with original material. "I decided against using
the tried and tested to support the originals because I felt the
songs could stand on their own," she says of the album, which she
produced, keeping her hand in all 10 tracks.
She describes the process of taking creative control of her work as
gratifying and alludes to being a control freak. "If it turns out to
be a mess, I want to have myself to blame," she says.
Her last album exudes remarkable longevity, with roughly six of the
nine tracks enjoying airplay. Her attempt at repeating her debut feat
begins on Saturday, in the company of the group Voices, who she knows
well, and Heavy Spirits, Sheer Sound's latest find.
Voices are the low-key version of the Sheer All Stars, only these
stars have not recorded solo projects. For their debut, Quintet
Legacy, they bring together experience spanning decades and a heavy
academic influence.
Acclaimed drummer Lulu Gontsana leads a quintet of respected session
musicians. University of Natal-educated pianist, Andile Yenana, notes
how Gontsana "holds us together. He is our link to what is happening
in the country jazz-wise."
Bass guitarist Herbie Tsoaeli's name has been in a number of Sheer's
projects, most notably with that of McCoy Mrubata. Together with
Yenana, they are the less-mentioned reasons for the success of
Mahube. On saxophone, the mean five " as Bosman calls them " have
Sydney Mnisi, a product of Pretoria Technikon who, since 1998, has
frequently performed in Holland. To round off, there is Marcus Wyatt
on trumpet and flugelhorn. Wyatt has extended his talents to arrange
for and produce Heavy Spirits, a new group that's also featured on
the bill.
Heavy Spirits hail from Atteridgeville in Pretoria and have been
together for just more than a year. They got their break at a
performance in Silverton. "We have a loose approach to our
composition and like to experiment" says tenor saxophonist Paul
Vranas. Their approach has yielded a fairly standard bass guitar and
saxophone blues set.
On their first major performance in Johannesburg, the group expects
"to put on a good show, but more importantly, to make people give the
album more than one listen".
The company on stage is likely to give them butterflies in the
stomach. But the irony of how, as they hit the big time, another
artist has just bowed out will not be lost to them.
Oliver Mtukudzi and others are performing on February 23 at Mega
Music Warehouse. For more info contact Sheer Sound on Tel: (011)
444?1818. Gloria Bosman is live in concert on February 24 at the
Civic Theatre from 7.30pm.
Tickets available at Ticketweb, Tel: 082?1400?500

Original_Search_Title: Yam, NYC, Youth Groups Organize Cleansing Exercise
Headline: Gambia: Yam, NYC, Youth Groups Organize Cleansing Exercise
Body_Text:
Banjul — Last Wednesday volunteers from youth groups such as Lend A Hand
society and the Red Cross in collaboration with Yam, the National
Youth Council organised a cleaning exercise coined "set-settal" at
the Banjul Ferry terminal. The purpose of the exercise was to help
the country and part of Yam's initiative.
Mr. Gibba President of the NYC, said Yam and his council felt it was
a "fantastic idea" to work together in a cleansing exercise that
brought together many youth.
Yam consists of 21 students from Denver, Colorado in the U.S.A. Mr.
Gibba said volunteers cleaned the whole area and painted the
building.
Sam Periannan who is the leader based in The Gambia aid he was
travelling by the terminal when he saw that the whole place was
dirty, and felt that it needed some cleaning. He talked it over with
Raymond Gibba and Pastor Forbes and others who endorsed the idea ad
received support from various administrators. He said Yam is the
mother of five Mercyships and that they have 160 places and employ
14,000 people full time. They have five mercyships. He said they are
a Christian organisation made up of youths and adults. They travel
from country to country and clean places.
Ousman Njie the Maine Operations Managers said that the cleansing
exercise was a welcome idea and has been done at the right time. He
further went on to say that volunteers appreciate the job they have
done.

Original_Search_Title: Doctor Raises Alarm Over Food Poison
Headline: Nigeria: Doctor Raises Alarm Over Food Poison
Body_Text:
Lagos — A medical doctor and the pioneer/executive chairman of Market of
Convenience, Dr. Bayo Kumolu-Johnson has raised an alarm over the existence
of adulterated and poisonous foods in Nigerian market.  Speaking during a
courtesy call on P.M. News recently, Dr.  Kumolu-Johnson said about 21
varieties of essential foods are in circulation and are prone to being
poisoned.
Some of the food listed are rice (imported only), beans (variety from
Maiduguri called Olo), sugar (granulated, cube and variety in sachet), milk
(liquid and powder), egg (large, medium and small sizes), palm oil, soup
condiment, salt, vegetable oil, groundnut oil, gari, fresh fish, stock fish,
frozen chicken, frozen turkey, yam tubers, potatoes, semovita (small packs,
plastic containers, poly bags) and fruits in the season.
Dr. Kumolu - Johnson said the foods are being adultrated or poisoned due
to the market environment and the greed of most manufacturers to make money
at the expense of the consumers. He said "palm oil sellers store the product
for a longer period than necessary, worse still, pap and potash are being
added to it to make fake vegetable oil from used oil, bleach palm oil for
more money as well as sell groundnut mixed with sawdust as ground pepper
among others".
Continuing, he said most of the ailments in the country which are being
diagnosed like typhoid fever and some other general sicknesses are caused by
food poisoning. To avert this, he said his organisation has established an
alternative market called Oja Irorun (Market of Convenience) in the
neighbourhood which sells to people in schools, offices, banks, churches. He
revealed that plans are in the pipeline to establish markets in all the
local governments in Lagos State first before moving to other states.

Original_Search_Title: I've Never Been Bothered About My Health - Obiageli Molobe
Headline: Nigeria: I've Never Been Bothered About My Health - Obiageli Molobe
Body_Text:
Lagos — A Pioneer of the Nigerian home video industry, Obiageli Molobe has
never ceased to show her talents and mastery in the artical arts. Now
a grandmother, she is not in the habit of disclosing her age, but she
admits she's has "many" children and has been around for "a while".
Besides featuring in countless movie productions, Obiageli spends
time doing radio drama on Radio Nigeria. She also junkets several
other studios doing voice overs for radio commercial jingles all in
the struggle to keep body and soul together. She however admits that
all too often, the spirit may be willing but the body too weak to
comply. So how does she keep it all together healthwise? Hear her...
"I wake up in the morning at about 7:00 am on a typical day that I
don't have to move out very early. Even then, I don't usually get to
bed later than midnight.
If NEPA allows it, I'm inclined to watch the late night movies
otherwise I read or switch on my battery-operated radio for some
music. Just before retiring to bed, I check my schedule for the next
day. My schedule usually determines whether I'd have to wait a little
bit more before hopping into bed. But I must be in bed by 12:15 am at
the latest.
I have a body system that makes sleep almost impossible whenever I
stay up till about 1:00 am or thereabout. I always leave home at
about 9:00 am. However, I'm in the habit of doing something before
leaving home.
After listening to the morning news, I do my daily chores. I'm a
grandmother now and do not have to cope with suckling babies anymore.
As a result, my morning chore is quite different.
Soon after, I step into the bathroom for a cold shower water which I
have breakfast which must be balanced like tea, bread and eggs.
Sometimes it is ogi (pap) and akara.
I hate taking heavy meals in the morning because they make me feel
heavy and unable to do my theartherical and other duties comfortably.
Lunch comes in the form of rice or eba. This is my real meal. I eat
heavily at this period mostly because it is the time for real work
and one needs to be "solid". For supper, depending on how I feel. I
may skip it altogether. On the other hand, I could make do with bread
and tea, a cup of beverage or boiled yam. When I'm hungry, I eat as
the weight of my hunger determines. When my body begins to tell me
its time for rest, I do not hesitate to rest. As soon as I feel any
form of fatigue, I rest and try to avoid undue stress.
I've never really been bothered about my health system, really
because I do not have that kind of consciousness.
My kind of work is stressful enough. I'm on the move most of the time
shunting here and there. Sometimes it could be all night in case of
the over night productions. Like I said, once my body begins to show
signs of fatigue, I relax.
As a rule, Fridays and Sundays are for relaxation. I try to remain
indoors throughout during these periods. It is at such times that I'm
able to read all the novels within my reach. My favourite authors
include Danielle Steel, Barbara Tyler and Bradford. They thrill me
any day. I love mystery novels. Sydney Sheldon's Bloodline
particularly thrills me. Novels that have been made into movies
generally give me great joy. I love I also watch any home movie that
may be around at that time. I could take a trip to the beach to rest
with anyone of my children who wants to accompany me.
What you would find me doing on a typical rest day is window-
shopping. just go down to Cash & Carry and while away the time
comparing the prices of various items on display. Believe it or not,
this helps to relax my tensed nerves."

Original_Search_Title: A Panel's Thorny Path To Peace
Headline: Nigeria: A Panel's Thorny Path To Peace
Body_Text:
Lagos — Apparently worried and in a bid to find a lasting solution to several
re- occurrences of bloody ethnic clashes which have resulted into
wanton destruction of lives and property, the Lagos State government
on December 1, 2000 inaugurated the tribunal of inquiry into the
civil disturbances in the state between December 1999 and October
2000.
The tribunal is headed by a retired High Court judge, Justice Victor
Famakinwa with other members drawn from various professions.
There is no doubt that the ugly incidents in the state termed, "Lagos
Mayhem" or "The dark days" are still fresh in the minds of many
Lagosians who were caught unawares by the bloody violence in October
last year allegedly unleashed on the state by members of the Oodua
Peoples Congress (OPC) and the Arewa Forum representing Northern
interest.
For many Lagosians, the prayer was 'never again' to such incidents
which had led to a colossal loss of lives and property worth millions
of Naira. Utter confusion, and a feeling of insecurity amidst
wailings were the order of the day during the mayhem.
Although Lagos State is the smallest state in the federation, yet it
has the highest population due to its cosmopolitan nature. The state
which was formerly the country's capital is still regarded as the
commercial nerve centre of Nigeria. Its population is over five
percent of the national estimate comprising various ethnic groups.
According to the 1991 national census, the state has a population of
5,725,116 out of a national estimate of 88,992,220. However, based on
a United Nations (UN) study, the state is estimated to have about 8.5
percent of the total state population with a land mass of 37 percent.
In a recent UN study however, the city is expected to hit the 24
million population mark and thus be among the ten most populous
cities in the world by the year 2015.
No doubt, the population explosion coupled with the high rate of
unemployment of the youths in the state, has in no small measure
contributed to the hydra-headed re- occurrences of ethnic clashes.
In his speech at the inauguration, Lagos State governor, Senator Bola
Tinubu had stated thus: "I am sure that the chairman and members of
the tribunal will apply themselves diligently to this arduous and
challenging task which will assist the state in putting an end to the
gruesome and senseless killings of innocent citizens under the guise
of ethnicity and bring succour to the common man. I am certain that
you will bring to bear your well known reputation for integrity and
diligence."
Though the tribunal was set up mainly as a fact- finding mission into
the crises, the following are also its terms of reference:
* To inquire into the remote and immediate causes of the civil
disturbances which occurred.
* To ascertain the circumstances surrounding the incidents.
* To determine the number and identities of casualties in the course
of the disturbances.
* To identify persons or groups responsible for the civil
disturbances.
* To determine the extent of loss of lives and damage to properties
occasioned by the disturbances.
* To inquire into the other matters incidental to the above which may
assist the state government in coming to a fair and just conclusion
on the subject matter of the inquiry.
* To make recommendations on this and any other matters incidental to
the above which in the opinion of the Tribunal would assist
government in preventing future occurrence.
According to Justice Famakinwa, "a total of 64 witnesses have been
heard, while over 300 memoranda / petitions have also been received
with more entries pouring in daily." In pursuance of its objectives
and in an attempt to have a stronger view and under-standing of the
situation at the affected areas, the Panel, therefore, had on January
23, 2001 embarked on a tour to make an on the spot assessment of the
different local government areas which were mostly affected by the
mayhem.
Among the local government areas visited are Kosofe, Ifelodun, Ojo,
Agege, Ifako- Ijaiye and Mushin.
The visits, however, have been evoking mixed feelings among Lagosians
particularly from the Hausa communities in Lagos who have at
different occasions protested the rationale of the Tribunal visit,
accusing it of discrimination as most of their (Hausa) affected areas
were allegedly intentionally over looked during the visit. In fact, a
brawl almost resulted at Ajegunle when some Hausa leaders had minor
misunderstanding among themselves on who should be the spokesman.
However, Justice Famakinwa rose in stout defence of his action.
Debunking the allegation, he said, "it is not true that we
intentionally left out some of their affected areas as claimed. As
human beings, there is a limit to the areas we can cover per day. We
cannot be everywhere in a day. It is not possible. But I am assuring
them that all areas shall be visited. All they have to do is to send
in their memoranda and we shall act appropriately."
Justice Famakinwa added that "I don't want our people to see this
Panel as a means to victimise them or to apportion blames, but rather
as a fact- finding body seeking first hand information in order to
arrive at a logical conclusion in its recommendation to the state
government."
Throughout the visits to all the council areas, two questions kept
re-occurring . These bordered on the issue of the Oodua Peoples
Congress (OPC) and the clashes between the three major ethnic groups
in the country. Counter-accusations were the order of the day as each
group tried to absolve itself of blame on who started the mayhem.
Different versions of how and what started it were freely traded.
The burnt Akanimodo and Ajelogo markets at the popular Mile 12 in
Kosofe area were the first point of call. It was reportedly burnt
down by some miscreants believed to be members of the OPC employed by
two warring parties belonging to the Sukura Yam Sellers association.
Power tussle within this association was said to have led to the
bloody clash last October. According to one of the many eye
witnesses, Sukura Market is predominantly composed of Hausas who call
the shots there. One of the stories of how it started has it that the
Yorubas in the area apparently irked by the dominance of the Hausas
decided to call their bluff by attempting to take control of the
market. This subsequently led to a bloody clash between the two
ethnic groups with the OPC featuring prominently.
Another version said the power tussle among the incumbent executives
at the Sukura market was responsible for the problem. It was said
that the executive refused to leave the post after their normal
tenure. This Vanguard Midweek Features (VMF) gathered did not go down
well with the other party which was accused of using OPC members to
threaten the executive. And the exco largely made of Hausas decided
to eject the Yorubas from the market after earlier asking them to
leave the place as their continued stay in the market constituted
insecurity to their lives and property.
Also miscreants mainly from Ajelogo and Akanimodo were said to have
been used for the attack rather than the wide spread speculation that
OPC members carried out the attack.
The council boss who expressed deep concern over the illegal
acquisition of the market place by touts and hoodlums described the
place as the "robbers den" where no human being except criminals dare
go into.
Barrister Braimoh said, all efforts by both the local and state
governments to eject them proved abortive as they kept coming back.
The Ajelogo and Akanimodo market was said to have been initially
planned to accommodate Kosefe High School, Health Centre and Police
Station by the then Shomolu Local Government before it came to be
under Kosofe Local Government.
According to one Mrs Yetunde Ogunleye, the coordinator of the market
(Mile -12) "the land belongs to the defunct Marketing Board and later
bought over by Kosofe Local Government before being hijacked by these
hoodlums.
The land span then was over 77.5 hectares of land".
The panel later moved to Ajegunle in Ajeromi Ifelodun area from where
the crisis was said to have spread to other areas in the state. It
was led by the council boss Prince Rabiu Adio Oluwa, who took the
panel members first to Achakpo and Alayabiagba where the crisis
recorded its highest casualties.
The case was however not different from that of Mile-12. The clashes
recorded here were also purportedly executed by miscreants who seized
the opportunity to unleash their criminal intent.
The council boss, described Achakpo area as a predominant Hausa
settlement, bedevilled with all kinds of problematic and provocative
lay abouts. He described Ajegunle as a model of Nigeria where you can
find all shades of tribes that make up Nigeria. "We have the Yorubas,
Hausas, Igbos, Efik, Deltalites, Ijaw, Fulani and so on," he said.
Prince Oluwa partly blamed the Police for their slow response to the
crisis and therefore called for a more efficient Police Force, with
adequate men on ground to combat crime.
Other areas also visited were the Lagos State Abattoir and Lairage,
Ayokunnu Bakery and Kampala Way, all in Agege area.
Others included Ajangbadi, Alaba International and PPL Street all in
Ojo area.
The last places visited were Alasatu, Oluwatosin Street and the
notorious Alaka Street, all in Mushin area.
Testifying at one of the sittings of the tribunal, the Commissioner
of Police, Lagos State, Mr. Mike Okiro described the emergence of
militia groups as a child of circumstances brought about by
repressive military regimes and also by the ailing economy of the
country. He said many of these hoodlums are able-bodied men who find
themselves in crimes due to unemployment and lack of proper
upbringing on the part of parents and guardians. He added that "lack
of confidence in the police can be anchored on our degenerating legal
system; it is not because we are inefficient."
Remarkably, the tribunal chairman, local government chairmen and
other petitioners seem to hold similar opinions as to how to check
further crisis apart from identifying similar traits as the causes of
the crises.
According to many of the complaints from petitioners, community
leaders, local chiefs and opinion leaders are always in the centre of
the crises. They are said to be guilty of making inciting statements
that often lead to bloody clashes and also fuel the crises beyond
imagination.
One of such allegations was made against the Seriki Hausawa of
Ajegunle, one Alhaji Watago. He was alleged to have urged his kinsmen
to take up arms against the Yorubas in the area which sparked off the
gruesome killings in October last year.
A cross-section of Lagosians also had opportunity to express their
views on the crises and what should be done to stem the ugly trend.
Apart from calling for a restructuring of the Police, they also said
that the panel would only remain relevant if it focuses on its terms
of reference and not mortgage its integrity. They said the panel's
terms of reference should also include justice and adequate
compensation for victims of the mayhem.
It was also suggested that government must also ensure that the
panel's findings and recommendations must not be swept under the
carpet, as was the case with this similar investigation panels set-up
in the past. Government was also advised not to tamper with the
panel's report before it is made public. If government retrains from
doing this they argued, the public will be encouraged to accept the
panel's findings and recommendations.

Original_Search_Title: Robbers Den Uncovered at Kosofe
Headline: Nigeria: Robbers Den Uncovered at Kosofe
Body_Text:
Lagos — A den of robbers has been discovered at the popular Akanimodo market,
Mile 12 in Kosofe local governments area. Making this disclosure,
chairman of Kosofe local government, Barrister Tunde Braimoh said the
den is located inside the market.
He told the Justice Victor Famakinwa panel of inquiry investigating
civil disturbances in the state when the members visited the local
government area that the market covers over 77.5 hectares of land. He
said the market is supposed to be yielding millions of Naira to both
the state and local government but that it is being inhabited by
people of questionable characters.
The panel was told that some parts of the popular Mile 12 market
especially, some of the uncompleted stalls are being used as
residence by those criminals.
The panel also gathered that the occupants of the sheds neither pay
any form of levy or revenue to the local government nor the state as
they are being shielded by some market leaders.
Mr. Essien Emmanuel Umoren the District police officer of Kosofe
local government told P.M.News in an exclusive chat that the police
is having a tough time flushing out the criminals.
"You see that there are many exits. It is not easy raiding this area
during surveillance," the police boss disclosed. Barrister Buraimoh
prayed tribunal to include the area in its recommendation to the
state government as the place was inherited from the old Shomolu
local government. He said the market contained displaced traders from
Jankara market in central Lagos.
During the visit the panel visited Ajelogo market, Mile 12, Sulewa
yam market and other areas of the local government.

Original_Search_Title: For The Love of Christ
Headline: Nigeria: For The Love of Christ
Body_Text:
Lagos — The aged, the poor, the widows and the orphans in St.
Timothy's Catholic parish, Ojodu, a Lagos suburb, had no reason to
worry about what they would eat or wear on last Christmas day. The
Justice Development and Peace Commission and St. Vincent De Paul
Society took care of their needs.
On December 21, the societies distributed new clothings, live
chickens, rice, yams and toiletries to the needy at the church
premises along Otunba Street after they were sumptuously fed at a
get-together party three days earlier. The party began with a
thanksgiving mass at 6:30 p.m. followed with Christmas carols sang by
various church groups which included the senior and junior choirs,
Catholic Women's Organisation, the singing ministry, the block
rosary, the parish brigade and the Catholic Youths of Nigeria.
The idea of the get-together party was first mooted by Ethelbert
Ifeanyi Iwu, the priest in charge of the parish, early in the year.
It could still have remained a dream but for the generosity of his
friend whom he simply referred to as Mr. V. T. Iwu told Newswatch: "I
have been thinking of how or what to do to help the poor in my parish
until one of my friends came around. He refused to allow me mention
his name but I prefer to call him Mr. V. T. He gave me a large sum of
money and told me to do something for the poor and the widows in my
parish. So it was like a dream come true when he gave me the money."
The Catholic priest said he decided to spend all the money on the
poor in order to give them a sense of belonging during the Christmas
celebration. "I am happy that many people were sponsored to celebrate
the festivities for the love of Christ and they were very happy," he
said.
The parish priest said there would be a better arrangement next year
for the get-together for the poor. Said he: "I didn't even campaign
for it this year until I got the money. By the grace of God, I will
start in time next year. I will go to my friends and some of the
companies that produce the materials that would be given to the poor.
I got the money and bought those things this year. I will get the
materials and meet more people. The poor will have more things to
enjoy from my friends next year."
Apart from the Christmas party, Iwu said the parish was also working
on a poverty alleviation programme. He said the man who gave him
money for the party had earlier donated money which he used to start
a parish clinic. He had, in addition asked him to come up with a
project idea which would help widows in the parish.
"I am still preparing the proposal. Before the middle of next year,
we shall set up something permanent that will benefit the widows. We
have more plans for the widows, the poor, the aged and orphans in the
parish," he assured.

Original_Search_Title: Troop to Receive Christmas Gifts
Headline: South Africa: Troop to Receive Christmas Gifts
Body_Text:
Even though they won't enjoy Christmas in the comfort of their homes with their families, the South African National Defence Force (SANDF) members deployed in Burundi and the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) will definitely welcome the special treat coming their way.
They will be presented with Christmas gifts collectively worth R31 500 on Dec. 27.
The SANDF contributed R27 500 towards the cost of the 900 gifts and Spar Supermarket sponsored the balance.
The gifts consist of two packs of biscuits, potato fries, fruit cocktail, two packs of sweets, biltong, a sports drink and two packs of peanuts.
Speaking in Thaba Tshwane near Pretoria today, SANDF Chief Siphiwe Nyanda said the aim of sending the gifts was to illustrate that 'the top structure' cared for them.
'We want to take this opportunity to hand over a small token of our appreciation to the deployed soldiers,' he said.
About 700 South African soldiers are deployed in Burundi's capital of Bujumbura to protect returning exiled Hutu leaders, while another 100 troops are in the DRC as part of the United Nation (UN) peacekeeping mission.
Last month defence minister Mosiuoa Lekota visited the troops in both countries and told them that their sacrifices would be recorded in South African history like those of freedom fighters such as former president Nelson Mandela.
'You are walking on the steps of great leaders of the country by making sacrifices that will benefit many men, women and children you do not even know,' he said.

Original_Search_Title: Gwazantini May Be Remanded Too Long
Headline: Malawi: Gwazantini May Be Remanded Too Long
Body_Text:
Blantyre — Chief State Advocate has not ruled out a possibility that Alice
Gwazantini, on allegations that she scalded her maid to death, will
remain in remand "too long" saying investigations take "too long"
sometimes.
Mc Lean Kamwambe said some remandees have had to wait for over nine
years to have their homicide cases heard. "She [Mrs Alice Gwazantini]
is only been in prison for a couple of months [Since August this
year]," he said.
But Civil Liberties Committee Executive Director Emmie Chanika
yesterday said Gwazantini is there "accidentally."
"It's not a thing she planned for and there is no need to say that
she can stay too long. They should speed up their investigations as
soon as possible to protect her rights" she said.
Investigations into the case in which the Namiwawa-based woman, who
allegedly doused hot water on 13-year-old Ntchayi Jakisoni after she
allegedly ate her boss's sweet potato, is progressing at a snail's
pace following the transfer of a police officer who was handling the
case.
The Chief State Advocate said that the case will only resume after
another police officer has been identified.
"We do not want to do our investigations in a hurry. We want this
case to be properly investigated," he said.
Kamwambe said the state had failed to immediately appoint another
person to handle the case.
Chanika, however, failed to make out why the policeman handling a
sensitive story could be transfered just like this.
Henry Phoya, lawyer for Gwazantini refused to comment arguing the
matter is in court.
Gwazantini, who was arrested mid-August this year was denied bail by
Justice Frank Kapanda on the grounds that her offence was not
bailable.

Original_Search_Title: Kamba Elders to Discuss Ngilu
Headline: Kenya: Kamba Elders to Discuss Ngilu
Body_Text:
Kamba leaders led by the Machakos Kanu branch chairman Mr Mulu Mutisya yesterday said that the community will meet to discuss Kaluki Ngilu for abusing the community by wielding quiver full of arrows, a bows and arrow in presence of men.
Mr Mutisya accused those who conducted the ceremony of making her a Kamba elder a blunder of giving a woman bows and arrows instead of a lesso and basket full of either cassava or sweet potatoes the befitting a woman.
The leaders who included ministers, MPs, councillors and other elected leaders from the four Kamba districts said that the Kitui Central MP will be fined several bulls and goats to cleanse the Kamba community for breaking traditions by wielding a bows and arrows in public.
On politics, the leaders accused the legislator of taking the Kamba community for a ride for along time by shifting them from one party to another for her own selfish games.
Mr Mutisya told journalists that the MP's new National Party of Kenya is a non-starter in Ukambani and told her to officially defect from Social Democratic Party of Kenya to a newly formed political party.
Councillor Peter Mwanthi of SDP said that priorities of the Kamba community are provision of clean piped water, well equipped medical institutions, tarmac roads and other essential facilities and not formation of new political parties without agenda to tell wananchi.
He challenged Ngilu to immediately defect to her new party to pave way for a by-election in her Kitui Central constituency and told her to stop her threats to those leaders opposed to her leadership.

Original_Search_Title: Claude's Worcester Source
Headline: South Africa: Claude's Worcester Source
Body_Text:
Dignitaries in the Boland town gathered to celebrate their first  cricket
'Springbok'
The number of buttons on a jacket can sometimes tell you where a man is from. When 150 burghers of Worcester gathered last week to celebrate  Claude
Henderson's selection to the national side, it was clear the guest  speaker in the hip, black four-button suit was not a local. Henderson's Western
Province captain HD Ackerman had made the trip "over the mountain".
Ackerman started well by reminding the audience that it was an old
Worcesterite who had decided on green jerseys for the national rugby  team.
The United Cricket Board is anxious to rebrand the cricket team as  something other than Proteas. In Worcester the flower hasn't caught on anyway:  for the locals, Henderson is their first cricket "Springbok".
The Lion's Club was the venue for the tribute dinner and with a cold  wind nipping in like a surprise arm-ball off the Du Toit mountains, it would  not have been surprising to see some empty seats. Henderson was expecting  to meet a few mates in the pub Ã± instead there were 18 tables, a full  house, flower arrangements, lashings of food and the best wines of the region.  With the nearby KWV Cellar being the biggest in the world, there was no  shortage of warming brandy either.
Parked closest to the hall were the diesel bakkies that delivered the  tender meats, salads, roast potatoes, sweet potatoes, rice and hot pudding  that made up the feast. In an impressive example of platteland organisation,  it took just three days to put the event together. Organiser Danie Nel  said every sponsor approached had come on board.
The idea for the dinner sprang from what must be the least successful betting group in the country. Nel and Claude's father, "Mossie"  Henderson, are founder members of the Rugby Raai Klub, started during the 1995  World
Cup. In six years no one has won the now massive pot but the club's  rules are peculiar and very particular: the score-line has to be exactly  correct and no one is allowed to bet against the Springboks!
There was lots of hilarity as the party was reminded of the unusual  domestic chore pattern in the Henderson household. Mossie chalked the lines to  make the garden look like Augusta for golf competitions and Lord's for the cricket season but it was mother Susan who cut the lawn to the correct specifications.
Although the evening was organised as a tribute to Henderson the effect  was to celebrate his parents, the town and its cricketers. Worcester was  joint host with Wellington of the first South African Country Week in 1954  and cricket on the platteland is still strong. There are at least 7Â000  country cricketers travelling vast distances on dusty roads all over South  Africa every week. The cricketers of Willowmore in the Eastern Cape were until recently making a 360km round trip to Graaff-Reinet to play home games.
Henderson's first chance in national colours came in 1989 when he was selected for the South African Country Districts (SACD) under-17 team  that toured England under the guidance of Anton Ferreira.
Lance Klusener averaged 85 for Natal Country Districts at the 1993 tournament and was part of the SACD team that beat host province  Western
Transvaal. Tertius Bosch, Fanie de Villiers and Makhaya Ntini are all examples of platteland talent.
Back in Worcester, cricket is largely Afrikaans Ã± like the town  itself. To
Mossie went the greatest tribute as the person who had taught his son  "die liefde vir die game (the love of the game)". This rubbed off on all the
Henderson boys. Oldest brother Kiffie played for the Boland Kavaliers  in his day and younger brother James is in Scotland as a pro after averaging  40 for
Boland in the Supersport Series last season. The active cricketers  share a house halfway between their rival provinces in Gordon's Bay.
Claude and James are not common names for Afrikaans-speaking boys but  then
Mossie's real name is Henry and he was an English-speaker before he  fell for
Susan and moved to Worcester. Henderson Snr did some research on his
Scottish roots last year when his sons flew him to Britain for a  holiday and now that he's retired he may get around to visiting the national  archives to find out more about the family lll history.
Ackerman's instinctslll about spin paid dividends last season: 400  overs from Henderson produced 36 wickets and Paul Adams took 18 wickets in  200 overs before he left for the West Indies. Only three (pace) bowlers  took more wickets. With coaches Vincent Barnes and Eric Simons encouraging  him to
"be brave", and with a supportive captain, Henderson's spin showed more  bite and bounce than ever before. Control he has always had.
Henderson's selection is proof that country boys can make it. He's had  to work very hard and at 29 he's earned his stripes as a professional.  This platteland cricketer has also played in the Birmingham, Derbyshire and
Lancashire leagues.
When Henderson steps on to the field for South Africa he will be aiming  to
"do what I've always done". In that, he is like the place that formed  him.
They don't take kindly to pretension in the Breede River Valley.
Even the wine estates keep it simple: at the dinner we drank Du Toit's
Kloof, Dassies Rood and Botha Kelders. Like the mature brandy of the  nearby
KWVCellar, Claude Henderson the cricketer is now ready for the  international market.
l Officially, Henderson is the second player from Worcester to make the national team. Eddie Fuller, who played in the 1950s, was born in  Worcester but went to school and played his club cricket in Cape Town.

Original_Search_Title: Braai Aces Cook Up a Hot Contest in City
Headline: South Africa: Braai Aces Cook Up a Hot Contest in City
Body_Text:
Cape Town — Forget the Boks and Bafana Bafana - Cape Town is to host a sizzling
contest that will really separate the men from the boys - an
international braai competition.
The Amabraaie Braaie will pit their barbecue skills against the best
in the world at the third annual World Barbecue Cook-off, to be held
from November 2 to 4 at Century City in Cape Town.
Teams from as far afield as Moscow and Texas will compete.
According to the SA Barbecue Association, an affiliate of the World
Barbecue Association, there are 6.5 million barbecue enthusiasts
worldwide.
The association's motto is: "Making people happy by fire, food and
fun is a way of saying: Let's barbecue and make friends around the
world."
The organiser, Lannice Snyman, said she was delighted with the
response from international teams so far.
"In addition to top South African braaiers, we expect superb
representation from all over the world.
In all, 50 teams will be competing and entries have been received
from the UK, the US, Belgium, the Netherlands, Reunion, Switzerland,
Austria, Germany, Russia and Japan."
Other attractions at the event will include a barbecue and outdoor
living expo.
The first annual world championship was held in Switzerland in 1999
and the second in Nashville, Tennessee, where the event attracted 40
000 visitors a day.
All competitors were required to use their braaiing expertise in five
categories: chicken, fish, beef/ostrich, pork/lamb, and dessert.
Snyman said Tennessee saw the Amabraaier Braaiers create a string of
South African dishes which delighted the judges, such as smoked fish
with bacon-wrapped figs, which earned them fourth place in the fish
category, Cape Malay spice rubbed beef fillet with African peanut
samp and beans, and an African-inspired dessert: sweet potato
fritters with amarula cream.
Billy Martin, brand manager of Chateau Libertas, which is sponsoring
the Cape Town event, said braaiing in South Africa had been born of a
rich and varied past in the many diverse cultures that exist here.
"It has been practised by South Africans for more than a thousand
years," he said.
The SA Barbecue Association has invited all barbecue lovers who would
like to enter the competition to contact Tamsin Snyman or Lannice
Snyman for information on the championships on 790-3367 or fax
790-1055.
l Fire in the Soul, a documentary film exploring the deeper stories
of the food, people and locations of barbecue, is currently being
filmed by an American/Canadian crew for worldwide distribution.
Filming began at last year's event and will continue at the world
championships in South Africa.

Original_Search_Title: Exporters Eye Chinese Market
Headline: Uganda: Exporters Eye Chinese Market
Body_Text:
The Uganda Export Promotion Board (UEPB) has drawn up a programme to
help local exporters penetrate the Chinese market, acting executive
director William Omongole has said.
The official said since the Chinese market is one of the biggest in
the world, a substantial share of it can turn around Uganda's
economy. "Now that we have seen a slowdown in Uganda's economic
growth in the recent past, its only the export market that can help
us escape a full blown up recession," Omongole told The Monitor,
August 2.
He said UEPB will later this month officially launch a comprehensive
programme involving embassies of the two countries, to encourage
joint ventures, exports and imports between them.
UEPB has singled out non-traditional commodities for promotion,
including beans, honey, bananas, handicrafts, sweet potatoes among
others.
Uganda's ambassador to China, Phillip Idro was in the country last
month, Meeting local businessmen and holding workshops on trade
opportunities in China.

Original_Search_Title: Rural Bank Makes Handsome Net Profit
Headline: Ghana: Rural Bank Makes Handsome Net Profit
Body_Text:
The Akuapem Rural bank has made a net profit of 632.4 million cedis in its operations last year as compared to 216.2 million cedis in 1999, representing 192.5 per cent.
Total income of the bank increased by 80 per cent from 621.8 million cedis to 1.1 billion cedis during the same period.
The Chairperson of the Board of Directors of the bank, Dr (Mrs) Gloria Nikoi, who made this known at the 20th  annual general meeting of the shareholders at Mamfe-Akuapem on Saturday said, total deposit increased from  two billion cedis to 3.6 billion cedis.
She said the bank granted loans and advances totalling one billion cedis last year, as against 852 million cedis the previous year.
Mrs. Nikoi  commended the board and staff for their dedication, discipline, commitment and diligence, which enabled the bank to achieve the feat.  Mr Joseph . B Clottey, Acting Head, Banking Supervision Department, Bank of Ghana (BOG), said, the bank is committed tosupporting the steady growth and development of an efficient rural banking system, capable of harnessing resources in the rural areas for rapid, sustainable socio-economic development.
He said the BOG would also continue to exercise its supervisory role strictly over rural banks and provide the required assistance for them to achieve improved performance.
Mr. Clottey urged rural banks to adopt measures to strengthen the credit management procedures and processes to achieve good loan portfolio quality while enhancing profitability.
According to him, it has been observed that appropriate appraisal of credits had been generally unsatisfactory, resulting in poor recovery  and ultimately losses.
He commended the bank for maintaining stable operations and  steady growth over the years.
Mr. Clottey noted that the general performance of rural banks in the country has recorded impressive developments of late despite the unfavourable micro-economic conditions. He said satisfactory rural banks, which numbered 53 at the end of March last year, increased to 64 at the end of March this year.
The Akuapem North District Chief Executive, Dr (Mrs) Eugenia Quist expressed appreciation to the bank for its Women-ln-Development programme aimed at assisting women to undertake income generating ventures.
She called on the people in the area to undertake viable projects such as sunflower, pawpaw, sweet potato and sweet pepper production to supplement their earnings.                            GNA

Original_Search_Title: Sabiiti Talks of More
Headline: Uganda: Sabiiti Talks of More
Body_Text:
Denis Sabiiti, the parliamentary candidate for Rubanda West constituency in
Kabale district, has promised to intensify the growing of clonal coffee to
boost the people's income.
He also pledged to introduce credit schemes in the parishes.
Sabiiti, who addressed the people of Kashaasha, Namilyango and Butanda
parishes
in Bufundi sub-county last week, urged the residents to grow irish and sweet
potatoes, tea, coffee and peas for external markets.
He pledged to avail sorghum milling machines besides the maize mills he
donated
recently.
Sabiiti also promised to lobby the Government to avail funds to reconstruct
the
main Kashasha-Muko-Kabale-Bufundi marram road.
Other candidates running for the Rubanda West parliamentary seat are Dr. Ezra
Suruma and Henry Banyenzaki.

Original_Search_Title: Solar Eclipse: A Sight to Behold
Headline: Zambia: Solar Eclipse: A Sight to Behold
Body_Text:
Lusaka — For most people who saw it-the June 21 total solar eclipse- was a
mesmerising spectacle, a sight to behold.  It was a sight that left an
indelible mark on their lives. A view most had not envisaged and when it did
happen, it created a feeling of awe and wonder at this spectacular sight
beyond human making. It was indeed of God.  Except for a few widely
travelled tourists, the total solar eclipse experienced on Thursday was the
first for many in Zambia, and what an exhilarating moment it was-the first
total solar eclipse in the new millennium.
"Have you ever experienced the night at 15:00 hours," I heard someone
close to where I stood ask his daughter, his voice filled with
excitement. It was really night time during the day.
Having driven all the way from Ndola to Chisamba to view the eclipse,
this author couldn't help but notice the number of vehicles on the road.
The Kapiri-Kabwe road had never seen such an influx of traffic in a day- all
heading southwards. The last time I saw so many cars dashing down to Lusaka
was when the Zambian national soccer team was at its peak and they were
having an international game. Then, soccer fans from the Copperbelt would
begin their journey 'early bells' to watch their stars.
But that was a long time ago! This time however, there was no question
about the destination of the vehicles. Just as well the day was declared a
national holiday.  And for the first time, I had never seen so many people,
especially Zambians, take an interest in an event such as this one. School
children (at least those whose teachers were not on strike) had been told
about the total eclipse and how to and not view it.
It was interesting to hear a four-year-old warn me about looking at the
sun directly because I would go blind! At least most of the children in
urban areas, and I want to believe in some rural areas, had been schooled on
this natural phenomenon that was about to engulf Zambia.
Kapiri-Mposhi service station on the way to Lusaka was agog with a large
number of travellers. The whole place was milling with people and women of
different shades thronged the fee paying lavatory. It was an indication of
the anticipation that had gripped most people. A colleague observed that
although the path of the eclipse was somewhere from Zambezi through Lusaka,
the event had created a business boom for people along the line of rail as
scores of people heading to Lusaka and other areas were making stopovers for
drinks and other services.
In fact, this author observed that most traders selling pumpkins, sweet
potatoes and other merchandise along the road had upped their prices.  And
the police, bless them, were at their best. There were no requests for
triangles and the many questions associated with them.  Their job was to
simply wave the cars to pass, or they would have created traffic jams.  This
author asked a police officer at a road block on the number of vehicles that
had passed. Never before had he seen so many cars in a day head towards the
capital.
Just after Kabwe, this author noticed several people had 'set up camp'
wherever they found a lay-by, in readiness for the big event.  I wondered
why some people were making noise about some lodges not allowing them to
enter and view the eclipse from there, when all they had to do was park a
vehicle and wait for the moon to block the sun from the earth's
inhabitants.
Several tourists, and I mean both local and international, were dotted
along the road to Lusaka waiting patiently.  At Banani where this author
viewed the eclipse, the mood was euphoric and some people were actually
picnicking and roasting meat on braai stands.  There was total anticipation
as the moon inched slowly towards the sun.  Suddenly, the shadows started to
show as they grew longer and bigger.  The moon was eating into the sun
making it look a half moon, then like a banana, then a thin arched
line.
It was indeed getting dark. One would have though it was 18.00 hours.
The evening butterflies were beginning to come out and the birds were going
to roost. How confusing it must have been for the insects and birds.
Then slowly, but surely, it had happened! It was 'night time' for just
over three minutes. And there in full view for all to watch without the
eclipse glasses, was the sun completely covered by the moon. It was only the
sun's corona or halo that was visible. It was a spectacular sight! The stars
were quite visible and someone noted those could be the planets like venus
which are close to the sun.  And in a frenzy, motorists blew their car horns
in a chorus while other people screamed 'it has happened', as though to
convince some critics who may have doubted.
"It was a beautiful moment and we gave God the glory because that is
only possible with God," a friend, Wendy Njovu remarked.
"It was quite an intoxicating moment for me. That was not a man-made
event, but what we say icilengwa Lesa (God made)," Nathan Zulu, a Kitwe
resident who had travelled all the way to Chisamba said.
In other parts of Africa, Angola, Zimbabwe and Mozambique thousands also
viewed the eclipse. On the island of Madagascar also witnessed the moon
cover the sun.  The event was not just exciting for those who experienced
the total eclipse. Even those in areas of the partial eclipse talked of the
excitement they felt when suddenly the afternoon looked like evening.  Some
even talked of a drop in temperature while a caller to ZNBC's live coverage
of the eclipse claimed to have a slight headache.
Psychological? We'll never know but there were different observations
made.  Scientists of course, had their own explanation of the eclipse, but
for the Christians, this was indeed, the work of the Almighty God. It
reminds one of the song "I sing the mighty power of God" for indeed it was
the might of the Most High at work.
It was one of nature's wonders that will leave a lasting impression on
the minds of those who saw this once-in-a-lifetime spectacle.

Original_Search_Title: From Cape to Cuba
Headline: South Africa: From Cape to Cuba
Body_Text:
Kalk Bay, with its air of shabby English chic, is at first glance an un-
likely place to find a restaurant redolent with the Spanish-African sounds
and symbols of Cuba. From Christ to Che Guevara, the walls of the Cape to
Cuba restaurant are covered with genuine icons and artefacts that reflect
the cultural and political history of the largest and most beautiful island
in the Caribbean.
And yet the strip of land between Kalk Bay's Main Road and the harbour is
rich with the smells and sights of a fishing village that probably has much
in common with towns along the Cuban coastline. Fishing boats unload their
catches of sleek, silvery snoek in the harbour. Rows of tarpaulin-covered
bakkies groan under loads of fresh fish that find their way to the street
markets of Mitchells Plain and countless households on the Cape Flats. This
is a people's place, and the lined, sunburnt faces of the fishermen and
vendors contrast with the smooth, tanned faces of the tourists who trawl the
art galleries, antique shops and restaurants along Main Road, below the
genteel homes that cling to the steep mountainside. Shades of both Battista
and Castro -- local reminders of the eternal divide between those who have
and those who do not.
Bart van Vuuren has long had a love affair with Cuba, so when the goods shed
of the Kalk Bay station was offered for sale last year, he and his wife
Deona set about transforming the ruin into a
restaurant-cum-cocktail-and-cigar bar where Cuban furniture and decor are
all for sale. On a trip to Cuba last year he filled a container with
"non-national heritage furniture, icons, cigars, black beans, sandals and
T-shirts" -- and you can pick them up here, after the Cuban bread-and-fruit
mayonnaise starter, but before the rum tartlet.
Strangely, it's not at all disconcerting to sip a chicken-and-lime soup with
avocado (R14) under a tableau of two revolutionary martyrs, Christ and Che,
whose images adorn the roughly plastered walls. Smudged orange and washed
cobalt blue complement each other in the main restaurant, where carved
Spanish colonial furniture competes for space with mismatched wooden, wicker
and plastic chairs and rough wooden tables. For those whose bank balances
can take it, there's a magnificent Cuban colonial cabinet for R39 000.
What do you order when the black beans run out, as they had when we dined
there? Try the Aves de Cubano -- chicken breasts in a fragrant blend of curry
and spices, served with rice and vegetables that include sweet potato in
pastry tubes topped with fresh basil (R39). Or a Hemmingway (sic) salad of
fresh Cape salmon brushed with pesto (R34) that tastes of the sea outside
and exotic places beyond. Nothing prepared us for the chilli ice dessert,
which is a parable for the way to perdition with its blend of cream and
hellfire. To help one along the path of destruction, wines range from a
rather bland Capells Court Chardonnay 2000 (R39) to Meerlust Merlot (R150).
Guests are welcome to dance to the sensual music of Cesaria Evoras CafÃ©
Atlantico or Los Guaracheros de Orientes Grandes Exitos under a collection
