of fabulous European chandeliers and ornate lamps.
Cape to Cuba has been fully booked every night since opening in December,
catering mainly to locals who flock from Clifton and Camps Bay for the kick
of the Cape to Cuba cocktail and any one of 19 varieties of Cuban cigars --
from a Romeo y Juliet to a Cohiba Corona. Guests laze on the Hemingway deck
under Papa's gaze from a photo on the wall and watch the last of the
graffiti-covered Metro trains whizz by -- colourful interruptions to the view
of the harbour and the expanse of False Bay, and the magical Hottentots
Holland mountains on the horizon.
Cape to Cuba: Restaurant, cocktail bar, cigars, Cuban furniture and
artefacts is in Main Road, Kalk Bay. Tel: (021) 788 1566; Fax: (021) 788
3695

Original_Search_Title: MK Veteran: The ANC Should Clear My Name
Headline: South Africa: MK Veteran: The ANC Should Clear My Name
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Johannes van Wyk clutches his crutch firmly for support each time he
rises from a chair as he goes about his household chores. Certified
disabled, he has lost the use of his left leg.
His cheekbone is also shattered. His scars bear testimony to his
three-year incarceration in African National Congress detention camps
in Zambia, Angola and Tanzania.
Although he received R4 530 as interim reparation from the Truth and
Reconciliation Commission in 1999 for that ordeal, Van Wyk is bitter
that he was never called to put his side of the story before the
commission.
At the Quatro detention camp he claims he was shot in the hip and
stomach in a bid by his torturers to extract a confession that he was
responsible for the assassination of anti-apartheid activist Dr
Fabian Ribeiro and his wife, Florence, in 1986.
Van Wyk cuts a battered figure, looking older than his 44 years. He
is unemployed; he lives on a R700 monthly pension as a former
Umkhonto weSizwe (MK) soldier, sharing a two-roomed mud house with
his Tanzanian wife and three children in a settlement outside
Kwaggafontein in Mpumalanaga.
Otherwise, his wife tends a vegetable garden, growing sweet potatoes
and pumpkins to provide food for the family.
An ANC and South African Communist Party member, Van Wyk says, "Now
that someone has confessed to the killing, the ANC should clear my
name."
Van Wyk is among the 9 771 members of MK and the Azanian People's
Liberation Army who did not join the South African National Defence
Force (SANDF).
These members will not benefit from the government's recently
announced R500- million pension plan for former MK veterans. The
pension plan " the details of which are yet to be released " will
benefit only those former guerrillas who entered into an employment
contract with the Department of Defence.
An angry Thandi Modise, chair of the parliamentary defence portfolio
committee, says she has asked the question: "What happens to those
who were injured, disabled and did not join the SANDF " She says Van
Wyk is fortunate to be in a position to receive at least R700 a
month. "There are others who receive nothing."
The defence department said the authority to redress the grievances
of those veterans who did not join the SANDF falls outside its
jurisdiction. The Mail & Guardian was advised to approach the MK
Veterans' Association (MKVA). The MKVA national chair Deacon Mathe
said the organisation runs projects which address the grievances of
all the MK veterans. He claimed they will be initiating projects
which will reach out to those who have not been covered, but was not
forthcoming with details.
A former Mamelodi resident, Van Wyk volunteered to work for MK in
Zambia in 1988 under the code name Bongi Zembe.
He says within three months of his arrival in Zambia he was accused
of being an apartheid government agent and sent first to the SRC
prison, and later Quatro in Angola, where he claims he was tortured.
"My Afrikaner surname caused suspicion," he adds, after he was
adopted by one of his aunts, who was married to a coloured man.
He claims he was released after a short stint at yet another prison
in Tanzania and made his way back to South Africa with his Tanzanian
wife Elizabeth in 1992.
"I don't want anything for myself " I just want to give some
opportunities to my children" " aged 18, seven and three years. He
indicates torn clothing piled on top of a heap of broken furniture,
and school shoes without laces placed in the corner. "That is their
wardrobe," he says.
Van Wyk says he has written several letters to President Thabo
Mbeki's office. In one of these letters he poignantly notes: "... I
still love the river [the ANC] that nearly took my life ..." All he
wants is someone to clear his name.

Original_Search_Title: 10 Years of RUF War: Progress Or Self-destruction?
Headline: Sierra Leone: 10 Years of RUF War: Progress Or Self-destruction?
Body_Text:
Ottawa, Canada — A country, like an individual, has a soul, has metaphysics, and has
something invisible that drives it-to what? March 23 marked 10 years of RUF
war in Sierra Leone. For progress or self-destruction, one may ask.
But sometimes, this does not happen in a straight line: the struggle for
progress, or self-destruction, comes in a painfully protracted fashion. The
reasons for such distractions toward progress may be because of either lack
of clear-cut vision or weak inner values and attitudes or history and
culture not informing goals. With such lack of balance the country swings
from one end of the developmental pendulum to another in a confused state of
animation.
In a much broader and deeper reflection, the 71, 620 square kilometers
area, shaped like human heart, that came to be called Sierra Leone by the
Portuguese explorer, Pedro da Cinta in 1462, or the mountains of what is now
Freetown peninsula, Lion Mountains. In some metaphysical twist, for the past
30 years the lion has been asleep, unconcerned by the stupidity, moral
decay, and senselessness wheeling around it. Then 10 years ago, the lion was
awakened, haven't taken the bullshit for too long. The result: a massive
scattershot and scatter brain of the entire Sierra Leonean system, reeling
from one senselessness to another, its spiritual bearing spinning in mid
air. The Revolutionary United Front, RUF, (pictured here) rolling out a
campaign, precisely from the eastern border area of Bomaru on March 23, 1991
to 'clean' the contaminated country, and in the interim, other 'cleaners'
such as the romantic West Side Boys, an offshoot of Johnny Paul Koroma's
junta Armed Forces Provisional Council, another presumed 'cleaner,' roaming
the country's outback.
But the RUF could not handle the forces of disorder it purported to
contain skillfully, and the result has been floodgate of actual evil,
confusion, arrogance, and general senselessness. And so instead of the civil
war and its on-again, off-again apparent slumber garnering Sierra Leoneans
for national unity and the ensuing reconstruction, the country is as much
more paralysed today than ever; its institutions weak, its capacity building
farfetched, its 13 ethnic groups much more disunited, and its psyche
terribly disordered. In a replay of the slave trade era, unknown to Sierra
Leoneans, even including its arrogantly self-centred elite, who really do
not understand the country, massive raids are the norms, arson is the daily
plasas (a vegetable stew made from either cassava or potato leaves),
lootings of diamonds are na God mark am, and mass movement of the civil
war's refugees, some 250,000, from one corridor to another a reminder of the
slave trade era, when during the later part of the 18th century the Freetown
port was one of the major slave trading operations along the West African
coast.
Ten and running, the country is not only intellectually divided, but
physically too. The RUF, enjoying the loot of diamonds, which the
heartlessly deceitful politicians have enjoyed 30 years ago, when Sierra
Leone became independent on 27 April 1961, are now much more entrapped in
the diamonds than any Chinese-type Cultural Revolution to rid the country of
stupidity, superstition, normlessness, moral decay, chronic confusion, and
disorder. The sweetness of the pap (a Sierra Leonean pudding) is in the
eating. Haven't eaten the 'diamond pap,' the RUF government, as they say in
the half of the area of the country under their control, is currently
playing chess game with peace, juggling diamonds in the air and using its
'magic' to charm the country. In the RUF's 10 years of senseless mayhem
everybody is diamond digger, wheeler, and dealer.
Two hundred and fourteen years ago, that's in 1787, Freetown and its
surrounding areas became a British colony. Today, thanks to the RUF, and
more appropriately, the Sierra Leonean rotten elite, the British are back in
some sort of re-colonization zeal: coming to teach these 'savages'
civilization. This is today the existential game in Sierra Leone, where its
police chief is British, where its army chief swings between a Nigerian to
Kenyan to British to Indian. Where the British, are managing its seaport
where its soldiers are being trained by the British, where its key advisers
are British, where its election matters are being sort out by the British,
where the British are the buffer against any eventual RUF second invasion of
the main prize, Freetown.
Foday Sankoh, the founder and former leader of the RUF, who is currently
in an undisclosed detention, reflects the awakened angry lion. And he is
variously seen as so. There may be 'General' Issa Sesey as the current
leader of the RUF but Sankoh remains the main spiritual vizier, oga, of the
RUF. The brutally well organized RUF reflects the dictum that deep down the
wailing and the mayhem of the country, Sierra Leone can emerge as a well
organized and productive society in the modern sense: its institutions
functioning normally, its capacity building assured, and its spiritual
affairs informed by its core cultural, ethical-moral regulations and
laws. If the RUF could organize such healthy campaigns, then the entire
Sierra Leone could, given the right elements and climate. But the RUF, come
to think of its evil deeds, have thrown overboard the moral mission it first
rolled out in the bush.
Either the RUF or the now defunct West Side Boys or the now dead Johnny
Paul Koroma's AFRC activities over the past 10 years is nothing new in the
Sierra Leonean scheme of things. One hundred and ninety-two years ago,
that's in is1808, when Sierra Leone was recognized as a British colony, when
ex-slaves from diverse ethnic groups and national backgrounds, developed an
English-speaking Creole culture unique in Africa, the natives of Sierra
Leone staged numerous rebellions against the British and the Creole elites,
resentment that focused on the tax system and on the privileges of the
Creole. Today, as refracted in the RUF and other marginal rebel groups, the
rebellion is largely between the 'country' and Freetown, between the rural
and the urban, between the fat cats in Freetown and the poor, slum rats in
not only the rural folks but also those in the urban areas.
The boiling of Sierra Leone is like the preparation of famed Sierra
Leonean pepper soup. The RUF, the SLPP, the APC, the NDP, Siaka Stevens, the
Margais, Valentine Strasser, the Maada Bio, the Johnny Paul Koroma, the dead
West Side Boys, the collapsed AFRC, the corruption, the chronic lies, the
disturbing deceit, the hatred, the psychic disorder, and the moral decay are
all ingredients boiling in the Sierra Leonean pot in the preparation of the
pepper soup. Later, much more later, the soup would be ready in the Sierra
Leonean development game.
In 1984 and 1987 Sierra Leonean students and public employees rioted
against the much hated Siaka Stevens' All People Congress, APC, government
1978 imposed one-party system. Out of this came the restoration of
multiparty system of the both Milton Magai and Albert Margai which had
existed before. Will the ten years of RUF campaigns metamorphosis, in the
years to come, into sound multiparty democracy, accountability, and rule of
law, freedom, equity, dignity, and fairness? I hope so in the pepper soup
preparation illustration, but it will not come easily.

Original_Search_Title: The Raging, Rising Toll On Our Highways
Headline: Nigeria: The Raging, Rising Toll On Our Highways
Body_Text:
Lagos — The 15 postgraduate students' death on Jebba - Mokwa highway on
December 7, 2000 epitomised that structure. They thus became 15 of
the obituary statistics out of a projected 8,000 in the red year
2000. And the tragedy continues unabated. Statistics is statistics.
Death is death. (See Nigeria's Table of Tragedy Scoreboard 1955-1998.
Regardless of our philosophically oriented feeling on the obituary
reports, and for some they run very deep, the overriding issue and
the most insidious evil in the overall evil of this said highway
crash is the spreading expectation that highway genocide will become
permanent and will continue to plague the nation without regard to
age, class, gender, creed, residence race... a philosophy of highway
accidents in Nigeria that's dangerously self-fulfilling.
With that epilogue, we come to the heart of what concerns us today
and I know of no better way to spell it out than to recall that
everyone now is eager to participate in the safety dialogue
especially after the recent sensational episodes at Ife-Road Toll
gate, consuming over 100 lives; the Ovia River tragedy on Benin-Ore
Road liquidating about 75 lives; the Carter Bridge Molue plunge
plucking 14 lives and of course the Jebba- Mokwa slaughter. But
unfortunately, there is a curious reluctance to face up and spearhead
the national agitation to reduce highway accidents. it is
paradoxical.
The cold blooded fact is that wailing, sighing and condolence will
not bring back dead people, neither would publishing the totals of
motoring deaths and injuries, nor public or gubernatorial
denunciations, jar the Nigerian motorists into a realisation of the
appalling risks of motoring on our highways. This is because we do
not translate the dry statistics into reality of blood agony. This is
the real antithesis of our highway annual traffic data.
The accident figures in university Ibadan tragedy for instance
exclude the pain, the horror of savage mutilation, the portrait of a
hysterical woman with her screaming mouth opening without a word; the
raw ends of bones protruding through flesh in compound fractures; the
frail and unanswered prayers of the dying students; the lady's corpse
still clutching her handbag on her laps as she has clutched it when
she felt the vehicle leaves the road; the trousers without legs, the
eyes on the back seat that popped out violently without stain, the
body that just lay lifeless without a scratch; the butchered pieces
of human flesh flying about like UFO; the brains scrapping out from
the skulls looking like mashed potatoes; the different shoes with no
feet in them the floor of the vehicle smeared with the crimson human
blood, faeces and urine forming a dry lake but slippery red; the
cracking of bones and joints that came apart like thick soft balloons
stretched too far, the two trousers and a blouse seemed entailed
under the wrought iron of the body of the vehicle, the blood leaping
out of mouths, ears, and noses, the head smashing into the
windscreen; the driver pinned against the steering shaft; and the
once shinning and imposing king of the road (the vehicle) becoming a
shabby paintless scrap. All in 10 seconds.
All these illustrated gruesome variable need to be brought closer
home to the Nigerian people who, over the years, have been accepting
fatalistically the inevitable prices to pay for the freedom of
romance with the automobile, so that we can truly appreciate the
latitudes, dimensions and magnitude of the highway trauma as a
national "life and death" problem.
That takes us back to the Ibadan tragedy scene where this pertinent
hypothesis could be tested.
They heaved. They sighed. They wailed. But the 15 postgrads would not
burge. "A stiff is a stiff, only the living heard you" said a Police
Sergeant at the morgue trying to get a breath of breathable air. "But
this is too much. Too much for anyone. Fifteen grads hacked down by a
technological monster under 5 minutes, wailed Sisi Debola.
I did not heave, I did not sigh, I did not wail. Not as a result of a
masticatory act to promote an image of professional insensitivity
toward the diseased (who were academicians like me) but for the
incomprehension of the homicide. I was just thinking, looking,
gaping. "Stop thinking Doc" says Sis Debola, "How do you stop
thinking," I murmured to myself but surmoned the face gesture to
correct her accident reconstruction timing of 5 minutes (as 10
seconds) before I opened up.
Sisi Debola, looked at that mashed skull of a student with his brains
nearly scrapped out so roughly one couldn't tell the frontal
cere-corticopontal tract from the sensory tract. And down below were
raw ends of bones protruding through flesh in compound fractures.
Look at the other guy with a complete separation of the brain matter
from the immediate organs below in such a way that the eye, the
internal ear, organs of balance, hearing and identification known by
the medicos as passingpoint would have resulted if he had been alive.
Take a close look at that lady. her body territorial integrity had
been abused. Look at the blind grad who had probably dived through
windows without superficial scratches and his guide-stick unbroken -
that's one of those examples where accident was only exercising its
prerogative of being erratic.
Sisi Debola cut in and said: "Doc, this was too much." What was much?
I snapped back, Remember each shattered man or woman who went to make
up the fatality statistics to be chalked up next year as 2000
statistics had to die personal death like them.
But then, death was death and raw death was raw death. Death that
does not allow one to say good-bye to the family or good night to his
roommate. Death that lets loose blood gurgle and choke the throat.
Death that screws out brains from skulls. That's death from
automobile.
But to me this death stepped beyond. "Beyond what Doc?" Beyond
reason, beyond rhythm, beyond comprehension. Reason has patterns.
Comprehension has sequences. All things have rhythm, But this Ibadan
case has none of the triple attributes.
And when something becomes very different, it becomes something new.
This post grads' death blew out of obituary proportions.
The 15 grads were just there motionless, lifeless with the taught of
their degree certificates which will lie deadly in their files
somewhere in the Academic office unseen, unclaimed, unused. And the
police would record it in the Accident Statistics Form: Number of
persons killed 16 (15+ 1 driver) Statistics is statistics. Death is
death.
As if I had been in a trance, the totality of the mindless highway
slaughters of the innocent on our highways struck me as never before
and I started thinking again. "What good is it to spend thousands of
naira to educate a youngster from Pre-UPE through the university and
beyond if only his or her life is to be snuffed out on our highways
unceremoniously before she or he has a chance to use his/her
knowledge and life."
I Postponed thinking and I started getting worried about the two
prominent cabinet Ministers (the Honourable Minister of Police
Affairs and Honourable Minister of Finance) within one year in quick
succession. Suddenly my mind went to the sad memory lane: the
accidents of Professor Achebe, the intellectual creator of Things
Fall Apart, Arrow of God, Man of the People, Edith Ihekweazu, the
first appointed female professor by the University of Nigeria Nsukka;
professor Chris Oparaocha, the vetenary Surgeon; Dr. Oluwole of
Medical Services of Unilag; Professor Fagbemi, the Unilag's first
professor of Informatics; Mary Ebigbo, the postgrad student. Others
include Segun Awolowo, Alhaji Adelabu Coloner Inenger; Mrs. Rasaki,
Mr. Fasakin And Emma Agese (the journalists); Chief Adedipe a
legislator of Ondo House of Assembly, Ambassador Isa Modibo from
Yola, Mr. Obateye, former Secretary of Old Western Region; Alhaji Ali
Akilu of the Northern Region Government, Commodore Wole Awe of former
DFRRI, Alhaji Sanni Bayero, (Galadima of Kano) Ambassador Sada
(former National Productivity; Michael Oghenede (Guardian Reporter);
Four President Shagari's children in one day in 1987; Alhaji
Mohammed, former Secretary to Government of Babangida, the Abacha
Escorts, Kunle Johnson (the son of late JMJ) first minister of
Sports, Mr. Godwin Osofie, the newspaper vendor in Benin. Some were
lucky because traffic death gave fine practical jokes on them. e.g.
Dr. Pat Utomi, Professor Achebe, the two cabinet Ministers, Dupe
Sadima in Texas, Adekunle Babalola and host of others. Motorcycle too
took its toll on A.G. Adejumo, a Nigeria Airways pilot on Badagry
road.
"Hold it Doc. Road Safety, I have heard enough of the doom roll call.
"Sisi Debola broke the trend. I quickly responded that she should
take cold comfort in the fact that the traffic accident's gloomy
picture is not a concern only in Nigeria. For instance, Charles Pete
Conrad, a veteran of four space flights died from a motorcycle
accident on July 8, 1999. (another traffic accident erratic
privilege? Others outside our shores killed in their prime include
Queen Adtrid of Belgium while on holiday in Switzerland; Pierre
Curie, a French Nobel Prize winner in Physics killed in Paris; James
Dean, world famous American film star, Isadora Duncan, the legendary
American dancer; Albert Camus, another French author and Nobel Prize
winner in Literature; Dr. Mohammed Tuleb Hachicha of Tunisia; Charles
Drew who invented the blood-bank system; Bessie Smith, empress of the
Blues, and ofcourse Princess Diana of Britain who died in Paris and
the most recent Mr. Tunji Ogunkanmi of Cornerstone Insurance who had
been erroneously labelled an armed robbery victim. The Ogunkanmi's
case eloquently illustrates the point that our knowledge of the
causes of traffic accidents in this country is fragmentary and
spurious.
And the raging epidemic continues unabated.
That's when the hospital door creaked open and the police sergeant
stepped gingerly outside looking to avoid the released body smells
with an overtone of sweet-tom-tom. It was the "eye witness story that
did it- the dead silence. It was one of the biggest stories of
highway suicides in Nigeria today. It was blacker than the
over-orchestrated armed robbery cases in Lagos in the past one month.
It was a scoop.
According to the story, they were singing and clapping. They were
happy. One was just dozing off. And suddenly there were screams and
awful tearing sounds "wham whoom," cracking bones and joints. The
butchered pieces of human flesh started flying about like UFO and in
a jiffy, the once shinning vehicle became a shabby paintless scrap.
It was incredible. 16 trunk bodies: 14 male and two female.
Ordinarily, it would have taken a good three-hour, job for a skilled
butcher to whack out the crevices seen in the wrecked vehicle and to
dismember the bodies as they were. But where were the butchers? Where
were the tools. The answers are shroud in secrecy in hi-tech safety
science.
The first doctor to arrive looked and made a blanket pronouncement of
death; 14 male; 2 female. And the 15 postgrads died, dead and
deadened. It was when the orthopaedic nurses tried to reassemble some
parts for identification (which they knew by sight would be
impossible) that I got effortlessly mad and suddenly discovered the
other beyond - reason element in this slaughter that I had already
stamped in my mind as beyond reason: MADNESS. Perishable madness.
Although we all know that man or woman is born under the sentence of
death but when 15 postgrads of a University in a developing country
like Nigeria with their brilliant promise only a quarter fulfilled
felled by an utterly incomprehensible tragedy through the use of
automobile, the loss is more than a great loss to the university as
postulated by Mr. Adebayo, the president of the Universities
Education Students Association (NUESA). It is a big loss to the
bereaved family. It is a loss suffered by the entire country, the
academic world, and all humanity.
That's one great reason today why it is necessary for the country to
take a more aggressive appraisal of the road accident scene. I am
sure that something fundamental within the subject matter demands
review, change, re-examination and redefinition. The magnitude and
alarming rate of fatality presently prevailing on our highways makes
it abundantly clear that something more than illiterate and
commercial driver, the age of the vehicle vis-a-vis poor maintenance,
the highway layout and the corrupt law enforcement agencies are the
epicentres of our traffic unsafety problems. the untimely death of
over 230,000 recorded persons and over 730,000 injured human beings
in over 920,000 accidents on our highways in the last 45 years
dictates a crying need to understand "WHY."
But that is statistics. Statistics, either mechanical or dry, is
statistics. And death, either mechanical or gruesome, is death. It is
therefore time to do away with double talk cosmeti and get down to
the substance of the specifics: saving lives. Statistics is
cosmetics. Death is substance.
The Question in our traffic scene is no longer when is it going to
happen but who's next? Your guess is as good as mine because as inter
alia stated, traffic accidents have no class barriers, geographical
boundaries or gender inequalities. Nobody is immune. It does not
respond to panic panaceas of fire brigade measures. it does not wait
for academic tautology or grandiloquent unfashionable theories. It
does not have patience for merger or no merger arguments.
It is a tough problem that does not believe in idle or
unsubstantiated experimentation but demand very touch solution. But
we can't get at any appreciable solution with our present level of
understanding and "Scissors and Paste" method. All the elements of a
highway safety matrix must be brought into balance. This is what the
social scientists call the "systems approach and the Engineers,
"systems engineering." There is need for an "umbrella" legislation
and administration in our highway safety beginning at the level of
the Presidency with the National Assembly providing good legal
foundations and adequate funding appropriated. The state's role must
become more prominent especially within its sub-political entities -
the local government, the private sector must function as the fourth
active partner in national highway safety effort.
And finally the Federal Government must make possible significant
innovative advances and extraordinary efforts in the delivery of
hi-tech highway safety services; federalistic approach; management
reforms, new funding system, traffic safety uniform standards, motor
vehicle safety standards, well-defined national goals, professional
manpower, and public acceptability.
So those who gathered at the funeral service for the 15 gone
academicians can begin to change the course of road safety in this
country. They should not only have gathered in desperation to bury
the departed students but also to start taking a realistic and
hard-headed look at road safety and the roles the university of
Ibadan must play within such a new framework. The emphasis should not
be upon negative but positive. From the educational stand point, why
can't the University start today the idea of establishing a Highway
Traffic Safety Centre? Michigan State University has one.
Missouri State University, south California University, Southern
Illinois University, University of North Carolina, Iowa University -
all have it.. Or in the alternative, spearhead the introduction of
traffic safety and driver education into the primary and secondary
schools in Nigeria. The UI Council, the Senate Academic Community,
and the Nations are ready to pay the price if such a project can help
bring an optimistic highway safety development not as astounding as
the 45 years of highway perishable madness behind us. Traffic safety
is an interdisciplinary subject and multi-jurisdictionary matter. The
subject can benefit from the University. Traffic safety need not be
all Abuja. The University of Ibadan can start The brainstorming today
because we don't have decades: we already had wasted decades. As
Mendelsohn aptly points out no one in his right mind wants to have an
accident. Safety itself needs no selling.

Original_Search_Title: Food & Drinks: Healthy Eating Even In Restaurants?
Headline: Nigeria: Food & Drinks: Healthy Eating Even In Restaurants?
Body_Text:
Lagos — It is not absurd that many people do not know how to choose what to
eat when they eat out.
You should choose only meals that are healthy for you. Restaurants
that serve foreign dishes now abound in Nigeria, making the question
of choice increasingly difficult. There are Italian, Chinese,
Lebanese, French, Mexican and Indian restaurants and fast food joints
fast out- pacing the local cafeteria.
The good news about Italian foods, for instance, is that the basics
are healthy. Italian foods include pasta, fresh vegetables, tomato-
based sauces, lean meats, poultry, seafood, and fresh bread. Calories
and fat slowly begin to mount when oil, butter, or cream is taken in
big portions. When you decide to dine at any Italian restaurant, some
of these healthy tips should guide you:
-Salad can be a good starter and filler. Ask for a salad with the
salad dressing or oil and vinegar on the side so that you can do away
with the excess. While olive oil is healthier than saturated fats
like butter, a tablespoon can still drizzle over 13 grams of fat on
your radicchio. Keep that number in mind when you're sopping fresh
Italian bread in deliciously flavoured olive oil on your table.
-If you love antipasto, go easy on the fatty cold cuts and cheeses;
order a vegetable antipasto. Watch the dressing.
-Classic pasta with marinara sauce can be a lean choice. Many
restaurants have fresh, shredded parmesan cheese. A sprinkle adds
only a smidgen of fat (one tablespoon) lus tons of sharp flavour.
-If you want a heavier entree such as fettuccini alfredo or lasagna,
then downsize. Forget about ordering it as an entree if the plate is
the size of a platter. See if you can get an appetizer-size portion.
By reducing the serving size, the fat and calories are also cut. You
can save additional calories and fat if you skip the dressing on your
salad and bypass the spread on the bread.
Here's another portion control trick: Ask for a half portion or have
only half of your entree served in the restaurant. Take the remainder
home for the next day's dinner. Think of this as dining out twice;
the bonus is that you only had to tip once (ha, ha).
For proper handling of take-home foods, ask the waiter to store your
leftovers in the refrigerator until you're ready to leave the
restaurant. Perishable food shouldn't stay at room temperature for
more than two hours. Re-heat food thoroughly to a temperature of 165
degrees F.
Although naked pasta is typically low in fat at about 200 calories
for a cooked cup, eating a large quantity of linguine could have you
bumping your daily calorie limit into the next day.
If you want something a little sweet at the end of the meal, order a
decaf cappuccino. Made with low-fat milk and topped with a sprinkling
of cocoa or cinnamon, it can replace the cannoli, or to make it
easier, share it with a friend.
However if tonight's dinner is a splurge night, just balance the
extra fat and calories over the next several days.
best bets in Chinese restaurants
The high increase in the number of Asian food lovers has created much
curiosity that a report was recently carried out to unravel exactly
what people got in Chinese food. After trekking across the country
and collecting 900 soups, appetisers, and entrees from 11 Chinese
restaurants, it was no surprise that the sodium content was hefty in
most items and the fat content high in deep-fried choices. But the
biggest surprise was that the average portion for the entrees tested
ranged from 15 to 26 ounces, which is enough food for a small Chinese
village. A surprise indeed! Here are some useful tips when eating
Chinese:
-Share among friends: When ordering, calculate at least two or three
people per entree. This will help alleviate the over-ordering
problem.
-Start with soup: Bypass more calorie-laden appetizers in favour of
soups, especially vegetable-based ones which are filling. Wonton soup
has less than 100 calories and only two grams of fat. Unfortunately,
at 641 mg per serving, the sodium is hefty. Most other Chinese
entrees also fare poorly when it comes to sodium. It is therefore
recommended that you keep your sodium intake under 2,400 mg daily or
balance it out the following day.
-Order steamed or stir-fried, not deep-fried portions: While sweet
and sour pork is a porker at over 20 grams of fat for a small, six-
ounce portion, the steamed fish had 11 grams of fat due, in part, to
its oil-based sauce. To bypass the fat, ask the chef to go easy with
the sauce, or better still, have the sauce on the side and only
drizzle a small amount on top. While you're at it, also ask the chef
to use a light touch with the oil when stir-frying.
-Go long on veggies: Few people can make vegetables taste as good as
the Chinese. Order a platter of steamed vegetables and mix them into
your entree. You may expand the entree to feed an army, and this
trick dilutes the fat and calories per serving. Make sure that the
steamed vegetables are free of sauce and seasoning that can spike the
sodium and fat content.
-Roll out the rice: You haven't lived it up, food-wise until you have
eaten steamed brown rice from a Chinese restaurant. It's absolutely
tasty and a low-fat filler. You won't miss the fattier fried rice
after you've tried it.
meatless fast foods
Some people now have to do without eating meat. Eating well while on
the run is difficult enough, but when you cut away meat, fish, or
poultry from the list of possibilities and add the desire to shed
unwanted pounds, it may seem like an Olympic challenge. But it's not
impossible.
Assuming that you eat dairy products, such as cheese, and legumes,
such as beans and chickpeas, I think these tips may help you scale
the hurdles:
-For a quick, no-fuss meal, head straight to a good supermarket or
deli salad bar. Typically, most are not only stuffed with tons of
low- calorie fresh vegetables, but also protein-rich items, such as
low-fat cottage cheese, chickpeas, three-bean salad, and possibly
too, chunks of tofu. Add some low-fat salad dressing and pita bread,
and you'll be delighted.
-Bean soups can also be part of a hearty lunch or dinner. Many take-
out delis, restaurants, and upscale supermarkets now serve takeaway-
soups. Order a hearty bowl and grab packed salad along with a whole
grain bagel, roll or bread. Pizza with vegetables and a salad is a
quick fix and can be found at most pizza parlours.
-Some fast-food restaurants offer baked potatoes. Order it plain
along with a side of steamed broccoli. Then head for the salad bar
and scoop low-fat cottage cheese to top your broccoli spud.
-Sometimes you may need to fortify your meals on wheels. Many folks
keep vegetarian burgers in their office freezers. When they are out
at noontime, they buy a salad 'on the run', and later at the office
zap the burger in the microwave and stick it in a pita. Try it, you'd
like it too.
seafood restaurants
To get he best low-cal options when ordering seafood requires that
when you dine at your local seafood restaurant, try to fish out the
best choices on the menu. Lean choices are endless.
-Most seafood is low in fat and calories: A three-ounce cooked
portion (about the size of a deck of cards) of either haddock, cod,
or halibut is less than 120 calories and no more than three grams of
fat. Shrimp, scallops, and clams are also great choices, with only
about 80 to 125 calories and one to two grams of fat. (Shrimp is
higher in dietary cholesterol, with a little over 165 milligrams in
three ounces.
The current recommendation is to keep daily dietary cholesterol under
300mg, on the average.) While salmon has a little more fat--nine
grams in three ounces cooked--the majority of it is the healthier,
unsaturated type. Ask the chef to go easy with the oil, butter, or
any other added fat when preparing your fish entree.
-Look for buzzwords: Words like baked, broiled, steamed, poached,
boiled, or grilled keep the calories and fat under wraps. Breaded
seafood such as fish and chips can soak up fat like a sponge. The
deep- fried Big Fish burger is a big catch at 720 calories and 43
grams of fat. Some of the added fat comes from the tartar sauce
served with the Big Fish. A tablespoon of tartar sauce can have 70
calories and eight grams of fat.
-Get a workout during your meal: A one-pound lobster in the shell has
less than 3.5 ounces of meat, about 85 calories, and less than a gram
of fat: the perfect entree to help you exercise perfect portion
control. And the best part is that you have to work at fishing out
all the meat, which slows down your eating and keeps your hands busy.
Bypass the dish of melted butter, which adds an extra 108 calories
and over 12 grams of fat per tablespoon. Instead, ask for an
additional empty plate and plenty of lemon slices. Squeeze the lemon
into the empty dish and then add a small amount of melted butter.
Presto: lemon sauce with a tinge of butter rather than the inverse.
-Don't go silly with the sauces: Creamy entrees, such as lobster
'newborn' (made with butter or margarine, egg yolks, and cream), can
tip the scale at 485 calories and over 25 grams of fat per cup. If a
creamy casserole or cheesy seafood dish is a favourite, order an
appetizer-size portion.
-Chow down only a cup of chowder: No one loves chowder better than
me. If you're a fan of this creamy delight, make it a cup, not a
bowl, to put a lid on the amount. Consider ordering a lean entree to
offset the calories. Tomato-based Manhattan clam chowder is typically
a leaner bet.
-Slide the bulk aside: Plain baked potatoes, garden salads, and
steamed vegetables will add bulk to your meal. Keep the spread and
salad dressing on the side and use it sparingly to help control the
calories.
Stay healthy.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria's Face For Africa
Headline: Nigeria's Face For Africa
Body_Text:
The Nigerian leg of the year 2001 Face of Africa finals coming up
later in the year in Sun City, Cape Town, South Africa yields pretty
Adeola Adenugba, YINKA IJABIYI reports
The atmosphere at the Pro-flex Gym., venue of the second stage of the
preliminary screenings in the Lagos regional search for the Nigerian
finalist to the year 2001 Face of Africa finals (presented by M-Net
and Anglogold), was highly relaxed on the surface. The girls arrived
early and quickly formed alliances and friendships while they waited
for the screening to begin. Many had even began exchanging addresses
long before the commencement of the day's activities.
Soon, the screening team arrived and the day's task kicked off. The
girls were called in for the first level of screening which was the
height check.
Those who could not measure up to 5'8 were simply asked to go. In
some rare cases, a few who where too tall were disqualified.
Waistlines, bursts and shoulders caused others the chance to be super
models like Oluchi. There were those who got screened out because
their figure did not meet the required specification. Others just
didn't have the face, look, teeth or any of the other tiny details
that helped determine who becomes a super model and who has a chance
of becoming a model.
At the end of the highly tasking and tiring process, nine of the
girls were determined to have qualities that would enthrall the
fashion and beauty world.
The nine girls that emerged from the screening were expected to join
the group of girls that had earlier scaled the hurdles in the race
for the Face of Africa bumper four year prize in November last year.
Together, the two groups would join the quarter finalists from the
Abuja and Port-Harcourt preliminaries.
On Wednesday, 31 January 2001, the search finally came to an end. The
duo of Jan Malan and Ipetla Moatshe flew in from South Africa to
choose that one girl that had the most potential to be the Face of
Africa 2001. The first part of the three part screening was concluded
before a small crowd that comprised mostly journalists.
At the end of the first round, only seven of the twenty-seven girls
passed the test administered by Face of Africa originator and
outgoing producer, Jan Malan and in-coming producer, Ipetla Moatshe.
Unlike the other girls who had dropped out before this stage, there
were plenty of wet eyes, sour faces and bitter miens amongst the
twenty girls who got kicked out in the quarter-final. These girls
were escorted out of the screening hall to await the neatly packed
potato-chips and chicken lunch packs which was to be handed out to
them as their share of the elusive Face of Africa largesse.
Male journalists too had a taste of how the defeated girls must have
felt when they were escorted out of the screening room. They too were
politely but unceremoniously shepherded out of the room. The next
round of screening involved the girls appearing in swim suits. The
M-Net crew must have believed that allowing the "boys" see the sweet
sixteens clad in such sheer briefs, might cause the loss of a few
senses. After it became obvious that the nooks and crannies where
they might slip into to see the best of the girls was non-existent,
the 'boys' slunk off. The girls had their bikini parade without the
male journalists and at the end of it, Jan and Ipetla still could not
decide who would carry Nigeria's flag in South Africa in May. The
bikini parade was meant to help reveal any tell-tale ugly birth-marks
or scars that might further help to scale down the number .
Obviously, none had such misfortune. Finally, Jan and Ipetla had to
put it to vote. Voting was done by secret balloting. They both had to
choose one girl out of the lot and they had just one vote a piece.
Well, they both chose the same girl at the first ballot.
Nenye Ochuba (17), Fadimiyi Tola, Omoruwa Ebhojie, Ebisan Tonwe (15),
Agbani Darego (18) and Odisika Odogwu (18) finally had to bow to the
superior beauty (or is it Africaness?) of petite, leggy and
innocent-looking sixteen year-old Adeola Adenugba. Adeola thus would
be on the ramp in South Africa come May, for the finals.
Adeola lives in Lagos. She only recently completed her SSCE
examination at the Federal Government College, Ijanikin. She was
waiting at home for her university admission to fall through before
the M-NET win. Now, her life has just changed. Come May, she will
compete for the final crown of Face of Africa alongside twenty three
other belles from thirty African nations.
Adeola stands the chance of winning a lucrative four year modelling
contract worth a whooping two hundred thousand dollars (twenty
million naira) with Ford Models Inc. America's largest and most
prestigious modelling agency. This prize is unlike the one hundred
and fifty thousand dollars for a three year modelling contract with
Elite Models that Oluchi, Benvinda and Nombulelo, the three earlier
winners, respectively pocketed.
Though, the Ford Models Inc. prize is not that different from the
Elite prize. The new winner will simply get a chance to work for an
extra year under Ford Models.
Apart from the final winner's mouthwatering prize, the twenty-three
other finalists will not be going home empty-handed. Courtesy M-Net,
the finalists (like Adeola, if she doesn't become the fourth Face )
will get an opportunity to pursue careers in modelling in South
Africa.
M-NET now has a modelling agency to help manage the girls who made it
to the finals. This implies that, as long as they choose to (or as
long as modelling jobs can be found for them), the girls will live
the lives of princesses in South Africa. Or even America or Europe.
Like Ojy Eguwuenu and Funmilayo Alaiyemola, both Nigerian finalists
at last year's Face. Funmi continues to be a wave making model in
South Africa even now. Ojy has chosen to pick up a modelling contract
in New York. Like Funmi and Ojy, win or lose, the fun has only just
begun for Adeola.

Original_Search_Title: UNB Plans R46m Offer to Buy Back Its Shares
Headline: South Africa: UNB Plans R46m Offer to Buy Back Its Shares
Body_Text:
UNITED National Breweries (UNB), the former National Sorghum Breweries, announced on Friday plans to launch a R46m buy-back offer to its minority shareholders.
The offer at 100c a share will enable shareholders in the unlisted company to realise their investment. No dividend has been paid since 1995, when the company moved into the red.
Only at the financial year end of December 31 1999, did the company report it had moved into the black, with a profit of R504000. The upward trend in profits was repeated at the end of last year with R18,5m.
UNB president and chief operating officer, Rajan Ranganathan, said that on the basis of advice from the company's investment bankers, Nedcor, the directors had resolved to offer the buy-back to minorities, so providing the large base of small shareholders with an opportunity to realise their investment.
The company would repurchase a total of almost 46-million shares (65,61%) of the share capital from about 9000 shareholders, including the Premier group, Gensec, the Transvaal Municipal Pension Fund and Nedcor.
Ranganathan said that in view of the volatility in the financial markets and the probability that this would continue in the short to medium term a listing on the JSE Securities Exchange was not feasible.
He said that the steady loss of consumer rands to fast-growing industries such as cellphones and the lotto, and the attrition of the industry's consumer base through age and the HIV/AIDS pandemic, had made it exceptionally difficult for the industry to increase profitability significantly.
Ranganathan said Deloitte & Touche, acting as independent advisers to UNB's minority shareholders, had reviewed the terms of the acquisition and considered them fair and reasonable.
The implementation of the planned scheme is subject to court approval.

Original_Search_Title: Rain Spoils Soft-Drink Sales
Headline: South Africa: Rain Spoils Soft-Drink Sales
Body_Text:
Manufacturers of soft drinks and ice creams are even gloomier than the average rainsoaked resident of Gauteng and KwaZulu-Natal because they are looking at disappointing sales figures for key markets at the beginning of their peak selling seasons.
Alan Clark, MD of Amalgamated Beverage Industries, the biggest bottler of soft drinks in SA, said yesterday that this was the sixth week of almost continuously cloudy weather in Gauteng. Weather in KwaZulu-Natal had also been poor.
The group does not operate in Cape Town, where the weather has generally been hot in recent weeks.
Clark said that year-on-year sales of soft drinks were holding up and the group was maintaining the positive levels it reported at interim stage. In the six months to September it grew sales volumes 3%.
However, sales in the past few weeks could have been a lot better, Clark said. "As soon as there is a slightly sunny day we see sales picking up sharply."
Amalgamated's parent, SA Breweries, which dominates local beer sales and sells cider and alcoholic fruit beverages, was unable to comment yesterday.
But it said with its interim results that beer volumes had shown growth on a like-for-like basis since October 1 and were now ahead of the same period last year.
Sales of traditional beverages are not particularly seasonal "unless it rains very hard", Awethu MD Tony Ford said, in which case sales could fall 5%-10%. But sales had not been affected this year.
Awethu manufactures sorghum beer and mageu and its franchise outlets are mainly in Free State, Gauteng and Northern Province.
Mike Vesey, local MD of UKbased HP Bulmer, best known for its cider, said sales for the company were doing reasonably well, but this could partly be a result of the launch of new products such as Tattoo, a spirit cooler.
Sales of alcoholic beverages were certainly sensitive to the weather and Bulmer was hoping for a warm December.
Ice cream was another weathersensitive product. Marnus Swanepoel, a brand manager at Dairymaid-NestlÃ©, said good weather on Saturdays and Sundays was particularly important. The ice cream season usually started in November.
Swanepoel said he had noticed a decline in ice cream sales in November compared with the corresponding period last year but he did not give numbers.

Original_Search_Title: Father Hormisdas Nsengimana: Accused of Genocide; Sheltered By the Church
Headline: Rwanda: Father Hormisdas Nsengimana: Accused of Genocide; Sheltered By the Church
Body_Text:
London — A priest accused of genocide, Father Hormisdas Nsengimana, is serving
today in the Parish of Diang in the Diocese of Bertoua, Cameroon.
Individuals like Father Hormisdas are extremely difficult to accuse and
even more difficult to bring to justice. People prepared to testify against
them have to be able to stand up against the might of the Catholic Church,
still one of the most powerful institutions in the world. In speaking out
about the crimes of the clergy, survivors and witnesses of the genocide have
to relive the horror of their past amid the pressure of criticism from
voices imbued with moral authority. In a country like Rwanda, where most
people are Catholic, this pressure is even more acute.
African Rights has decided to publish the findings of its research into
the case against Father Hormisdas Nsengimana, despite persistent efforts on
the part of those close to the Church to discredit the organisation and its
sources. We are confident that the conviction in Belgium on 8 June of two
Rwandese nuns, Sister Gertrude Mukangango and Sister Julienne Kizito, whose
involvement in the 1994 genocide was detailed by African Rights in previous
reports, will have given pause for thought. But at present it seems that the
Church is still more likely to hinder than to help to those responsible for
bringing genocide suspects to trial, as is apparent in the case of Father
Athanase Seromba. Although the International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda
(ICTR) managed to arrest another priest, Father Emmanuel Rukundo in
Switzerland, tribunal officials attempting to arrest Father Seromba in Italy
have so far been prevented by a wall of judicial bureaucracy, while his
parish authorities have spirited him into seclusion. It may be impossible to
disentangle the extent of the Church's influence in hampering his arrest,
but in a traditionally Catholic country, host to the Vatican, there can be
little doubt that its authority holds some sway. The Church should have used
its influence in a positive way to smooth the path of justice.
In this report, consistent accounts of Father Hormisdas' involvement in
the killings in the Parish of Nyanza in Butare, are given by genocide
survivors, witnesses and former members of Father Hormisdas' staff. There
are also chilling descriptions of the priest's cruelty from genocide
detainees who confirm that he was among the organisers of the
slaughter.
These may help to shine more light through the curtains the Church has
drawn over information linking certain of its clergy to the genocide.
Survivors and witnesses have given examples of Hormisdas' conduct,
during the genocide and before it. Their statements are careful records of
their own pain or of the suffering of other members of their community. It
would be extremely distasteful to try to silence their voices by trumpeting
the claim that the Catholic Church is a victim of a campaign sponsored by
the Rwandese Patriotic Front (RPF) and its allies, as some have done in
response to similar cases.  Tragically it is a fact that suffering and death
was inflicted upon Tutsis in Nyanza in 1994. Father Hormisdas was present
during the killings, and all the evidence suggests he was complicit in the
atrocities. The victims whose plight should be the focus of concern are the
dead, the maimed and the grieving people of Nyanza. For their sake it is
essential that Father Hormisdas be put on trial.
Teaching Hatred
Father Hormisdas was appointed the principal of Christ Roi, a respected
secondary school, in Nyanza, commune Nyabisindu in Butare in 1989. From the
time of his arrival there, both his sermons and his lessons were
divisive.
Many considered him an unlikely priest, because of his armed bodyguards
and his support for the ultra-extremist political faction, the Committee for
the Defence of the Republic (CDR). Tutsis were so wary of him that when the
violence against them began they did not seek sanctuary in the Parish of
Nyanza. This was unheard of at a time when all over the rest of the country
churches were crammed with displaced people. In the words of one witness:
"The Christians of Nyanza were afraid of seeking refuge at the parish
because they knew Hormisdas would massacre them immediately." Father
Hormisdas had long been an ally of prominent Hutu extremists, including the
sous-prÃ©fet (governor), GaÃ«tan Kayitana, and the commander of the
gendarmerie, Captain Birikunzira. In April 1994, these three began to meet
very regularly and their purpose was soon apparent to the staff and people
living near Christ Roi. They blatantly exploited their positions in the
community to organise the genocide in Nyabisindu and to convince people to
participate. Their programme was launched with a meeting on 21 April,
convened by Fr. Hormisdas, to draw up a list of Tutsi victims. A man who
attended the meeting recalled Hormisdas' speech:
This evening we're quickly going to compile the list of Tutsis to
kill.
The leaders of the country are very angry with us because we still
haven't started the genocide, despite the fact that there are plenty of
Tutsis in Nyanza, especially educated people. I have to give the list that
we're going to compile to the soldiers so that they can start killing very
early in the morning I know that you, the Hutus of Nyanza, aren't used to
killing the Tutsis. Now you must kill. Things have changed.
The following morning the killings began.
A Leader Among the Killers
Christ Roi became a base for the planners of the genocide and the
militia.
The central role played by Fr. Hormisdas is apparent from the nature and
frequency of the meetings that took place there. Joseph Semuhunyege was in
charge of security at the college at night and kept a close eye on the
comings and goings.
After Habyarimana's death, clandestine meetings were held at Christ
Roi.
Hormisdas received a lot of people at his house. There were also
soldiers, natives of Gikongoro, who came to these meetings. These meetings
began at 8:00 p.m.; all the participants left at about 3:00 a.m.
Jean-Claude Higiro worked at the dairy under a man who is accused to
taking part in the genocide, Callixte Mirasano, a member of CDR.
Jean-Claude's father was also a CDR supporter and is imprisoned on charges
of genocide.
As Jean-Claude said: "no one could prevent me from moving around. I saw
everything." He is, therefore, well placed to comment upon the activities of
Fr. Hormisdas and others.
Birikunzira, Kayitana and Fr. Hormisdas organised and supervised the
genocide in Nyanza. They were always in the same car, inspecting the
roadblocks. I saw Kayitana and Birikunzira in Fr. Hormisdas' car. Fr.
Hormisdas was one of the people who set traps for the Tutsis.
Marthe Nyirahabiyambere, who sadly has died since her interview was
recorded, had the courage to put her own son behind bars for the crime of
genocide. Marthe, like Fr. Hormisdas, was a native of Gikongoro, and she
knew the priest's family. She was a Tutsi but had married a Hutu and moved
to Nyanza where she raised her children, seven girls and three boys. Her
daughters were all married to Tutsis and were killed with their families in
the genocide. But one of her sons, Pascal Mutabazi, became a
militiaman.
Pascal was, Marthe said, "the right-hand man of Fr. Hormisdas" in April
and May 1994.
I always forbade my son from participating in the massacres. But he
never listened to me. He considered me to be a traitor and a collaborator of
the Inkotanyi [RPF].
My son did whatever Fr. Hormisdas wanted him to do. He was his
right-hand man. I often saw them together at the home of ThÃ©rÃ¨se, a militia
woman who worked at Nyanza hospital. She also had a bar where the militiamen
met after they had been out killing. During the genocide, Hormisdas was no
longer a priest. He became a killer.
I saw Hormisdas at Gakenyeri with a lot of militiamen like Murindangabo,
an ex-FAR and a native of Bwakira commune in Kibuye, the brother of ThÃ©rÃ¨se;
Augustin Munyarugamba, a cellule responsable; Alphonse Nyiriminega, my
neighbour; and my son, Pascal. They had machetes and massues. As he was the
most educated, Hormisdas was their advisor.
It was Hormisdas who distributed the machetes to the prominent
gÃ©nocidaires. The machetes were stocked at Christ Roi. My son often went to
fetch them from there in order to hand them out to the militiamen in our
district.
Marthe's son came to search her house on several occasions to see if she
was hiding Tutsis. He warned her saying: "If I find a Tutsi here I'll kill
you straightaway. Hormisdas said we should not have pity on any Tutsi."
Around 4:00 a.m. one morning two women and their four children who were
fleeing the militia came to Marthe looking for somewhere to hide. She fed
them and gave them clothes. But before long Pascal returned. He followed
Hormisdas' orders and showed no mercy.
When my son saw them, he shouted and accused me of "hiding Inkotanyi."
He had a machete and a massue. I pleaded with him not to kill them. He beat
me seriously before he pounced on them, killing the women and two of the
children. He continued to maintain his relationship with Hormisdas. This
showed me that the priest was a friend of the gÃ©nocidaires.
According to several witnesses, Fr. Hormisdas was responsible for the
selection of an an elite team of militiamen known as "the dragon" who were
dispersed around Nyabisindu commune to lead the massacres.  Innocent
Habyarimana, a carpenter from Nyanza, commented:
It was the dragon which exterminated all the Tutsi of Nyabisindu
commune.
The dragon had all sorts of weapons. They used the Christ Roi car, the
car of the dairy in Nyabisindu and the vehicle that belonged to
Electrogaz.
After killing, these militiamen would meet at the home of either
Hormisdas or Simon Kalinda [a builder at Christ Roi] to eat and drink.
Janvier Habimana, who is under arrest charged with taking part in the
genocide in Nyabisindu, described the relations between Fr. Hormisdas and
the militiamen who included his own brother, Cyprien, a teacher.
The Tutsis of Nyanza were killed by soldiers and a group of gÃ©nocidaires
that Mirasano, Fr. Hormisdas and the sous-prÃ©fet GaÃ«tan had trained. This
group consisted of people who came from Ruhengeri and Gikongoro who worked
at the Nyabisindu dairy and at Christ Roi.  The head of this team was
Segema, an employee of the dairy. It was a very well-known team of
killers.
I often saw Fr. Hormisdas; GaÃ«tan; Birikunzira and Mirasano driving
together in the same car, around the roadblocks. This team of killers
followed the militiamen; we could see that they were on friendly terms.
This indicated that they were the ones who established the militia.
They were their employers.
The blue Toyota which belonged to Christ Roi was often seen transporting
militiamen on their killing sprees. Under Fr. Hormisdas' control, the
Church's resources were used to support the genocide.  Indeed, the school
buildings themselves were a home to killers.
Christ Roi: A College for the Genocide
A roadblock was established outside Christ Roi and was manned by a group
of extremist students from the college. These students had been displaced by
fighting between the government and the rebel Rwandese Patriotic Army (RPA)
in their home region of Byumba, and had come to Nyanza in 1992. Fr.
Hormisdas had quickly identified them as a receptive audience for his
views and he continued to influence them during the genocide.  Emma-Marie
Nyirazaninka survived the genocide because of the protection she received
from the headmaster of Nyanza primary school, Nyamulinda, who hid her in his
house. It was her ability to brew the sorghum beer known as ikigage that
endeared her to him and to the displaced students from Byumba who were
staying at the school, who told her of their exploits. She learned that they
were acting upon Fr. Hormisdas' orders.
Three of these students were terrible-Cyprien, Mudenge and Beyaka.  They
killed a lot of people. They were like wild animals. Every student had a
massue, but these three also had grenades and guns.  These three students
came in very tired, looking for me to give them sorghum beer. When they
started drinking, they laughed and said to me: "Despite everything that
Hormisdas told us about not having pity for a single Tutsi, we're going to
spare you." One day these students came in with a bucket containing
meat.
They told me: "We've just finished the mission that Hormisdas and the
employees of the dairy gave us yesterday during our meeting at Christ
Roi.
Really, there are no Tutsis left in Nyakabuye and Remera [in Nyanza].
We've combed these areas thoroughly."
Inside Christ Roi was another group of killers that were at the
forefront of the genocide in Nyanza. These were soldiers from the Ecole
SupÃ©rieure Militaire (ESM), who used the college as a barracks, sleeping in
the dormitories. Hutu employees of the college who remained on the premises
noted the links they established with Hormisdas. Callixte Kalisa, a cook at
Christ Roi, saw the soldiers both at the college and outside. He commented:
Hormisdas housed the soldiers who killed the Nyanza Tutsis at Christ
Roi.
He welcomed them to Christ Roi and they ravaged Nyanza, murdering the
Tutsis. They were officers. They blocked all the paths leading to the
college so that it was difficult to go there. These soldiers stayed at the
college during the entire period of the genocide, housed and fed by
Hormisdas. Sometimes I could hear them talking in the evenings or at
mealtimes. They were womanisers. Sometimes they would come to our
neighbourhood and ask us where they could find Tutsi women and girls.
Fear held most Tutsis back from looking to Christ Roi as a possible
shelter. But a few acquaintances of Hormisdas-old family friends, neighbours
and some students from Christ Roi-thought their ties with him might still
have influence and asked him for a hiding place at Christ Roi.
Some of those he denied refuge, in the expectation that they would be
killed, survived to speak of how he delivered their relatives, neighbours
and fellow-refugees to their deaths.
Amurani Mbabariye's family lived behind the college. He and his seven
relatives ran instinctively there on 25 April when they became too fearful
of remaining at home. They were startled to find Fr.  Hormisdas in the
company of an armed militiaman. Nevertheless, Amurani's brother pleaded with
the priest to give them refuge.  Hormisdas' reply was brief. He said:
"You're all Tutsis, I can't hide you." Amurani realised there was no
hope.
I became overwhelmed with fear. I told my family to leave as I saw that
we could be killed. I ran away immediately. After jumping back over the
wall, I heard gunshots. My mother had stayed at home because she was
Hutu. She went to see if it was her children who had died.  When she came
back, she was crying a lot because she had seen their bodies. My brothers,
sisters, nephew and niece had been killed at the college.
Amurani then hid in a forest near Christ Roi. But in May he was almost
discovered by a group that included Hormisdas.
A group of militiamen from Mugonzi came to clear the bush all around the
college. I was lying in the bush; they didn't see me but I saw
everyone.
When the militiamen arrived where I was, I could see Fr. Hormisdas,
FranÃ§ois Gashirabake, Simon Kalinda and the students from Byumba who had
stayed at the school. Hormisdas and FranÃ§ois weren't holding anything in
their hands as they weren't clearing the bush. I saw that they were
supervising the others. Whilst I was waiting to be killed, I saw the
militiamen running and shouting because they had found a young Tutsi boy
hiding in the bush next to me. I didn't know he was there. The boy was
killed. I saw them looking at his body. Luckily, they left before they
reached the place where I was hiding. I stayed in this bush until the end of
the genocide in Nyanza.
Bresile Mukandera, the headmistress of Nyanza's primary school, had come
to know Hormisdas well through teachers' meetings. She sought his help to
hide her eldest daughter, Marie-Claire, who was 14 at the time. When Bresile
reached Hormisdas' office, she saw many cars; she looked through the window
of his office and saw a meeting in progress. The participants included a
number of leading gÃ©nocidaires.
I was afraid to enter, so I sent a gardener in to tell Hormisdas that I
had a message for him. Hormisdas came out to see me. I said that I wanted
him to hide my daughter in his school; I explained that no-one could search
for her there because he was a priest. He shouted and said: "You think that
I, Hormisdas, can hide the child of an Inkotanyi like Munyawera? The Tutsis
killed our President. Because of that I cannot hide any Tutsi. I hope you
have understood. I want to go back to the meeting." I continued to plead,
telling him I was even prepared to pay for anything the child would eat
while at his house.  Hormisdas told me to look elsewhere. Why could he not
hide Tutsis?
A number of witnesses have accused Fr. Hormisdas of delivering Jean
Twagirayezu, the president of the district court in Nyanza, to his death in
early May. Many Nyanza residents mistook Jean for a Hutu, and he had
survived in various hideouts during the first weeks of the killings in
Nyanza. In search of a more secure sanctuary, he decided to ask Hormisdas
for help. Marie-ThÃ©rÃ¨se Mukankusi lived next door to Christ Roi. She was one
of the last people to see Jean Twagirayezu alive and described the events
leading up to his murder.
At the beginning of May, I saw Jean going into Christ Roi. That day I
was just next to the college and Jean passed me by. He asked me if Fr.
Hormisdas was there. I said "yes." He told me that he was being
threatened and that he wanted to ask Hormisdas for a place of refuge.  He
said: "I trust he will be able to take me in because he is a priest." I told
him to go and try.
Jean went into the school. It was about midday. In the evening, I saw
Hormisdas and Jean. But just before that I saw the militiamen Cyprien,
PhÃ©neas and Simon running out of the school. They were going towards the
church.
Jean and Hormisdas passed by just next to me. I heard Fr. Hormisdas
saying: "Go back to your home. Anyone who escapes today will live."  They
arrived at the church and Hormisdas then went back to his home.
Straightaway I heard gunshots. I was told that Jean had been killed.  I
realised that Hormisdas had delivered him to his death.
Complicity in the Murder of Priests and Nuns
From the time of his arrival at Christ Roi, Fr. Hormisdas had made
apparent his dislike of the Tutsi priests at the Parish of Nyanza. He had
repeatedly accused one of them, Fr. Justin Furaha, of supporting the RPF,
eventually forcing him to move to another parish at Save.  During the
genocide, Fr. Hormisdas showed how deep this hostility ran.
Because of the esteem in which he was held by militiamen, Fr.  Hormisdas
had the power to protect the priests in Nyana-Fr. Mathieu Ngirumpatse,
Fr. Innocent Nyangezi, Fr. Callixte Uwitonze and Fr. Jean-Bosco
Yirirwahandi.
But they were all killed. More than that, he is accused of arranging
their deaths and that of Fr. Furaha in Save.
When the priests realised that, as Tutsis, their lives were under
threat, the three younger men decided to leave the parish and hide in an
orphanage in the neighbouring commune of Ntyazo. But Fr. Mathieu was elderly
and ill, and hoped to find sanctuary at Christ Roi.  Workers at Christ Roi
and the parish gave corresponding reports of his decision and its
consequences. Fr.
Mathieu was seen arriving at the college; he left soon afterwards having
been "bluntly" been turned away by Fr. Hormisdas. Later that night, Fr.
Hormisdas and a group of militiamen armed with machetes and nailed clubs
left the college, heading for the parish. The following morning, news of
Fr. Mathieu's murder spread. His corpse was "strewn across the yard" at the
parish, but those who volunteered to bury him were deterred by
Fr. Hormisdas.
Joseph Semuhunyege was on duty as the night watchman at Christ Roi when
Fr. Mathieu arrived at the college.
When Fr. Mathieu realised that he was in danger, he fled to Christ Roi;
he thought that his colleague, Hormisdas, would protect him. He came in the
evening and I was at the college. I went to tell Hormisdas about it. He came
and told Mathieu that he should go back to the parish because the college
did not have more houses than the parish. He turned him away in a blunt
fashion; old Fr. Mathieu returned to the parish where he was killed the same
night.
Joseph's testimony leaves little doubt about Hormisdas' direct
complicity in the murder of Fr. Mathieu.
The group which killed him left the college at about 9:00 p.m. Fr.
Hormisdas was with them. It was a group of militiamen, including the
workers from Gikongoro recruited by Hormisdas. Two of the men had spent the
night in Hormisdas' own room. They were carrying machetes and massues.
Hormisdas left with the group, although he wasn't carrying a
machete.
DÃ©o Ntacyonzaba, who had been employed by the priests for 15 years,
spoke of how he found out the details of the incident and Fr.  Hormisdas'
role in it. DÃ©o had left the parish soon after the priests, preferring to be
at home because of the insecurity. After a week at home, he had received a
visit from a fellow-employee at the parish, John, who was a militiaman and
the brother of Simon Kalinda, a leader of Hormisdas' team known as the
"dragon." John told DÃ©o that Fr. Hormisdas wanted to see him "urgently." He
also told DÃ©o about Hormisdas' role in Fr. Mathieu's death and in the
murders of the other three priests who had hidden at the orphanage.
John said: "You know that Fr. Mathieu stayed at home on his own.
Hormisdas went to ask him the whereabouts of the other priests, supposedly
to protect them. Fr. Mathieu immediately told him they had gone to hide at
the orphanage. Afterwards Hormisdas came back to where we were assembled and
told us what Fr. Mathieu had said. Then Hormisdas sent the people who were
staying at his house to kill the priests in the orphanage. Fr. Mathieu was
also killed by someone despatched by Hormisdas. What was sad was that
Hormisdas would not let us bury Fr. Mathieu, telling us to let his body be
eaten by dogs."
Despite his distress at the news, DÃ©o went to see Hormisdas, along with
another parish worker, Gaspard.
When we arrived at the parish, Gaspard and I stayed in front of the
church. John went to fetch Hormisdas, who arrived with John, PhÃ©neas and
Simon Kalinda. The priest had a large sword. He greeted us and told us that
the priests-Innocent, Bosco and Mathieu-were dead and that Mgr. Gahamanyi
[the Bishop of Butare] had asked him to find the people who worked for the
priests so that they could look after the church's property.
Accompanied by Hormisdas and the two militiamen, DÃ©o and Gaspard entered
the priests' residential quarters.
When we arrived at the priests' stables, I saw crows eating a corpse.  I
took a good look and saw that it was the corpse of Fr. Mathieu.  Hormisdas
was laughing as he watched the crows eating the corpse.  There were only
bones left. His eyes and flesh had been eaten by the dogs and crows. We
wondered why Fr. Hormisdas did not bury a fellow priest.
Later, DÃ©o and Gaspard buried Fr. Mathieu, a decision that angered
Fr. Hormisdas.
Vianney, related to Simon Kalinda, came to tell me to escape because
Hormisdas and Simon had drawn up a plan to kill me, accusing me of having
buried Fr. Mathieu. Vianney told me that Hormisdas no longer trusted me,
saying that I was like other Nyanza Hutus with Tutsi mothers.
Both DÃ©o and Gaspard left the parish and hid until the interim
government was driven out of Nyanza.
The belief that Fr. Hormisdas was behind the murder of another priest,
Fr. Justin Furaha was voiced not only by people in Nyanza, but by priests
who were at the Parish of Save during the genocide. They point out that
Fr. Furaha was the only priest at the parish who was killed and that he was
not targeted by the local militia, who frequently allowed him through
roadblocks. One of the priests said a group of people came to Save to warn
them that Fr. Furaha was going to be killed by the militia from Nyanza.
Asked about this, Fr. Furaha explained that Fr. Hormisdas was his enemy
since he "kept on accusing him of being a member of the RPF."  Soon
afterwards, the gendarmes arrived to take Fr. Furaha away.
On Saturday 7 May, at 1:00 p.m., we were having lunch when three people
in civilian clothes turned up. They asked me to call Fr.  Furaha. I went to
call him. He came to say hello, then they went into his office. I asked
Furaha who they were. He replied that they were policemen from Nyanza who
had come to take him away. They put him in their car and they all left. I
then contacted Furaha's brother-in-law, FÃ©lix Semwaga, an important figure
among the killers, to see if he could save Furaha. He told me it was
impossible because his file had come from Nyanza. Furaha was taken to the
police station in Butare, then to Karubanda central prison.
Sister FÃ©licien, who died after the genocide, went to Butare town to
look for Fr. Martin Kabalira, the military chaplain in Butare. She was a
Benebikira nun. She wanted to ask him to look for some way to save
Fr. Furaha. Kabalira explained to the nun that it was impossible to save him
because Hormisdas had himself drawn up the file for his arrest.  This is
what she told us.
A few days after Furaha's arrest I received a letter from him. He wrote
that the person who had accused him was the one I knew to be his enemy in
Nyanza. He had been accused of holding meetings with the Inkotanyi, of
having purchased a large piece of land in Nyanza to build houses for the
Inkotanyi and of giving large amounts of money to the RPF. He had given the
letter to a prisoner who had been with him in Karubanda; this prisoner was
allowed to leave the prison and return there later. We gave this prisoner
money, food and clothes for Fr. Furaha. Later, another prisoner came to tell
us that Furaha was dead, along with some other priests who had been in
prison with him.
Furaha is the only priest who died in Save. He was killed by Hormisdas
because he had been his enemy for a long time.
A gendarme who saw Fr. Hormisdas frequently in their camp when he came
to visit their commander, Captain Birikunzira, said Fr.  Hormisdas was also
complicit in the deaths of two Benekibira nuns, Sr. FranÃ§oise and
Sr. Augustine.
Hormisdas had become a great gÃ©nocidaire. Some days after the genocide
began in Nyanza, Hormisdas came to our police camp, driving the college
car. I was there. It was between 8:00 a.m. and 9:00 a.m.  He told the
gendarmes who were there that they had forgotten to attack the Sisters'
convent. Hormisdas went then immediately went into the office of the
commander. The commander called some gendarmes, including his escorts,
Corporal Gahutu, a corporal who was a storekeeper and some others. He gave
them the order to leave with Hormisdas in order to attack the convent. Some
of them left on motorcycles and others in Hormisdas' car. They were
well-armed. It was Hormisdas who had these nuns killed. No one had dared
attack the convent.
A Benekibira nun noted his refusal to take part in the mass for their
burial.
We telephoned Fr. Hormisdas to get coffins. We also asked him to come
and say mass at the burial, saying that he should represent the Catholic
Church at the service since it was a funeral for nuns. He refused to give us
coffins.
Since Fr. Hormisdas had not come to join the service and help us pray, I
asked him to come and celebrate mass for the two nuns.  Hormisdas flatly
refused.
Hormisdas was well aware that our colleagues were dead but he never came
to visit us. He also knew that the militiamen had stolen our food but he
never came to bring us anything to eat. On the contrary, he went around with
the members of the militia who had attacked us, in particular the
soldiers.
Hormisdas' behaviour towards his fellow-priests astonished even some
other militiamen. They point out that while he conducted the burial ceremony
for a leading figure among the killers, he refused to either bury his fellow
priests and nuns. Jean-Claude Higiro asked "how could Hormisdas have pity
for other Christians when he did nothing to protect his colleagues?" While
Janvier Habimana pointed out that nothing prevented Hormisdas from hiding
the priests threatened with death.
Fr. Hormisdas is the first person who should explain, in a court of law,
how the priests of the Parish of Nyanza died. What prevented him from hiding
them? The people who killed the Tutsis were his collaborators; they could
not, therefore, touch anyone who was protected by Hormisdas.
A Call for Action
These are just some of the many allegations concerning Fr. Hormisdas
contained in African Rights' report. The task of establishing the identities
of the victims, the circumstances in which they died and the exact role
played by Fr. Hormisdas has only just begun. Angry that the planners of the
genocide reside abroad in comfort, prisoners in Rwanda are increasingly
willing to talk openly about how the genocide was orchestrated and how it
unfolded.
Further evidence about Hormisdas' conduct during the genocide will
eventually be exposed, whether the Catholic Church decides to assist in the
process of discovering it or not. But in the meantime the survivors of the
genocide in Nyanza find it difficult to bear the knowledge that their
persecutor has gone unpunished.
African Rights has long been supporting the demands of survivors in
Rwanda for the arrest and trial of the members of the clergy they hold
responsible for the deaths of their families and friends. We have identified
several of the accused clergy to the Church and to relevant judicial
authorities and have provided notice of their locations.
In an open letter to Pope John Paul II more than three years ago we
asked His Holiness to launch an inquiry into the role of the Catholic Church
in the 1994 genocide. We listed some of the allegations against Father
Hormisdas Nsengimana amongst others. No action has yet been taken. Those
responsible for Father Nsengimana's transfer first to Italy and then for his
appointment to Cameroon should take the time to read the anguished
statements of ordinary Rwandese people and reflect upon where their
responsibility lies. We are hopeful that the Catholic Church hierarchy will
see the exposure of the allegations against Father Hormisdas Nsengimana as
an opportunity for it to take a new approach to the issue of justice in
Rwanda.

Original_Search_Title: Sorghum Beer Losing Ground
Headline: South Africa: Sorghum Beer Losing Ground
Body_Text:
UNITED National Breweries, formerly National Sorghum Breweries, is concerned that SA's sorghum beer industry was declining in volume terms, despite the group reporting a profit of R18,9m for the year ended December 31, compared with R504000 previously.
