United National Breweries president and chief operating officer Rajan Ranganathan said yesterday that although the company had managed to become profitable, the sorghum beer industry was still facing difficulties.
"The organised sorghum beer market is being sandwiched from both sides while on one side home brew is gaining market share, on the other end clear beer is taking over sorghum beer drinkers as sorghum beer is fast going out of fashion," he said.
Ranganathan said consumer spending patterns had changed over recent years with cellular phones, casinos and the lotto also contributing to the drop in numbers of sorghum beer drinkers. The HIV/ AIDS pandemic also contributed to the erosion of the current base of sorghum beer consumers.
He said that despite the problems facing the industry, the group's performance during the year under review was significantly better than in the previous year. Operating profit improved to R41,1m in 2000 from R27,6m.
The company hopes to maintain profitability by continuing to cut costs.
Last year the company acquired Traditional Breweries Investment (TBI) from SA Breweries (SAB) for R30m.
The total purchase consideration was discharged by the group by taking a loan from SAB, repayable over five years with interest at prime rate.
Ranganathan said even though the group's bankers approved the transaction and provided an additional R5m overdraft facility to fund restructuring costs, all payments had and would be funded fully out of operational cash flow.
The operations of TBI were integrated with the group's and restructured at a cost of R5,4m substantially less than the originally estimated R8m.
Headline earnings for the year was 30,6c a share compared with 0,3c a share for the previous year. No dividends were declared.
Ranganathan said the group's cash flow was positive and it expected to reduce its long-term liabilities, which stand at R88,4m, by R25m during the current year.
"The huge challenge at the moment for the group's executives is to realise returns for the shareholders who have not had any return on their investments for many years," he said.
Ranganathan said the situation would not be improved by listing the group on the JSE Securities Exchange SA as was originally intended. "This option is currently not possible as not only is the industry facing difficult times, the stock market is undergoing serious turmoil," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Emir Commends Nestle
Headline: Nigeria: Emir Commends Nestle
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Emir of Kano Alhaji Ado Bayero, CFR has commended Nestle Nigeria
for its immense contributions and long-term commitment to the
economic growth and development of Nigeria. He made this remark when
he received in audience, the managing director and chief executive of
Nestle Nigeria, Mr.
Jean-Louis Chaumeil who paid him a courtesy visit in his palace
recently.
Speaking during the occasion, the Nestle boss said that Neslte
Nigeria had been involved in commercial activity in Kano State since
1968. He said that Kano State has had the good fortune of producing
the number two distributor of Magi products in Nigeria and, indeed,
the world. Chaumeil said that the courtesy visit to the emir was part
of to the re-launch activities of Maggi Cube in Kano. He said that
Kano State has contributed tremendously to the phenomenal growth in
turnover for the product. Besides, the company also gets most of its
local raw materials such as sorghum and Soya beans from Kano.
The Nestle managing director said that the magnificent edifice known
as "Gidan Maggi" located at Abubakar Rimi's market in Kano is an
eloquent testimony of the wide acceptability of Maggi Cube among
Ksano consumers. In appreciation of this consumer loyalty, he said
that the company decided to accord Kano a pride of place by
re-launching it first in Kano before any other location in Nigeria.
The Nestle boss said that the company had powerful brands which
command strong market positions in Nigeria and around the world. He
said that those brands will continue to benefit from significant
investment in terms of both marketing and research and development.
He assured that Nestle Nigeria will continue to monitor consumers'
perception of its brand so as to ensure sustainable, capital
efficient and profitable long term growth of the company and
satisfaction of its consumers.

Original_Search_Title: 'I Hid Under Corpses to Avoid Death,' Genocide Survivor Tells Tribunal
Headline: Rwanda: 'I Hid Under Corpses to Avoid Death,' Genocide Survivor Tells Tribunal
Body_Text:
Arusha — A prosecution witness who survived the 1994 Rwanda genocide today
told the International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda (ICTR) that he
hid under corpses to escape death during an attack by 'Interahamwe'
militiamen at the Gikomero parish, central Rwanda. The Interahamwe
was the youth wing of the then ruling Movement of the Republic for
National Development (MRND).
The witness identified only as -- "GEE" for his security - - claimed
that 400 ethnic Tutsi sought refuge at the parish prior to the
attack. GEE is the eighth witness to testify against Jean de Dieu
Kamuhanda, former Rwandan minister of higher education and scientific
research.
Kamuhanda, 48, is charged with nine counts of genocide, crimes
against humanity (murder and extermination), rape and violations of
the Geneva conventions. He has denied the charges.
GEE, who is Tutsi, alleged that the Interahamwe attacked the refugees
using machetes and other traditional weapons. The Tutsi sought refuge
at the parish following the death of Rwandan President Juvenal
Habyarimana in a plane crash on 6 April 1994. Violence erupted in
Rwanda after Habyarimana's death, claming more than 800,000 lives
between April and June 1994.
The militiamen arrived at the parish in three vehicles, GEE alleged.
The witness claimed that Kamuhanda, who also ordered the massacres,
led the Interahamwe during the attack.
"I lay among the dead to escape death," GEE told the court. "After
the Interahamwe completed the killings, they left. Soon thereafter, I
escaped and hid in a nearby sorghum field," GEE explained.
Led by prosecution attorney Ibukunolu Babajide of the United Kingdom,
the witness stated that he learned of Habyarimana's death through a
radio broadcast.
"The next day, Tutsi refugees sought shelter at my house. The
Interahamwe pursued them and killed them," he claimed.
The cross-examination of GEE was adjourned until tomorrow after Aicha
Conde of Guinea Conakry, lead counsel for Kamuhanda, told the court
that she was not feeling well.
During proceedings yesterday, another witness -- identified as only
"GAF" --- completed his testimony.
Grace Amakye, co-counsel for Kamuhanda who cross- examined GAF,
alleged that the witness concealed his identity as a former soldier
of the Rwanda Patriotic Front (RPF) in his written statement.
Amakye claimed that GAF was an RPF soldier from 1994 until last year.
In response, GAF said: "Beginning this year, I'm now a businessman."
Earlier, GAF testified that between 2500 and 3000 Tutsi sought refuge
at the parish. Most of them were killed at the parish, he said, but
others were killed while fleeing.
Twenty-one prosecution witnesses are expected to testify in the
trial, which opened on 3 September.
The trial is held before Trial Chamber II of the ICTR, comprising
Judges William Sekule of Tanzania (presiding), Winston Matanzima
Maqutu of Lesotho and Arlette Ramarason of Madagascar.

Original_Search_Title: Pabo is Alive
Headline: Uganda: Pabo is Alive
Body_Text:
Kampala — If government was to issue directives against cutting of trees, none
of us would be alive
Some calm seems to have returned to the north after 15 years of war
by Kony's Lord's Resistance Army (LRA). Caroline Lamwaka visited one
of the many camps in the area and reports
A vibrant and relaxed atmosphere hungs over Pabo protected camp, an
encampment area with over 45,000 displaced people on a half-square
kilometre of land. It is Saturday, July 7. At one end of the centre,
there is loud disco music resounding in the air. At another end at
the nearby Catholic parish, the church choir is singing, while other
children between the ages of seven to seventeen years are performing
traditional dances. The children are specifically dancing in
preparation for the day of the Holy Child, scheduled for the
following day. The dancing is also part of therapy to get them out of
the trauma sustained over the years of war.
Francis Moro, 32, the man who has been playing loud music at Pabo
centre's park sells cassette tapes to earn his living. He started his
business in February 2001. He plays very good music in order to
attract the attention of people so that they can buy his cassettes.
The last time the rebels came to the area was in April 2001, when
they looted a radio call, a mobile phone, a solar system and some
money from the Catholic parish. After one week, they came again and
were repulsed by the UPDF soldiers.
Does he fear Joseph Kony? "The fear of Kony does not stop one from
doing business," he says with a smile. "There are people who when
they are happy, come and dance in this environment where music is
blaring. People have little money and that is why it takes time to
restock my business."
Like the majority of the people in protected villages, Francis Moro
has learnt to survive from one small job to another. He rented half
an acre of land on which he grew rice for three years. Later the
owner took away the land, saying he wanted to use it. Then from the
capital he accumulated through the sale of rice, he advanced to
selling tapes. At the time when he was growing rice, he got six bags
of unhulled rice from the garden worth sh200,000, including labour.
Half an acre of land in Pabo is rented at sh20,000 and a full acre at
sh40,000. But getting a piece of land to rent is not easy, while
getting money to rent a piece of land is also a dream for many
people. Since September 1996, people have been going to the
countryside to look for food.
World Food Programme (WFP) food is never enough. It takes three
months before more food is distributed. The last time WFP gave out
food in Pabo was on April 21, 2001.
Pabo residents note that although the food is little, lasting only
three to seven days depending on the household, the food helps a lot
of them.
"We do odd jobs in order to survive. Business people bring sorghum,
beans, simsim to the market and the people here buy it. Most women
gather firewood and sell it in order to survive," observes Geoffrey
Lukakabwono
People collect firewood, mainly the terminalia (Opok) species, from
Olinga sub-parish near Kilak hills, a forested area, nine miles away.
The terminalia is high-class firewood. Other places for collecting
firewood are Pawel, Palwong, Labala and Otong.
Most of the trees at the trading centre have been cut.
"We rush to collect the firewood and bring it to the centre to sell
to businessmen, soldiers and other people who can afford to buy it so
that we can buy simsim and small fish (wanjiri) for our meals. People
barter the firewood with food items.
"Collecting firewood is one means of survival. It is most marketable
when it rains because some of the poor quality wood gets wet and
cannot light. Charcoal burning is also on the increase, while women
are making pots to earn a living and they have formed associations.
"If government or the forest officer were to issue directives against
the cutting of trees, we would all not be alive now."
People do odd jobs like carrying water for building lock-up shops or
working for food, under the Food for Work project by the Catholic
Relief Supply (CRS).
Among the people at the centre, there are those who grew rich. These
are mainly people who originally resided at the centre. These have
land, and they employed people to work in their fields and paid them
cash or food items.
Displaced people have been renting pieces of land from the original
people at the centre. Those whose homes are situated within a radius
of five kilometres from the camps are allowed to dig.
Father Santo Ojok of Pabo Catholic parish observes: "You can't be
strong in such a situation, only that on the bare minimum, you have
to survive."
What about the people's faith?
Father Santo Ojok notes: "It is always strange that in times of
difficulties, people tend to pray more. In Pabo, because of the
congestion in the camps, my church is packed every Sunday."
Even witch doctors have surfaced in larger numbers to practise
traditional healing."Witch doctors are very many here, but that is
primitivity," says Father Ojok.
"You know someone is suffering from malaria or a self explanatory
type of illness and you still insist he or she is charmed. I chase
them away," he adds.

Original_Search_Title: Trade Link Established Through Port Sudan
Headline: Ethiopia: Trade Link Established Through Port Sudan
Body_Text:
A Sudanese embassy official in Addis Ababa has confirmed that Ethiopia has
begun importing and exporting goods through Port Sudan, Reuters said on
Wednesday. "An agreement between the two countries has been concluded and
goods have already started moving to Ethiopia via Port Sudan," Fada
al-Rahman Ali, a counsellor at the embassy, told Reuters. The official
added that the highway linking the port with Gedaref (Al-Qadarif), 150 km
from the border with Ethiopia, had been completed, and goods were being
shuttled from there without any problem. The official also told Reuters
that the Ethiopian Maritime and Transit Service Enterprise (MTSE), a
state-run company which handles the bulk of Ethiopia's foreign trade, was
planning to build warehouses at Gedaref.
Last year, the Ethiopian roads authority announced it was embarking on
"general maintenance" of the 185-km road from Gonder to the border
crossing at Metema. Humanitarian sources, however, told IRIN on Thursday
that work to upgrade the road to an all-season standard, which included
the construction of 10 bridges, was still in progress and not expected to
be completed until towards the end of the year. Similar work is also
currently taking place to upgrade the gravel road between Gedaref and
Gallabat on the Sudanese side of the border. Until the work on both sides
was complete, the route from Gonder to Gedaref would remain impassable to
heavy trucks in the rainy season, the sources said. In May last year, the
UN World Food Programme in Ethiopia successfully transported about 1,000
mt of sorghum purchased locally in Sudan, using the same route. This was
reported to be the first time the Metema crossing had been in use since
the border between Ethiopia and Sudan was closed in the aftermath of the
1995 attempt on Egyptian President Husni Mubarak's life in Addis Ababa.
The road, however, became impassable to WFP-contracted trucks following
heavy rains in mid-2000.
In January, the Ethiopian government announced that it was examining the
feasibility of building a 2,000 km railway to link Port Sudan with
Ethiopia, with the line crossing the border through Gallabat and Metema,
and passing southwards eventually to link the existing Djibouti-Addis
Ababa railway with Moyale on Ethiopia's southern border with Kenya. The
announcement was followed by the signing in May of a trade agreement
between the Sudanese and Ethiopian governments, in which both sides were
reported to have emphasised the need to enhance Ethiopia's access to Port
Sudan and facilitate Sudanese oil exports to Ethiopia.

Original_Search_Title: US Loan to Be Used for Food
Headline: Eritrea: US Loan to Be Used for Food
Body_Text:
The United Sates has agreed to provide a loan of US $10 million to the
Eritrean government, the pro-government Visafric news agency said on
Wednesday. The loan will be used to purchase approximately 81,000 mt
tonnes of wheat and sorghum, Visafric said. The agreement between the two
countries was signed by US Ambassador to Eritrea William D. Clarke and Dr
Woldai Fitur of the Eritrean government in Asmara, the Eritrean capital.
Eritrea is expected to repay the loan, at one percent interest, within 30
years.

Original_Search_Title: Kenya's Maize Deficit Stands At 3 Million Bags
Headline: Kenya's Maize Deficit Stands At 3 Million Bags
Body_Text:
The country faces a maize shortfall of nearly three million bags.
The deficit was reduced from eight million bags to three million following the rains in January, the national coordinator of the Arid Lands Resource Management Project, Mr Mahboub Maalim, said yesterday.
He, however, said the situation was not worrying though pastoral communities were in need. "Between now and September, we expect importation of up to one million bags of maize to cater for the deficit," he told the Nation.
Mr Maalim said Kenya requires 2.7 million bags a month, an average of 33 million bags a year.
He said the situation in some districts which had been included in the emergency relief bracket had improved with the onset of the unseasonal January rains
Mr Maalim said some districts that recieved the January unseasonal rains had been dropped out of the list of emergency operations.
"The government is now planning to face out relief operations in these districts and introduce other aspects of development," he said.
Mr Maalim said Northeastern, Eastern, Turkana and Tana River districts did not receive the January unseasonal rains and were still under state of emergency.
He appealed to Kenyans to promote traditional foods like Cassava, millet and sorghum.

Original_Search_Title: Nile Breweries In Bigger Beer Group
Headline: Uganda: Nile Breweries In Bigger Beer Group
Body_Text:
Kampala — South African Breweries plc (SAB), which is listed on the London
Stock Exchange and the Castel Group, Africa's two largest beverage
companies, have joined into a strategic alliance. Among the main
objectives are to rationalise and strengthen their operations on the
continent.
The alliance was announced in London last week, by SAB's Chief
Executive, Graham Mackay and the Castel Group Executive Chairman,
Pierre Castel.
According to a press release, the deal becomes effective April 1,
2001 and will be executed through SAB's subsidiary, South African
Breweries International (SABI) Africa and Castel Group's beer
division, CBB. SABI (Africa) has a notable minority stake in Nile
Breweries owned by the Madhavani Group of Companies, based at Njeru.
The release further discloses that in order to cement the
relationship, SAB will acquire a 20% stake in CBB, whilst the Castel
Group will acquire a 38% stake in SABI Africa, effectively by way of
share exchange.
SABI Africa is the holding company for SAB's African beer, sorghum
and carbonated soft drink interests outside South Africa and has
operations in 12 countries. Including Angola, Botswana, Uganda,
Ghana, Kenya, Malawi, Tanzania, Zambia and Zimbabwe.
Castel Group has beer, carbonated soft drink and mineral water
interests, in Francophone Africa. Including Angola, Benin, Burkina
Faso, Cameroon, Central African Republic, Chad, Cote d'Ivoire, DRC,
Ethiopia, Gabon, Mali, Tunisia, Morocco, Niger, Senegal and Togo.

Original_Search_Title: Beer, Cigarettes, Wines Price Up
Headline: Botswana: Beer, Cigarettes, Wines Price Up
Body_Text:
Gaborone — Government has imposed an increase on duty on all the different types
of beer, other than sorghum beer, and the duty on imported beer has
also been increased. In a statement this week to Parliament the
Assistant Minister of Finance and Development Planning, Boyce
Sebetela says the duty has been increased by 5.98 % or increase from
P1.58 to P1.68 per litre of alcohol content.
The rate of excise and duty has been increased by 4.97 %, an increase
of 23 thebe per kg to 24.6 thebe per kilogram.
The rate on unfortified still wine has been increased by 4.83 thebe
per litre to 53 thebe per litre, while duty on fortified still wine
is increased by 11 thebe per litre to P1.20 per litre while sparking
wine by 13.3 thebe per litre to P1.47 per litre.
Imported alcohol spirits such as whisky and gin will attract P2.16
per litre more from P20.83 to P22.99 per litre. The smokers too have
not been spared the duty increase.
They will now pay P1.00 more for 10 cigarettes. This is a seven-
percent increase.
Soft drinks will now cost 1.4 thebe less per litre as the duty has
been decreased.
In reaction to the latest duty increases, the local brewers,
KGALAGADI Breweries (Pty) Ltd says it will be absorbing the excise
duty increase which was part of budget speech of the South African
Minister of Finance last week.
Kgalagadi Breweries statement says the decision by management to
absorb the duty increases that entails an increase on clear beer
amounting to 2.3 cents per 340 ml can or a 6% increase underscores
the company's commitment to exercising price restraint in the face of
tougher trading conditions. Earlier this month, Kgalagadi Breweries
(Pty) Ltd announced a 7,0% increase in beer prices that is well below
the rate of inflation of 8,6%.

Original_Search_Title: Police Smash Import Cartel
Headline: Kenya: Police Smash Import Cartel
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Mandera police have unearthed a cartel of smugglers on the Somali border.
The racket, thought to have started last year, is being masterminded
by the town's wealthy traders.
But a spokesman for the business community, Mr Abdullahi Haji, denied
the reports.
The contraband are mainly sugar, wheat, sorghum and electronic equipment.
Police said the traders were transporting goods at night without police
escort, as required by the authorities.
The police chief noted that because of the security situation all vehicles
must be escorted up to Garissa police station for checking and verification
of their customs documents by the Garissa KRA officers and for inspection
by the police against the possible smuggling of firearms.
Mr Kimani dismissed allegations by Mandera businessmen that the police
were supporting the Garissa businessmen. Police do not choose who brings
what commodities it would be a backward step for the force to go against
the policy of free trade. It would not work and should not work. He told
the press in his office during an interview.
Mandera businessman Mr Abdullahi Haji, denied the existence of any cartel
and blamed trade rivals between themselves and some Garissa business tycoons
who are out to frustrate the Mandera business community.
He accused the Garissa businessmen of using the police to frustrate
them by delaying their goods unless they parted with bribes.
Infact there is a trade war between Garissa and Mandera business men
over clearing agents and brokerage on import goods. He said adding Garissa
traders have won the blessing of the police and senior KRA officials to
monopolise the importation of goods from the war-torn Somalia.

Original_Search_Title: South Africa Breweries And Castel Join African Operations
Headline: South Africa Breweries And Castel Join African Operations
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — South Africa Breweries (SAB) and Castel Group yesterday entered an
alliance that will see their African operations join forces.
Castel and SAB the two biggest brewers on the continent said they
would enter into 50-50 partnerships to jointly tackle the few African
countries where neither have operations. The agreement included a
crossshareholding arrangement.
The most obvious target would be Nigeria, although neither of the
brewers would explicitly confirm this.
Castel operates mainly in Francophone African countries, while SAB
operates mainly in Anglophone countries.
Because of the lack of overlap, few synergies are expected to flow
from the alliance, but SAB finance director Malcolm Wyman stressed
the ability of the groups to better organise procurement,
distribution and capacity, as well as the sharing of equipment.
The deal would enable SAB to spread its investment risk across
Africa, and make more efficient use of resources.
However, in the short term the alliance would be earningsneutral, the
companies said.
Wyman said it would be up to the companies to ensure that savings
were developed down the line. To assist in this, board members of
each company would be joining the other.
The alliance which excludes SA and Namibia will not immediately
result in each company's beer brands being sold in the other's
markets.
But Wyman said the groups would look at what was most appropriate for
the new ventures, emphasising the strong recognition that the Castle
brand was getting across the continent.
Operations of both groups on the continent are roughly the same size
in terms of earnings and volume. Each is the dominant brewer in many
of the countries in which they operate.
As part of the deal, SAB will acquire a 20% interest in the beer
division of Castel Group, while Castel will take up a 38% stake in
SABI Africa, a subsidiary of SAB, by way of a nil-premium share
exchange.
The value of Castel's assets being transferred to SAB is about $64m,
and those of SABI Africa's assets being transferred to Castel is
about $73m.
Castel's executive chairman Pierre Castel said he was confident that
the close relationship with SAB would result in faster growth in
Africa for both groups.
SBI Africa the holding company for SAB's African beer, sorghum and
carbonated soft drinks outside SA produced 13,9-million hectolitres
of beer for the period ending March last year, while Castel's beer
production was 9,1-million hectolitres.

Original_Search_Title: South Africa Draws Budget Lines For 2001
Headline: South Africa Draws Budget Lines For 2001
Body_Text:
Cape Town, South Africa — South Africa's 2001 budget,
announced Wednesday in Parliament by Finance Minister Trevor
Manuel, provides for expenditure of 32.2 billion US dollars in
2001/02, subsequently increasing to 37.1 billion dollars in
2003/04.
Revenue increases from 29.1 billion to 34.1 billion dollars
over the same period.
A 2,5 per cent GDP deficit is anticipated on the main budget
in 2001/02, falling to 2,1 per cent of GDP by 2003/04.
Including social security funds and foreign technical
cooperation, consolidated national expenditure of 33.3 billion
dollars is projected in 2001/02 or 27,1 percent of GDP - 9,6
percent more than the revised 2000/01 estimate.
The revised economic outlook allows government to increase
allocated spending on public services by 1.25 billion dollars in
2001/02 and 2 billion dollars in 2002/03 above the levels
projected in the 2000 budget forward estimates.
These adjustments compensate for somewhat higher anticipated
inflation and raise real non-interest expenditure growth to 3,8
percent a year over the next three years.
Provinces receive 56 per cent of the available additional
resources, national departments 38 percent and the local sphere
gets 6 percent.
Provision for state debt commitments amounts to 6.01
billion dollars in 2001/02.
Interest on debt was 5,5 percent of GDP in 1999/00, and
will fall to 4,4 per cent of GDP in 2003/04.
As in the past, the budget framework for the next three
years includes a contingency reserve to allow for future
uncertainty and unforeseeable spending commitments.
The revised budget framework allows for substantial
additions to the baseline projections for the next three years
including, among other areas, additional allocations of 2 billion
dollars proposed for provinces to strengthen social service
delivery and other priority needs, more than 500 million dollars
to the criminal justice sector, and 975 million dollars proposed
for supplementary infrastructure investment and maintenance.
The budget framework also accommodates several spending
proposals not yet allocated to departmental votes, amongst which
is 15 million dollars in 2001/02 for short-term poverty relief
measures, including interventions in response to cholera
outbreaks, and 37.5 million dollars in 2002/03 for targeted
HIV/Aids interventions.
Spending in 2001/02 from these supplementary allocations will
be brought to the Assembly in the Adjustments Estimate, once
likely project disbursements have been finalised.
Signalled raises in specific excise duties (which have been
a buoyant source of revenue for government) would involve beer
and cider taxes (6 percent), sorghum flour duties (5 percent),
all other alcoholic beverages (10 per cent), tobacco products
(between 11,8 and 20,2 percent).
The reverse is the case for soft drinks and mineral water
where a 25-percent cut in duties is proposed.

Original_Search_Title: A Packet Of Cigarettes To Cost Almost R9 - Tobacco Institute
Headline: South Africa: A Packet Of Cigarettes To Cost Almost R9 - Tobacco Institute
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — The recommended retail price of a pack of 20 cigarettes in the most
popular price class will increase from R 8.40 to R 8.80, Edward
Shalala the CEO of the Tobacco Institute of South Africa. Commenting
on Finance Minister Trevor Manuel's Budget 2001/2, Shalala said that
the increase of excise duties on cigarettes by 12% which was above
inflation.
"Over the last seven years, excise duties on cigarettes have gone up
by almost 460% which has led to VAT fraud, smuggling and
counterfeiting," said Shalala in a statement on Wednesday.
The Bureau of Market Research (BMR) at Unisa recently predicted a 17%
decline in the sale of tobacco products in the formal retail sector
for 2001.
"If the BMR is correct, this year will see a massive switch in the
sale of tobacco products from the formal retail sector to the
informal retail sector which will further erode the government's tax
base" said Shalala.
People enjoying a drink and/or a smoke are being hit by "sin taxes"
again. Manuel announced in this year's Budget that these taxes "have
been a buoyant source of revenue" and announced the following: * beer
and cider taxes are raised by 6 percent or 2,3 cents a can * sorghum
beer and sorghum flour duties are raised by 5 percent * duties on all
other alcoholic beverages are raised by 10 percent * taxes on tobacco
products are raised by between 11,8 and 20,2 percent to maintain the
50 percent tax incidence. This increases the price of a packet of 20
cigarettes by 33,8 cents * duties on soft drinks and mineral water
are reduced by 25 percent, or 2 cents a litre "These measures will
raise estimated revenue by R779 million. Not so sweet," said Manuel.
These measures will take affect from February 21, 2001.
He said that a recent study on smoking trends in South Africa between
1993 and 1999 revealed that aggregate cigarette consumption has
decreased by about 22 percent between 1993 and 1999 and smoking
prevalence among the adult population decreased by about 14 percent
over the period.
"However, the total number of smokers remained constant at
approximately 8 million," said Manuel.
The study found that the average cigarette consumption per smoker
declined by about 21 percent and smoking among young adults is below
the national average and falling.
He said that real price increases, partly due to increased excise
taxes, contributed to these decreases in prevalence and consumption.
"Real price increases appears to be effective at reducing smoking
prevalence among lower income and young people," said Manuel.

Original_Search_Title: "Another NDC Victory Would Have Killed Me"
Headline: Ghana: "Another NDC Victory Would Have Killed Me"
Body_Text:
Accra — In the early 50s only a few people from the north, especially
Dagbon, were interested in taking up residence in the south. Those
who dared move out were regarded as venturing into a 'wilderness'. By
and large it was those who could afford to be indifferent to the
pleas of their people to return home who made the south their
permanent abodes.
Many who came down did so because they were involved in the selling
of an assortment of grains produced in the north which they brought
down to the southern markets. After selling these commodities they
bought manufactured goods for the home markets. Eventually some of
these traders took up permanent residence in this part of the
country.
The traders brought down to the south an array of items like maize,
sorghum, millet, guinea fowl and shea butter among others. All these
were in high demand in the south. The progress of this trade
encouraged the establishment of a lucrative business in transport
ownership. In those days commercial drivers were very respected
especially those involved in the long distance trips to and from the
south.
A few Islamic scholars also took up residence in the south where they
studied and established places of learning the Holy Quran. With the
passage of time people from the north Dagombas mostly, joined the
already established Zongo community whose people they shared a common
religion and culture with. In some instances there were
intermarriages between the 'immigrants' and the Hausa, Yorubas and
other ethnic groups who had long been residing in these areas with
Hausa remaining the predominant language.
In Accra the late Alhaji Tahiru of blessed memory was one of the
early settlers who lived here. There were also personalities like the
late Haruna Barazim and others.
When Alhaji Alhassan Abdulai Afatogma, the son of the late Islamic
scholar of Yendi Alhaji Abdulai Baba Gomda arrived to take up
residence in the capital he did not have much problems as his cousin,
the late Hajia Maata, wife of the late Alhaji Tahiru was around to
give a helping hand.
This was his second attempt at settling down in the south. His first
was in 1950 when he settled at Sekondi/Takoradi where he worked with
the Elder Dempster Lines and later with the Royal Airforce base where
he served British officers. He came to the south for the second time
because he had lost his beloved wife at home. Perhaps he took this
decision so he could get over the shock at the death of his wife in
Yendi.
As fate would have it Accra became his permanent home where he
married another woman in 1953 with whom he had many children. In the
course of his sojourn in Accra he became involved in the activities
of the CPP through the late Mr. J.H. Allassani through whom he came
into contact with other big names like Kweku Baako, S.D. Dombo,
Welbeck, the late Alhaji Yakubu Tali and others
When his hometown of Yendi was torn between joining the Mandated
Togoland or the then Gold Coast he joined those who were for the Gold
Coast. His late Uncle W.H. Husein was a strong figure in the 'Tidi
pirgi' or 'let's not divide' campaign.
When the colonial administration was encouraging people in the north
to send their wards to school it was his uncle the one-time District
Commissioner, Alhaji Abdul Majeed Husein who was given out to be
schooled. He however took advantage of the Teacher Kaleem inspired
mass education drive. This together with self-education equipped him
with the ability to read and write a little. His job as a pilgrims
agent to then West African Airways Corporation and later Ghana
Airways was made easy with this literary ability. He showed profound
interest in current affairs relying mostly on BBC for his news
update. Mention world leaders of yore like Kruschev, Broz Tito and
Winston Churchill and he would have something to say about them.
He recalled how in his youthful days he used to follow his father to
the Information Centre in Yendi where they followed the proceedings
of the Second World War with his idol Winston Churchill at the helm
of affairs on the allied side.
In Accra he was one of the first from the growing number of Dagombas
to enter the transport business. He bought his first lorry, which was
a Morris, when he was in his twenties for three hundred pounds. This
was followed by an Austin which went for seven hundred and fifty
pounds cash. In those days he said the journey to the north passed
through Senchi and Kete Krachi and it took about two or three days.
The journey took a break at Senchi where a ferry was available for
lorries. From this point there was another break some distance away
from Kete Krachi at a place called Banda. The last lap took them
finally to Yendi.
His love for politics was tested when on one occasion he abandoned a
mouth-watering deal to cart some goods worth seventy pounds for Accra
from Yendi. He did so because had he waited he would have missed the
voting which was billed for the next day in Accra.
Even though he was a die-hard Nkrumaist in those days, he had to jump
the CPP train when according to him the late president promulgated
the Preventive Detention Act (PDA) under which people were
arbitrarily arrested and detained. This did not go down well with
him. This move was temporary as eventually he found himself in love
with the CPP tradition once again when the Limann PNP came up.
From then on, he never hid his disdain for Rawlings, the man who
overthrew the PNP and this unflinching position was kept all the time
to December 28 2000 when Rawlings was finally removed through the
ballot box. It was very common for his friends to tease him with a
joke like 'Alhaji we hear you have joined the NDC'. This was usually
followed by a long tale of how the NDC administration was messing the
country up. No wonder when the NPP ousted the NDC in the last polls
many people wanted to see him and find out the mood in which he was.
Elated Alhaji Alfatogma said what happened on December 7 and 28 was
the work of God who has heard the cries of Ghanaians. He even said
had the results not gone the way they did, life would not have been
worth living anymore for him.
As a respected and popular man among members of his ethnic group in
Accra and indeed in the south, he felt disappointed when the results
of the first round of voting showed that his people up north had by
and large opted for the NDC. His only little consolation was that
Yendi where he hails from refused to toe the line of the other
constituencies in Dagbon where copious amounts of money of changed
hands.
His disappointment was shared by a majority of Dagombas in Accra and
Kumasi. When the decision to send a high powered delegation of his
people to the north to campaign for the run-off he was one of those
who went for the mission which can pass for an 'Operation Save Dagbon
from the intrigues of the NDC'.
When things got so bad in the country he resorted to prayers in line
with the dictates of Allah who he said directs in the scriptures
thus: 'When you are faced with a problem you cannot surmount, fight
it with your heart and prayers.'

Original_Search_Title: Gemade's Re-Election Battles
Headline: Nigeria: Gemade's Re-Election Battles: the Odds, the Strategies As the Leadership of the Ruling Peoples Democratic Party (pdp) Gets Set for Its National Convention in Early November, Wale Olaleye Unveils the Plot to Unseat the Incumbent National Chairman, Chief Barnabas Gemade And His Strategies for Survival
Body_Text:
The next national convention of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP)
promises to be an interesting one not just for members of the party
alone but also for the generality of Nigerians who would be watching
the event closely as the outcome could decide where the pendulum
swings in the next general election between the party on one hand and
the other parties. It could also signify which direction Nigeria
would move after 2003. As the convention moves closer the shape of
what to expect is getting clearer and an epic battle for the national
Chairmanship is looking increasingly likely between the incumbent
Chief Barnabas Gemade and second Republic Minister of Communication,
Audu Ogbe
The convention which some members have dubbed the real convention
being the first to be conducted by an elected leadership of the party
could also be a test for Nigeria's democracy as it could showcase
Nigeria'scivilian government ability or lack of it to conduct a
general election successfully. This 2001 convention would be a
decider of many fates and a finisher of many aspirations. But beyond
the surface of the normal voting, characteristic of a convention,
stares many a surprise. From the seat of the National Chairman to the
least elected position in the party, awaits the biggest stun.The stun
is simply entrenched in some meticulously thought out plans to cut
short the dreams of many elected party office holders by not
returning them to office. This would be in apparent contrast with
what obtained at the November 1999 convention that brought the Gemade
executive to power. Even though tensions were high as candidates
scouted for offices at all cost, little was known about what were at
stake then. After all, new executives were just elected to chase
aside the appointed protem officers. Suffice to say that normal
political manouvering of wanting some candidates at the expense
others equally played out
The Plot Against Gemade
The arrow head of the plot to get Gemade out of the exalted
chairmanship post is believed to be no other man than Mr. Fix It
,himself. The man who perhaps, aside from President Olusegun Obasanjo
is the only one who can get things done both within the government
and the PDP. He is the Minister of Works and Housing, Chief Tony
Anenih. THISDAY investigation revealed that the entire game plan had
begun at the meeting allegedly called at the instance of Anenih. At
the meeting, it was further learnt, were the Vice President, some
members of the National Executive Committee (NEC) including some
governors of PDP controlled states. Anenih, sources said had called
the meeting to unveil to them, the purported decision from the top
that the Chairman, Chief Barnabas Gemade had not done enough since he
was elected national chairman two years ago to merit a second term in
office. In justifying this position, Anenih was also said to have
told them that the caucus that decided the fate of Gemade had also
resolved to field as the party's new boss, Audu Ogbe, second republic
Minister of Communication. Surprisingly Anenih did not have it as
envisaged as he encountered stiff opposition to the plot from most
members of the NEC and some of the state governors who were present.
And behold, the meeting ended in deadlock.
Allegations Against Gemade
Ironically, Anenih's apparent failure at getting these people behind
this projct was said to be largely due to the unconvincing reasons he
adduced for the plot. His allegations against Gemade was said to have
been dismissed as lacking substance and of no political weight. He
was quoted to have told the gathering that Gemade was becoming unduly
powerful and independent minded. He was further quoted to have
alleged that the PDP chairman had courted too much, the state
governors and was therefore becoming an institution. Beside these,
however he was said to have said nothing damning against the Chairman
that could possibly have been tenable to the meeting,hence those
present were not swayed by his arguments.
Gemade's Adamance
Apparently unpertubed, Gemade was said to have staked his neck that
come rain, come shine he would seek re-election. Even while Ogbe was
tipped as the annointed candidate, he was said to have insisted at
the meeting that he would test his popularity and electability before
the rank and file of the party at the convenion. At this stage a
source at the meeting said Gemade got more of the sympathy of some
governors and members of the NEC present They thereafter, THISDAY
gathered, kicked against the resolve to push aside Gemade basically
because of what it described as too much interference from the
government, specifically the Presidency. They further argued that
some of the problems plaguing the party, even at some state levels,
was because of the vested interest and interference the government
was having in the affairs of the party. They picked quarrel with the
Samuel Ogbemudia led-convention committee for releasing the
convention time table without having gone through the ratification of
the NEC. Such development, they described as insurbodination and
would not let go. And this perhaps, may have been responsible for the
slight alteration in the party's convention time-tableby the NEC at
its meeting in Abuja last Thursday .That meeting was said to have
been polarized by the chairmanship election as some of the state
governors who have been routing for Gemade, had under pressure,
supposedly from the Presidencysuccumbed o backing Ogbe .At the
meeting no fewer than 12 out of the 21 PDP governors may have changed
over to Ogbe. Their reason being that with Ogbe increasingly becoming
the favoured candidate of the Presidency, it would be politically
suicidal for them to oppose him knowing fully well that the
Presidency holds the knife and the yam so to speak in Nigeria.
Moreover majority if not all of them are interested in a second term
in office and the support of the Presidency would be very crucial to
their aspiration. Another development that could have swayed their
support for Ogbe was that PDP National Secretary, Okweisilezie Nwodo
may have finally accepted not to recontest but take up the offer of
Nigeria's High Commisioner to Zimbabwe believed to have been
offeredby Obasanjo. InNwodo's place Chief Vincent Ogbulafor is being
tipped and highly favoured. But these notwithstanding Gemade, THISDAY
gathered,was still insisting on giving Ogbe a good fight,even in
spite of the offer of a ministerial appointment made to him, which he
may eventually be persuaded to accept.
The Strength and Support of Gemade
In as much as it could not be ruled out that Gemade may have risen to
power on the strength of the Presidency support, there is also the
tendency that the incumbency factor which he may have wielded while
in office remain a useful tool to him as his faithfulls moves round
the party members to garner support for him. These loyalists have
begun serious campaign to thwart all the plans against his
re-election. This was sequel to the decisions reached at the end of
their caucus meeting. A competent party source who also attended the
meeting gave myriad reasons why the party boss would be re-elected.
He cited amongst several other reasons, the fact that Gemade had
brought the party to a lime light and that through him, a lot of
personalities have begun to join the party. He was instrumental in
luring two prominent Senators and a member of the House of
Representatives from the rival Alliance for Democracy party (AD),
into the PDP. Similarly he influenced prominent politician, Pa
Anthony Enahoro into teaming up with the party as well as getting one
time Foreign Affair Minister Chief Tom Ikimi from the rival All
Peoples Party (APP) to come on board.Also his not belonging to any of
the groupswithin the pdp has enabled him to cut across members from
all the sides building bridges . The source maintained that any
attempt to frustrate Gemade's re-election could in turn spell doom
for the party. The source which cited continuity as another major
factor for the man to be re-elected, reasoned that the task of
repositioning the party which Gemade had started would only
materialise if a stretch of sufficient time is allowed for such
planning and execution of innovations that his era had brought into
the party politics.
The Presidency As License
Even as the inter-play of interest continues to take its toll on the
major players, the only joker and guarantor of position that is being
used is the consent of the Presidency. And this has been used
severally used in the past even without its knowledge and consent for
selfish and personal reasons. Now, the name had become prominent in
this chairmanship struggle. And the way the anti-Gemade group had
been using the name may have confirmed the belief that the Presidency
was practically sponsoring some of the issues that are now
contentious in the party. But this was ironically being done as if
the Chairman did not know or have a one-on-one relationship with the
President. Gemade who seemed not perturbed by the trends had
therefore resolved to jettison whatever threat and put to test, his
credibility to win election, the source further hinted.
...And the Probable Consequences
Certainly, all the interplay of interest and muscle flexing could not
come and take its leave without leaving behind marks, either
positively or negatively.That the PDP is about the only party with
some seeming credibility is more of a mere fallacy now. The three
political parties are no better than one another. The difference
could be that the PDP at all times had tried to manage its crisis
such that it did not degenerate into a lousy affair, though this has
failed often times. But with the convention approaching faster and
the events still trailing, the out-come could be a surprising
misfortune that could spin around any likely success. If the party
does not put its house together and design further plans towards next
election, the November convention could surprisingly vitiate whatever
dreams it may have been nurturing of winning the next round of
elections. All other parties that are today seen as sleeping may
after all be working on their own success story to push the PDP out
of power.
